questions.subject: U.S. History
questions.stem: The June 1774 meeting of Philadelphia freemen was called as a direct result of

choices.text: Great Britain's punishment of Boston for the destruction of British tea
the creation of an armed colonial militia in New England
pressure from loyalists to resolve tensions between Pennsylvania and Great Britain
establishing a committee to elect representatives to speak to the British king

questions.explanation: After the Boston Tea Party (December 1773), Parliament passed the Coercive Acts (1774)—closing Boston Harbor, voiding the Massachusetts charter, and quartering troops—which colonists called the Intolerable Acts. Philadelphia freemen met in June 1774 to protest these punishments and coordinate colonial resistance, directly paving the way for the First Continental Congress in September 1774. Students often confuse this meeting with the Constitutional Convention; the key distinction is that it was a reaction to British coercion, not a founding moment.

questions.subject: U.S. History
questions.stem: What is one impact of this amendment on American society?

choices.text: Members of the press cannot hold public office.
The Supreme Court cannot limit free speech during wartime.
Congress cannot mandate a national religion.
Religious groups cannot lobby Congress.

questions.explanation: The First Amendment prohibits Congress from making any law 'respecting an establishment of religion' (the Establishment Clause) or 'prohibiting the free exercise thereof' (the Free Exercise Clause), meaning the federal government cannot create or mandate a national religion. This separation of church and state, a cornerstone of American constitutional law, is reinforced by Supreme Court decisions such as Engel v. Vitale (1962), which banned state-sponsored prayer in public schools. Students sometimes confuse the Establishment Clause with the Free Exercise Clause—both are in the same sentence of the First Amendment.

questions.subject: U.S. History
questions.stem: The Mayflower Compact (1620) and the Virginia House of Burgesses (1619) are early examples of

choices.text: resistance to British rule
economic systems in the colonies
steps toward representative government
restrictions on religious freedom

questions.explanation: The Mayflower Compact (1620) created a self-governing body for the Plymouth Colony by having signers agree to obey 'just and equal laws' for the general good—an early expression of popular sovereignty and majority rule. The Virginia House of Burgesses (1619) was the first representative legislature in English North America, giving colonists a voice in their own governance. Both documents reflect the tradition of self-government that would culminate in the Declaration of Independence and the Constitution.

questions.subject: U.S. History
questions.stem: One way in which the Declaration of Independence (1776) and the original United States Constitution (1789) are similar is that both documents

choices.text: guarantee voting rights to all persons
list grievances against the British monarchy
include a specific bill of rights
state that government receives its power from the people

questions.explanation: Both the Declaration of Independence (1776) and the original Constitution (1789) affirm the principle of popular sovereignty—that government derives its legitimate authority from the consent of the governed. The Declaration states this explicitly as a natural right, while the Constitution's Preamble opens with 'We the People,' embedding popular sovereignty in the founding document. This shared principle distinguishes American government from the monarchical theory of divine right of kings that prevailed in Europe.
questions.sourceIssue: Question text depends on a source image, but no verified stimulus crop is available.

questions.subject: U.S. History
questions.stem: Why were the writers of the United States Constitution willing to compromise on these issues?

choices.text: to gain Southern support for ratification
to give Northern states an advantage in the House of Representatives
to end slavery after 1808
to make approval of the Bill of Rights easier

questions.explanation: At the Constitutional Convention (1787), Northern and Southern delegates disagreed over whether enslaved people would count toward congressional representation. The Three-Fifths Compromise settled this by counting three-fifths of the enslaved population for both representation and taxation, giving slave states more seats in the House. Framers accepted this and other compromises—like the 20-year continuation of the slave trade—because ratification required consensus from both Northern commercial and Southern agrarian states.

questions.subject: U.S. History
questions.stem: Based on this passage, the purpose of the Federalist Papers was to

choices.text: argue for a bill of rights
discourage the creation of political parties
support ratification of the Constitution
justify the American Revolution to the colonists

questions.explanation: The Federalist Papers (1787–1788) were a collection of 85 essays written by Alexander Hamilton, James Madison, and John Jay under the pseudonym 'Publius' to persuade New York voters to ratify the Constitution. Key essays like Federalist No. 10 (on factions) and No. 51 (on checks and balances) explained the constitutional design and argued that a large republic with separated powers would protect liberty better than a small direct democracy. The Regents uses these essays to test understanding of the founders' intent regarding the constitutional system.

questions.subject: U.S. History
questions.stem: Which constitutional power was most directly responsible for the action taken in this letter?

choices.text: power of the Senate to approve proposed treaties
power of Congress to override vetoes
power of the president to appoint justices to the Supreme Court
power of impeachment by the House of Representatives

questions.explanation: The House of Representatives has the sole power of impeachment under Article I of the Constitution, meaning only the House can formally accuse a president or other federal official of 'high crimes and misdemeanors.' In the Nixon impeachment inquiry (1974), the House Judiciary Committee voted to recommend articles of impeachment for obstruction of justice, abuse of power, and contempt of Congress before Nixon resigned. The Regents pairs this with separation of powers questions to test students' understanding of congressional oversight.
questions.sourceIssue: Stimulus image was reused by a different released Regents source; hidden until re-imported from the original PDF.

questions.subject: U.S. History
questions.stem: Which statement most accurately expresses the main idea of the cartoon?

choices.text: The Marshall Plan will limit Europe's economic recovery.
American taxpayers hope the Marshall Plan will quickly stabilize Europe's economy.
Europe will not take advantage of the Marshall Plan.
The Marshall Plan will bankrupt the American taxpayer.

questions.explanation: The Marshall Plan (1948–1952), proposed by Secretary of State George Marshall, provided $13 billion in economic aid to rebuild Western European economies devastated by World War II. Its twin goals were humanitarian—preventing starvation and economic collapse—and strategic: wealthy, stable democracies would be less susceptible to communist influence. The cartoon likely depicts American taxpayers as hoping their investment would quickly stabilize Europe and halt the spread of Soviet influence during the early Cold War.

questions.subject: U.S. History
questions.stem: The purpose of this statement was to

choices.text: provide justification for declaring independence
urge support for the Albany Plan of Union
criticize the Articles of Confederation
advocate obedience to Great Britain

questions.explanation: The passage from the Declaration of Independence that Jefferson included—drawn from Locke's social contract theory—lists colonial grievances against King George III to justify the break from Britain, framing independence as a natural right when government fails its obligations to the governed. The Declaration served as both a revolutionary manifesto and a diplomatic document, seeking to win foreign recognition and support for the American cause. Regents questions pair this with the Constitution to test understanding of the continuity and differences between these founding documents.
questions.sourceIssue: Stimulus image was reused by a different released Regents source; hidden until re-imported from the original PDF.

questions.subject: U.S. History
questions.stem: The presidential elections of 1876 and 2000 were controversial because the winner in both elections

choices.text: was a third-party candidate
had fewer popular votes than his opponent
carried fewer states than his opponent
had fewer electoral votes than his opponent

questions.explanation: In 1876, Rutherford B. Hayes won the presidency despite Samuel Tilden winning the popular vote, through a contested Electoral Commission decision; in 2000, George W. Bush won the Electoral College while Al Gore won the popular vote, with the Supreme Court halting a Florida recount in Bush v. Gore. Both cases highlighted the tension between popular will and the Electoral College system, renewing calls for direct election of the president. The Regents uses these parallel cases to probe students' understanding of the constitutional design of presidential elections.

questions.subject: U.S. History
questions.stem: According to the passage, the principal goal of President Truman in issuing this executive order was to

choices.text: end segregation in the armed forces
increase opportunities for women to serve in combat
ensure an adequate number of troops to fight in the Cold War
stop the military draft

questions.explanation: Executive Order 9981, issued by President Harry Truman on July 26, 1948, desegregated the U.S. armed forces by declaring that 'there shall be equality of treatment and opportunity for all persons in the armed services without regard to race, color, religion, or national origin.' Truman acted by executive order because Congress would not pass anti-segregation legislation, demonstrating the president's power to effect civil rights change without legislative action. This order predated the Civil Rights Act by 16 years and is a key milestone in the desegregation movement.

questions.subject: U.S. History
questions.stem: Based on the information provided by the cartoon, President Theodore Roosevelt's goal was to

choices.text: use federal power to control monopolies
assist businesses in resisting interference by investors
persuade businesses to accept nationalization
establish worker safety regulations in factories

questions.explanation: President Theodore Roosevelt used the Sherman Antitrust Act (1890) to file 44 lawsuits against monopolistic corporations—earning the 'trustbuster' nickname—most notably breaking up John D. Rockefeller's Standard Oil Company and J.P. Morgan's Northern Securities railroad trust. Roosevelt believed large corporations had abused their power at the expense of workers, consumers, and small businesses, and that federal regulation was necessary to restore competition. The Regents links Roosevelt's antitrust actions to the broader Progressive Era (1890s–1920s) reform movement.

questions.subject: U.S. History
questions.stem: Which action was taken to deal with the criticism expressed in this passage?

choices.text: giving states the ability to check the powers of Congress
amending the Constitution to eliminate the elastic clause
assigning the power of judicial review to the Supreme Court
adding a Bill of Rights to the Constitution

questions.explanation: Anti-Federalists like George Mason and Patrick Henry refused to support the Constitution without explicit protections for individual rights, fearing the new central government would abuse its powers. In response, James Madison drafted the Bill of Rights (the first ten amendments), ratified in 1791, which guaranteed freedoms of speech, religion, press, petition, and assembly, as well as protections for the accused in criminal proceedings. The Regents uses this sequence to test how constitutional compromise shaped the final document.
questions.sourceIssue: Stimulus image was reused by a different released Regents source; hidden until re-imported from the original PDF.

questions.subject: U.S. History
questions.stem: The decision in Marbury v. Madison (1803) was significant because it established that the Supreme Court

choices.text: had limited powers over state courts
could impeach the president and other government officials
had the power to choose its own members
could declare a federal law unconstitutional

questions.explanation: In Marbury v. Madison (1803), Chief Justice John Marshall ruled that Section 13 of the Judiciary Act of 1789 was unconstitutional because it expanded the Supreme Court's original jurisdiction beyond what Article III of the Constitution allowed. In so doing, Marshall established the doctrine of judicial review—the power of the Supreme Court to declare laws passed by Congress unconstitutional—a power not explicitly stated in the Constitution. This is the most frequently tested Supreme Court case on the Regents, appearing across multiple exam cycles.

questions.subject: U.S. History
questions.stem: Judicial review allows the Supreme Court to

choices.text: approve nominations to the president's cabinet
remove elected officials from office
determine the constitutionality of federal laws
oversee the financing of the lower federal courts

questions.explanation: Judicial review, established by Marbury v. Madison (1803), is the Supreme Court's authority to examine federal and state laws and executive actions and strike them down if they conflict with the Constitution. This power makes the judiciary a co-equal branch and a check on both Congress and the president, though it is exercised cautiously—the Court strikes down only a small fraction of laws it reviews. Regents questions on judicial review often test whether students can distinguish it from other court powers like original jurisdiction or appellate review.

questions.subject: U.S. History
questions.stem: Which power regarding the federal judiciary was established in Marbury v. Madison?

choices.text: The president appoints all federal judges.
The Congress creates lower federal courts.
Federal laws may be declared unconstitutional.
Members of the federal courts serve life terms.

questions.explanation: Marbury v. Madison (1803) established judicial review; McCulloch v. Maryland (1819) upheld the constitutionality of the national bank and ruled that states could not tax federal institutions; and Gibbons v. Ogden (1824) gave Congress broad power to regulate interstate commerce. Together, these landmark decisions under Chief Justice John Marshall dramatically expanded federal power relative to the states, laying the legal foundation for a strong national government. The Regents often tests all three Marshall Court decisions in a single set of questions.

questions.subject: U.S. History
questions.stem: The case of Marbury v. Madison (1803) established the principle that

choices.text: the states have power over the federal government
the president nominates federal judges
Congress can override presidential vetoes
the Supreme Court can declare federal laws unconstitutional

questions.explanation: Marbury v. Madison (1803) established judicial review, the power of the Supreme Court to invalidate acts of Congress that violate the Constitution. Chief Justice Marshall wrote that 'it is emphatically the province and duty of the judicial department to say what the law is,' elevating the Supreme Court to a true co-equal branch of government. This case appears on almost every cycle of the U.S. History Regents and is the single most important Supreme Court case students must know.

questions.subject: U.S. History
questions.stem: What was an important impact of the McCulloch v. Maryland (1819) decision?

choices.text: It stated that the bicameral legislature established by the Constitution was illegal.
It determined that the creation of the National Bank was constitutional.
It established the idea that states should have the power to overturn federal laws.
It limited the power of Congress to add new states to the Union.

questions.explanation: McCulloch v. Maryland (1819) arose when the state of Maryland tried to tax the federally chartered Second Bank of the United States; Chief Justice Marshall ruled that Congress had the implied power to create the bank under the Necessary and Proper Clause and that states could not tax federal institutions. The decision established two critical principles: broad implied powers for Congress and federal supremacy over the states. This ruling forms the constitutional cornerstone for nearly all federal regulatory power exercised today.

questions.subject: U.S. History
questions.stem: What was a result of the events reflected in these magazine covers?

choices.text: President Richard Nixon was defeated in his bid for reelection.
Freedom of the press was limited.
Many Americans lost trust in their government.
The Supreme Court ruled in favor of the president's actions.

questions.explanation: The Watergate scandal (1972–1974) and the Vietnam War (1964–1975) each severely damaged public trust in the federal government: Watergate revealed presidential criminality and abuse of power, while Vietnam demonstrated that the government had systematically misled the public about the war's progress. Together, these crises contributed to a lasting drop in American confidence in government institutions, a trend measured by Gallup polling that persisted for decades. The Regents uses this question to connect specific historical events to the broader theme of government credibility and civic trust.

questions.subject: U.S. History
questions.stem: Which action was taken to deal with the criticism expressed in this passage?

choices.text: assigning the power of judicial review to the Supreme Court
giving states the ability to check the powers of Congress
amending the Constitution to eliminate the elastic clause
adding a Bill of Rights to the Constitution

questions.explanation: Antifederalists like George Mason, Patrick Henry, and Elbridge Gerry refused to sign the Constitution without a Bill of Rights, warning it gave the central government too much power over individuals. Madison responded by drafting twelve amendments in the First Congress; ten were ratified in 1791 as the Bill of Rights, guaranteeing freedom of religion, speech, press, assembly, and petition, as well as protections for the accused. The Regents uses this question to test how the ratification debate produced lasting constitutional protections.

questions.subject: U.S. History
questions.stem: One way in which the Declaration of Independence (1776) and the original United States Constitution (1789) are similar is that both documents

choices.text: state that government receives its power from the people
list grievances against the British monarchy
include a specific bill of rights
guarantee voting rights to all persons

questions.explanation: The Declaration of Independence (1776) and the Constitution (1789) both affirm popular sovereignty—the principle that government derives its authority from the consent of the governed. The Declaration articulates this as a natural right, while the Constitution institutionalizes it through elections, representative government, and the phrase 'We the People' in the Preamble. This principle distinguishes American constitutional government from the monarchical systems of 18th-century Europe.
questions.sourceIssue: Question text depends on a source image, but no verified stimulus crop is available.

questions.subject: U.S. History
questions.stem: One way in which the Square Deal, the New Frontier, and the Great Society are similar is that each was a

choices.text: campaign to extend woman's suffrage
plan to promote big business
presidential program of reform 49
book written by a muckraking journalist

questions.explanation: Theodore Roosevelt's Square Deal (1901–1909) targeted corporate regulation, consumer protection, and conservation; John F. Kennedy's New Frontier (1961–1963) aimed at space exploration, civil rights, and economic growth; Lyndon B. Johnson's Great Society (1964–1968) created Medicare, Medicaid, and major civil rights legislation. All three were presidential programs that expanded the federal government's role in addressing domestic social and economic problems. The Regents tests students' ability to identify continuity in presidential reform traditions across different eras.

questions.subject: U.S. History
questions.stem: What was one method that was used to deny African Americans the voting rights mentioned in this speech?

choices.text: literacy tests and poll taxes
deployment of federal troops
student protests
bus boycotts and sit-ins

questions.explanation: After the 15th Amendment (1870) technically granted Black men the right to vote, Southern states used literacy tests, poll taxes, grandfather clauses, and white primaries to systematically disenfranchise African American voters. These practices effectively nullified Black voting rights throughout the Jim Crow era, particularly in the Deep South. The Voting Rights Act of 1965 finally outlawed these discriminatory mechanisms, prohibiting voting practices that discriminated on the basis of race.

questions.subject: U.S. History
questions.stem: What was a common goal of the Proclamation of Neutrality (1793), the Embargo Act (1807), and the Monroe Doctrine (1823)?

choices.text: encouraging independence movements in Latin America - 10th amendment, United States Constitution
avoiding conflicts with European nations
forcing Great Britain to grant independence to Canada
providing wartime aid to European nations

questions.explanation: Washington's Proclamation of Neutrality (1793) kept the U.S. out of the Anglo-French war following the French Revolution; Jefferson's Embargo Act (1807) tried to avoid the Napoleonic Wars by cutting off trade; Monroe's Monroe Doctrine (1823) warned European powers to stay out of the Western Hemisphere. All three policies shared the goal of keeping the young republic out of costly European entanglements while it built its domestic strength. This isolationist foreign policy tradition dominated U.S. strategy until World War I.

questions.subject: U.S. History
questions.stem: The Supreme Court's decision in Dred Scott v. Sanford was nullified by the passage of the

choices.text: Compromise of 1850
Reconstruction Act 21
Kansas-Nebraska Act
13th and 14th amendments

questions.explanation: The Supreme Court's Dred Scott v. Sanford (1857) decision declared that enslaved people were property, not citizens, and that Congress had no power to ban slavery in the territories—invalidating the Missouri Compromise. The 13th Amendment (1865) abolished slavery, and the 14th Amendment (1868) granted citizenship to all persons born or naturalized in the U.S., directly overturning Dred Scott's holding that Black Americans could never be citizens. These Reconstruction amendments represent the most significant expansion of constitutional rights since the Bill of Rights.

questions.subject: U.S. History
questions.stem: Which action would most likely have been rejected by the authors of this passage?

choices.text: passage of an amendment for the direct election of senators
creation of the Pure Food and Drug Act
establishment of the Federal Reserve System
announcement of the Roosevelt Corollary

questions.explanation: The Monroe Doctrine (1823) declared the Western Hemisphere closed to further European colonization and warned that any attempt to extend European political systems to the Americas would be considered a threat to U.S. security. The Roosevelt Corollary (1904) extended the Monroe Doctrine by asserting the U.S.'s right to intervene in Latin American nations to prevent European powers from collecting debts—effectively making the U.S. the 'policeman' of the Western Hemisphere. An anti-imperialist author would reject the Corollary as it transformed a defensive doctrine into a justification for U.S. imperialism.

questions.subject: U.S. History
questions.stem: One goal of the Marshall Plan (1947) was to

choices.text: provide defensive weapons to Great Britain
rebuild the economy of Western Europe
bring Nazi war criminals to trial in Nuremberg
fund the construction of the iron curtain

questions.explanation: The Marshall Plan (European Recovery Program, 1948–1952) provided $13 billion in U.S. economic aid to rebuild Western European nations devastated by World War II, with the primary strategic goal of creating stable, prosperous democracies resistant to communist expansion. Secretary of State George Marshall announced the plan in a June 1947 Harvard commencement address, framing it as humanitarian aid open to all European nations—though the Soviet Union rejected it for its satellites. The plan is considered one of the most successful foreign policy initiatives in U.S. history, credited with stabilizing Western Europe during the critical early Cold War years.

questions.subject: U.S. History
questions.stem: The Roosevelt Corollary (1904) to the Monroe Doctrine proclaimed the right of the United States to

choices.text: determine the border between Alaska and Canada
intervene in the internal affairs of Latin American nations
impose trade restrictions on European imports
promote democracy in Africa

questions.explanation: The Roosevelt Corollary (1904) stated that the U.S. would intervene in Latin American nations to stabilize their governments and finances if they were unable to meet their international obligations, thereby preempting European intervention. Roosevelt first applied the Corollary in the Dominican Republic (1905), then it was invoked to justify interventions in Nicaragua, Haiti, and elsewhere. The Regents tests students' ability to distinguish the original defensive Monroe Doctrine from the more aggressive interventionist Roosevelt Corollary.

questions.subject: U.S. History
questions.stem: The Kentucky and Virginia Resolutions (1798) held that states could nullify the Alien and Sedition Acts because these laws

choices.text: placed an unfair tax on whiskey made by western farmers
established limits on universal suffrage
repealed the Northwest Ordinance
violated the Constitution

questions.explanation: The Kentucky and Virginia Resolutions (1798–1799), written secretly by Madison and Jefferson, argued that the Alien and Sedition Acts were unconstitutional because they violated First Amendment freedoms of speech and press. They advanced the doctrine of 'nullification'—that states could declare federal laws void within their borders—setting a dangerous precedent later invoked by Southern states before the Civil War. Congress never accepted nullification, and the issue was ultimately resolved by the Civil War, which established federal supremacy.
questions.sourceIssue: Stimulus image was reused by a different released Regents source; hidden until re-imported from the original PDF.

questions.subject: U.S. History
questions.stem: Which government action most directly prompted the publication of this poster?

choices.text: passage of the Kansas-Nebraska Act
rejection of the Wilmot Proviso
adoption of the Missouri Compromise
issuance of the Emancipation Proclamation

questions.explanation: The Emancipation Proclamation (January 1, 1863), issued by President Lincoln under his war powers as commander in chief, declared that all enslaved persons in Confederate states in rebellion were 'forever free.' While it did not immediately free anyone (it applied only to areas under Confederate control), it transformed the Civil War into a moral crusade against slavery, discouraged European recognition of the Confederacy, and authorized the enlistment of Black men in the Union Army. The Regents tests students' understanding of both the legal limitations and the transformative symbolic impact of the Proclamation.

questions.subject: U.S. History
questions.stem: What was a primary purpose of the Marshall Plan?

choices.text: to support revolutions in the Middle East
to develop better relations with the Soviet Union
to contain the spread of communism
to expand United States territory

questions.explanation: The Marshall Plan's strategic purpose was to contain Soviet communism by rebuilding the market economies and democratic institutions of Western Europe, making them less susceptible to communist political movements that thrived in poverty and instability. Secretary Marshall offered the plan to all European nations, including the Soviet Union, but Moscow rejected it as an American attempt to dominate Europe economically. The Regents tests this dual humanitarian-and-strategic purpose within the broader framework of Truman Doctrine containment policy.

questions.subject: U.S. History
questions.stem: Congress enacted the Sherman Antitrust Act so that

choices.text: trade with foreign nations would be restricted
labor unions could bargain collectively
business practices that attempted to eliminate competition could be regulated
large businesses that hurt consumers could be nationalized by the government

questions.explanation: Congress passed the Sherman Antitrust Act (1890) to address the rise of industrial monopolies—particularly trusts like Standard Oil and the steel, sugar, and railroad combinations—that were using their market dominance to fix prices, crush competitors, and exploit workers and consumers. The Act declared every 'contract, combination in the form of trust or otherwise, or conspiracy in restraint of trade' to be illegal. It represented the federal government's first major regulatory intervention into the free market, reflecting the Progressive-era belief that unchecked capitalism harmed the public interest.

questions.subject: U.S. History
questions.stem: The greatest weakness of the Sherman Antitrust Act was that it

choices.text: exempted companies that lobbied members of Congress
established fines that were considered unreasonable
denied individuals their right to trial if accused of violating the act
contained vague wording and could not be easily enforced

questions.explanation: The Sherman Antitrust Act (1890) used vague language—prohibiting 'restraints of trade' and 'monopolization' without precisely defining either term—which gave corporations and their lawyers room to argue their practices were legal. For the first decade after its passage, the Act was used more against labor unions (which courts deemed 'combinations in restraint of trade') than against corporations. It was only under Theodore Roosevelt's aggressive Justice Department (1901–1909) that the Act was effectively enforced against major corporate trusts.

questions.subject: U.S. History
questions.stem: What is one impact of the Patriot Act on the United States?

choices.text: it increased international travel
it threatened civil liberties
it decreased militarization
it expanded immigration

questions.explanation: The USA PATRIOT Act (2001) expanded federal surveillance powers in ways critics argued violated the Fourth Amendment's protections against unreasonable search and seizure. Provisions allowing 'roving wiretaps,' library record searches, and monitoring of internet activity without traditional judicial oversight alarmed civil liberties organizations including the ACLU. The Regents uses this question to test students' understanding of the fundamental tension between national security and civil liberties that recurs throughout American history.

questions.subject: U.S. History
questions.stem: One way in which the Chinese Exclusion Act (1882) and the Gentlemen's Agreement (1907) are similar is that they

choices.text: led to an increase in Asian immigration
reflected nativist attitudes in the United States
eased requirements for citizenship
encouraged a policy of popular sovereignty

questions.explanation: The Chinese Exclusion Act (1882) barred Chinese laborers from immigrating to the United States—the first federal law to restrict immigration based on race and nationality—while the Gentlemen's Agreement (1907) was an informal diplomatic arrangement limiting Japanese immigration to the U.S. at Japan's request to avoid an outright exclusion law. Both policies reflected nativist fears among white workers that Asian immigrants were taking jobs and lowering wages, combined with racial prejudice. These acts are key examples of how U.S. immigration policy has been shaped by racism and economic anxiety.

questions.subject: U.S. History
questions.stem: One result of the situation shown on the graphs is that the United States

choices.text: must find markets for its surplus energy
relies mainly on European nations for its energy
leads the world in the production of oil and steel
must consider its need for energy when making foreign policy decisions

questions.explanation: The oil dependency charts show that U.S. consumption has consistently outpaced domestic production since the early 1970s, requiring massive petroleum imports from the Middle East, Canada, Venezuela, and other oil-producing regions. This dependence has directly shaped U.S. foreign policy decisions—from maintaining military forces in the Persian Gulf to supporting authoritarian oil-producing regimes. Regents questions use this data to illustrate how economic self-interest shapes foreign policy, a core theme in U.S. diplomatic history.

questions.subject: U.S. History
questions.stem: What was the primary cause of the trends shown on the graph?

choices.text: completion of the transcontinental railroad
closing of the western frontier
industrialization in the North and the Midwest
passage of the Homestead Act

questions.explanation: The dramatic shift of population from rural to urban areas between 1860 and 1920 was driven by industrialization in Northern and Midwestern cities—factory jobs in cities like Chicago, Pittsburgh, Detroit, and New York pulled workers from farms and small towns. This urbanization was reinforced by mass immigration from Southern and Eastern Europe (1880–1920), as newcomers settled in industrial cities where manufacturing jobs were available. The Regents connects this demographic transformation to Progressive-era urban reform movements and immigration restriction debates.

questions.subject: U.S. History
questions.stem: Based on the map, the Erie Canal and Hudson River contributed directly to the

choices.text: decisions to restrict immigration
acquisition of land from Canada
settlement of New England
rapid increase in population in upstate New York

questions.explanation: The Erie Canal (completed 1825) connected the Hudson River to Lake Erie, opening the Great Lakes region to commerce and making New York City the dominant port on the East Coast. The canal dramatically lowered transportation costs, enabling western grain to reach eastern markets cheaply and accelerating settlement of upstate New York and the Old Northwest. The map question tests students' understanding of how internal improvements—infrastructure projects—transformed American economic geography in the early 19th century.

questions.subject: U.S. History
questions.stem: The low level of immigration between 1930 and 1945 is most directly related to

choices.text: the Cold War and the civil rights movement
the Great Depression and World War II
improvements in living standards in Europe and Asia
passage of the Chinese Exclusion Act

questions.explanation: Immigration dropped sharply between 1930 and 1945 due to two overlapping factors: the Great Depression (1929–1941) made the U.S. economically unattractive to immigrants and led to restrictive deportation policies, while World War II (1939–1945) disrupted Atlantic shipping and made emigration from Europe dangerous or impossible. This graph question tests students' ability to identify multiple causes for a historical trend using data, a key skill in the Regents document-based analysis format.

questions.subject: U.S. History
questions.stem: Which United States foreign policy was most often used to carry out the actions shown on the map?

choices.text: Kellogg-Briand Pact
Open Door
Roosevelt Corollary to the Monroe Doctrine
Wilson's Fourteen Points

questions.explanation: The Roosevelt Corollary (1904) to the Monroe Doctrine asserted the U.S.'s right to intervene militarily in Latin American nations unable to pay international debts, making the U.S. the self-appointed police force of the Western Hemisphere. The map of U.S. military interventions in the Caribbean and Central America (1898–1934) directly reflects this policy, which led to interventions in Cuba, Haiti, Nicaragua, the Dominican Republic, and Mexico. The Regents uses this map to test students' ability to connect a specific foreign policy doctrine to its real-world applications.
questions.sourceIssue: Stimulus image was reused by a different released Regents source; hidden until re-imported from the original PDF.

questions.subject: U.S. History
questions.stem: Which conclusion is most clearly supported by the information provided on the graph?

choices.text: Unemployment after the New Deal was the same as before the stock market crash.
Deficit spending ended unemployment.
New Deal programs only partially relieved unemployment.
World War II increased unemployment.

questions.explanation: The New Deal unemployment graph shows that while FDR's programs—the Civilian Conservation Corps, Works Progress Administration, and Public Works Administration—reduced unemployment from its 1933 peak of 25%, joblessness remained above 14% through 1940 and only fell to pre-Depression levels with wartime mobilization in 1941–1942. This data supports the conclusion that the New Deal provided significant relief but did not end the Great Depression—it was World War II that finally restored full employment. Regents questions use this graph to evaluate the effectiveness of New Deal economic policy.

questions.subject: U.S. History
questions.stem: What is one impact of this amendment on American society?

choices.text: Congress cannot mandate a national religion.
Religious groups cannot lobby Congress.
The Supreme Court cannot limit free speech during wartime.
Members of the press cannot hold public office.

questions.explanation: The First Amendment (1791) prohibits Congress from making any law 'respecting an establishment of religion'—the Establishment Clause—or 'prohibiting the free exercise thereof'—the Free Exercise Clause. This dual protection means the government cannot create an official national religion AND cannot prevent people from practicing their chosen faith. Landmark Supreme Court cases like Engel v. Vitale (1962) have applied the Establishment Clause to prohibit state-sponsored prayer in public schools.

questions.subject: U.S. History
questions.stem: Which conclusion is best supported by the information on the chart?

choices.text: Business advertising had succeeded in selling more products.
Economic conditions had become worse.
Violence by labor had increased throughout the country.
The stock market had recovered in 1933.

questions.explanation: Economic data from the Great Depression (1929–1941) consistently shows rising unemployment (peaking at 25% in 1933), falling wages, collapsing bank deposits, and declining industrial output—all indicators that economic conditions had become dramatically worse than in the prosperous 1920s. The chart question tests students' ability to draw valid historical conclusions from economic data, a core skill in the Regents document analysis sections. Students should connect the data to the failure of Hoover's laissez-faire response and Roosevelt's New Deal alternative.

questions.subject: U.S. History
questions.stem: Based on these statements by Senator Long, one reason for the suffering caused by the Great Depression was the

choices.text: uneven distribution of income that favored the rich
overproduction of military equipment
failure of President Franklin D. Roosevelt to sign relief legislation
failure of labor unions to accept shorter work days

questions.explanation: Senator Huey Long (D-LA) argued during the Great Depression that the fundamental problem was extreme wealth inequality: a small elite controlled most of the nation's income and wealth while millions suffered poverty. His 'Share Our Wealth' plan proposed capping personal fortunes and guaranteeing every American family a minimum income of $5,000. Long's populist critique parallels the arguments of other Depression-era figures like Father Coughlin and reflects a broader challenge from the left to FDR's New Deal as not going far enough.
questions.sourceIssue: Stimulus image was reused by a different released Regents source; hidden until re-imported from the original PDF.

questions.subject: U.S. History
questions.stem: Which point of view is being conveyed by the artist?

choices.text: opposition to the purchase of Alaska
praise for Manifest Destiny
encouragement of industrial development
opposition to the end of slavery

questions.explanation: The painting 'American Progress' (1872) by John Gast depicts a luminous female figure (Columbia) floating westward, leading settlers, railroads, and telegraph lines into the frontier, while Native Americans and wildlife flee before her. This image is a classic visual expression of Manifest Destiny—the 19th-century belief that the United States was divinely ordained to expand from the Atlantic to the Pacific. Regents questions use this painting to test students' ability to identify ideological justifications for westward expansion and their consequences for Native Americans.

questions.subject: U.S. History
questions.stem: The author of this statement is expressing dissatisfaction with a provision included in the

choices.text: Compromise of 1850
Kansas-Nebraska Act (1854)
Oregon Treaty of 1846
Treaty of Ghent (1815)

questions.explanation: The Compromise of 1850, crafted by Henry Clay and modified by Stephen Douglas, admitted California as a free state but included a tough new Fugitive Slave Act requiring Northerners to assist in capturing escaped enslaved persons. The author expressing dissatisfaction likely represented Northern Free Soil or abolitionist opinion, outraged by the federal government compelling Northern citizens to participate in slavery. This Northern anger at the Fugitive Slave Act accelerated the growth of the Republican Party and deepened sectional tensions in the 1850s.
questions.sourceIssue: Stimulus image was reused by a different released Regents source; hidden until re-imported from the original PDF.

questions.subject: U.S. History
questions.stem: The foreign policy illustrated in this cartoon was used by the United States to

choices.text: enforce the Monroe Doctrine against Great Britain
announce the Open Door policy
secure control of land for the Panama Canal Zone
punish Mexico for siding with Germany in World War I

questions.explanation: The Roosevelt Corollary (1904) was most directly applied to justify the acquisition of the Panama Canal Zone, where the U.S. engineered Panama's independence from Colombia in 1903 and quickly negotiated the Hay-Bunau-Varilla Treaty giving the U.S. perpetual control of a 10-mile-wide canal zone. The completed Panama Canal (1914) fulfilled a strategic goal of connecting the Atlantic and Pacific fleets and dramatically shortened maritime trade routes. This episode illustrates how 'Big Stick' diplomacy combined military power with economic and strategic goals.

questions.subject: U.S. History
questions.stem: The point of view expressed by this cartoonist is that this "new" United States foreign policy is

choices.text: risky and may not succeed
certain to lead to another war
a violation of the United Nations Charter
too expensive to support

questions.explanation: The cartoon depicting a 'new' U.S. foreign policy as risky likely refers to either the policy of containment (Truman Doctrine, 1947), Eisenhower's brinkmanship (massive retaliation), or Reagan's more aggressive confrontation with the Soviet Union. The cartoonist expresses skepticism that the policy will succeed and warns of potential catastrophic consequences. The Regents tests students' ability to identify the cartoonist's point of view and evaluate historical foreign policy debates.

questions.subject: U.S. History
questions.stem: The information provided by the map best supports the conclusion that

choices.text: the transcontinental railroad linked the West and the South
the South's transportation system was more efficient
the North had transportation advantages by the start of the Civil War
most railroads were owned and operated by the United States government

questions.explanation: By 1861, the North had approximately 22,000 miles of railroad track compared to the South's 9,000 miles, and the North's rail network was far more integrated and capable of carrying heavy military traffic. This transportation advantage allowed the Union to move troops and supplies rapidly across large distances, while the Confederacy struggled with logistics. The Regents uses Civil War infrastructure maps to test students' understanding of how economic and industrial advantages translated into military outcomes.

questions.subject: U.S. History
questions.stem: Which constitutional amendment was adopted in response to the issue raised on this postcard?

choices.text: graduated income tax
limit on the number of years a president can serve
ban on poll taxes in presidential elections
direct election of United States senators

questions.explanation: The 22nd Amendment (1951) established a two-term limit for the presidency, responding to public concern about the concentration of executive power after Franklin D. Roosevelt's four consecutive terms (1933–1945). Before the 22nd Amendment, the two-term tradition was an unwritten norm established by Washington, broken only by FDR in 1940. A postcard campaign or petitions calling for a formal term limit would have preceded and contributed to Congress proposing the amendment.

questions.subject: U.S. History
questions.stem: The alleged danger referred to in this passage contributed to the

choices.text: failure of the American Federation of Labor
ratification of the Treaty of Versailles
elimination of the military draft
changing of United States immigration policy

questions.explanation: The post-World War I Red Scare (1919–1921) was fueled by fears of communist revolution inspired by the Russian Bolshevik Revolution (1917) and domestic labor unrest. Attorney General A. Mitchell Palmer used the alleged danger of immigrant radicalism to justify the Palmer Raids, which deported hundreds of foreign-born suspected radicals. Congress responded by passing the Emergency Quota Act (1921) and the National Origins Act (1924), which sharply restricted immigration from Southern and Eastern Europe—the regions associated with anarchism and communism.
questions.sourceIssue: Stimulus image was reused by a different released Regents source; hidden until re-imported from the original PDF.

questions.subject: U.S. History
questions.stem: According to the passage, the principal goal of President Truman in issuing this executive order was to

choices.text: ensure an adequate number of troops to fight in the Cold War - Mike Wallace interview with Arkansas Governor Orval Faubus, September 15, 1957
end segregation in the armed forces
stop the military draft
increase opportunities for women to serve in combat

questions.explanation: President Truman's Executive Order 9981 (July 26, 1948) desegregated the U.S. armed forces, ending the practice of maintaining separate Black and white military units that had prevailed since the Civil War. Truman acted by executive order—bypassing a Congress that would not pass civil rights legislation—after the Fahy Committee reported that segregation degraded military effectiveness and morale. This was a landmark moment in the civil rights movement, preceding the Supreme Court's Brown decision by six years.
questions.sourceIssue: Stimulus image was reused by a different released Regents source; hidden until re-imported from the original PDF.

questions.subject: U.S. History
questions.stem: Based on this passage, in 1920 the Attorney General of the United States advocated the deportation of

choices.text: wartime workers from Latin America
men who avoided the draft in World War I
citizens who criticized the government
communist and anarchist immigrants

questions.explanation: During the first Red Scare (1919–1921), Attorney General A. Mitchell Palmer led mass arrests and deportations of suspected foreign-born radicals, arguing that communist and anarchist immigrants posed an existential threat to American democracy. Palmer's use of emergency powers and disregard for due process was heavily criticized but reflected widespread public fear following the Bolshevik Revolution and a wave of bombings attributed to anarchists. The Palmer Raids are a key example of how national security fears can lead to the suppression of civil liberties.

questions.subject: U.S. History
questions.stem: The policy described by Secretary Dulles was most evident in the later actions of the United States during the

choices.text: energy crisis of 1973
Camp David talks between Egypt and Israel $
Nuclear Test Ban Treaty
Cuban missile crisis

questions.explanation: Secretary of State John Foster Dulles (1953–1959) advocated a foreign policy of 'brinkmanship'—pushing Cold War confrontations to the edge of nuclear war to force Soviet back-down—and 'massive retaliation' as a deterrent. The Cuban Missile Crisis (1962) was the clearest example of brinkmanship in action: Kennedy's naval quarantine brought the U.S. and USSR to the nuclear brink before Khrushchev agreed to remove the missiles. Regents questions connect Dulles's policy statements to specific Cold War crises to test cause-and-effect analysis.

questions.subject: U.S. History
questions.stem: What was a major reason for President Harry Truman's June 27, 1950, statement?

choices.text: the United States commitment to the Cold War policy of containment
the continuing presence of Japanese forces in Korea after World War II
an attempt to gain the support of China to halt aggression against South Korea
the announcement of a plan to reunite North and South Korea

questions.explanation: On June 25, 1950, North Korean forces invaded South Korea across the 38th parallel, and two days later President Truman ordered U.S. forces to Korea under the UN flag. Truman justified intervention as essential to the containment policy—the Truman Doctrine (1947) pledged U.S. support to free peoples resisting communist subjugation—and he acted without a formal congressional declaration of war, using UN Security Council resolutions as his authority. The Korean War (1950–1953) became the first test of containment through armed conflict.

questions.subject: U.S. History
questions.stem: Which event led to the issues described in this passage?

choices.text: Spanish-American War
World War I
Mexican-American War
Civil War

questions.explanation: The Spanish-American War (1898) resulted in the U.S. acquiring Puerto Rico, Guam, and the Philippines from Spain under the Treaty of Paris, making the U.S. an imperial power with overseas territories. The U.S. then imposed military government on the Philippines, triggering the Philippine-American War (1899–1902) when Filipino independence fighters refused to accept American colonial rule. The passage's expression of dissatisfaction likely reflects either anti-imperialist opposition or Filipino nationalist resistance to American colonialism.
questions.sourceIssue: Stimulus image was reused by a different released Regents source; hidden until re-imported from the original PDF.

questions.subject: U.S. History
questions.stem: Which action would most likely have been rejected by the authors of this passage?

choices.text: passage of an amendment for the direct election of senators
creation of the Pure Food and Drug Act
announcement of the Roosevelt Corollary
establishment of the Federal Reserve System

questions.explanation: The Monroe Doctrine (1823), which warned European powers to stay out of the Western Hemisphere, was a defensive statement of American interest in regional security and non-entanglement with European affairs. The Roosevelt Corollary (1904) transformed this defensive doctrine into an aggressive assertion of U.S. right to intervene in Latin American internal affairs—precisely what the Monroe Doctrine's anti-interventionist authors would have rejected. Regents questions test students' ability to distinguish the original intent of a document from its later application.
questions.sourceIssue: Stimulus image was reused by a different released Regents source; hidden until re-imported from the original PDF.

questions.subject: U.S. History
questions.stem: Which later presidential program is most similar to the idea expressed in this passage?

choices.text: President Ronald Reagan's supply-side fiscal policy in the 1980s
President George W. Bush's War on Terror in the 2000s
President Jimmy Carter's Camp David Accords in the 1970s
President Lyndon B. Johnson's Great Society in the 1960s

questions.explanation: Franklin Roosevelt's second bill of rights speech (January 1944) proposed economic rights including employment, food, clothing, housing, and education as essential for true freedom—ideas that influenced Lyndon Johnson's Great Society programs (1964–1968). Johnson's Great Society created Medicare, Medicaid, the Elementary and Secondary Education Act, and the Economic Opportunity Act, all aimed at eliminating poverty and expanding economic security. The Regents tests students' ability to trace connections between FDR's liberal vision and LBJ's domestic policy legacy.
questions.sourceIssue: Stimulus image was reused by a different released Regents source; hidden until re-imported from the original PDF.

questions.subject: U.S. History
questions.stem: What was the primary goal of the protest shown in this photograph?

choices.text: to oppose restrictive immigration legislation
to disagree with American involvement in World War I
to gain support for a national woman's suffrage amendment
to promote the ratification of the Treaty of Versailles

questions.explanation: Suffragists like Alice Paul and the National Woman's Party picketed the White House (1917) and organized mass marches—including the 1913 Woman Suffrage Procession in Washington, D.C.—to build public and congressional support for a national constitutional amendment guaranteeing women's right to vote. These protests demonstrated that women's suffrage required federal action, not state-by-state campaigns, and contributed to the passage and ratification of the 19th Amendment (1920). Regents questions connect protest tactics to the broader suffrage movement's legislative success.

questions.subject: U.S. History
questions.stem: In his 1902 message to Congress, President Theodore Roosevelt expressed support for the

choices.text: European colonization of Latin America
expansion of the United States as a world power
traditional United States foreign policy of isolationism
creation of a permanent United States military

questions.explanation: In his 1902 annual message to Congress, President Theodore Roosevelt articulated a vision of American expansionism and global responsibility, arguing that the U.S. should play an active role in world affairs commensurate with its growing economic and military power. This message laid the intellectual groundwork for the Roosevelt Corollary (1904), American intervention in Cuba and the Philippines, and the building of the Panama Canal. Roosevelt's foreign policy philosophy—'speak softly and carry a big stick'—represented a decisive break from 19th-century U.S. isolationism.

questions.subject: U.S. History
questions.stem: Which policy is most directly related to the point of view expressed in this cartoon?

choices.text: World War I refugees should be given jobs.
Quotas should be established for immigration.
Federal authorities should provide assistance to immigrants.
Tariff rates on imports should be increased.

questions.explanation: Post-World War I nativist cartoons typically depicted immigrants (often shown as threatening, disorderly, or un-American) flooding into the country, building public and congressional support for restricting immigration through national-origin quotas. The Emergency Quota Act (1921) and National Origins Act (1924) established strict caps on immigration from Southern and Eastern Europe, reducing total immigration from over one million annually to under 200,000. These quota laws, inspired by nativist fears of racial and ideological 'contamination,' reflected the dominant anti-immigrant sentiment of the early 1920s that such cartoons helped to create and reinforce.

questions.subject: U.S. History
questions.stem: What made the response to North Korea's invasion of South Korea different from past conflicts?

choices.text: Members of the United Nations supplied troops to defend South Korea.
The Soviet Union and the United States became allies.
The aggression of North Korea ended when the Soviet Union intervened.
Neighboring Southeast Asian nations were divided over which side to support.

questions.explanation: The Korean War (1950–1953) was the first major conflict fought under United Nations auspices, with troops from 21 nations contributing to the defense of South Korea under unified UN command (led by U.S. General Douglas MacArthur, then Matthew Ridgway). This multilateral framework distinguished Korea from World War I and II, where the U.S. joined existing alliances, and from later conflicts like Vietnam and the Gulf War, where UN authorization was more limited. The Regents tests students' understanding of how the UN system of collective security was intended to work in the post-World War II era.

questions.subject: U.S. History
questions.stem: The main idea of the cartoon is that President Barack Obama

choices.text: is better prepared than President Lyndon B. Johnson to lead the nation in war
is determined to end President Lyndon B. Johnson's foreign policy commitments
should follow the advice of President Lyndon B. Johnson
should learn from President Lyndon B. Johnson's failure in Vietnam

questions.explanation: The cartoon comparing Obama's Afghanistan surge (announced December 2009) to LBJ's Vietnam escalation reflects the debate over whether the U.S. was repeating the same mistakes—gradual troop increases without clear exit strategy, fighting a guerrilla insurgency in a culturally complex country. Johnson's escalation in Vietnam (1965–1968) ultimately produced over 58,000 American deaths without a decisive military victory, ending his presidency and driving him not to seek re-election in 1968. The Regents uses this type of comparative cartoon to test students' ability to identify historical parallels and lessons.
questions.sourceIssue: Stimulus image was reused by a different released Regents source; hidden until re-imported from the original PDF.

questions.subject: U.S. History
questions.stem: Which generalization about labor unions in the United States is most clearly supported by information on the map?

choices.text: Labor union membership was limited to mine workers.
State governments offered to arbitrate labor disputes.
The federal government supported labor union activities.
Strikes by labor unions were often suppressed by government actions.

questions.explanation: Labor union strikes in the late 19th and early 20th centuries were frequently met with state and federal government intervention on behalf of employers: the federal government used injunctions and troops to break the Pullman Strike (1894) and Homestead Strike (1892), and state governments deployed National Guard units against striking miners and mill workers. The map showing strike locations and government suppression illustrates how the legal and political system consistently favored capital over labor until the New Deal's National Labor Relations Act (1935) formally protected union organizing rights. The Regents connects labor history to the broader theme of power and government intervention in the economy.

questions.subject: U.S. History
questions.stem: According to the passage, the principal goal of President Truman in issuing this executive order was to

choices.text: stop the military draft
end segregation in the armed forces
increase opportunities for women to serve in combat
ensure an adequate number of troops to fight in the Cold War - Mike Wallace interview with Arkansas Governor Orval Faubus, September 15, 1957

questions.explanation: Executive Order 9981 (July 1948) represented President Truman's use of executive power to accomplish civil rights goals that a segregationist-influenced Congress would not legislate. Truman was motivated partly by Cold War concerns—racial segregation in the military embarrassed the U.S. in its ideological competition with the Soviet Union—and partly by the report of his Civil Rights Committee (1947), which called segregation a moral and national security problem. The order began a years-long process of desegregating military units, largely completed by the Korean War.

questions.subject: U.S. History
questions.stem: The immediate result of the action demanded in this passage was the

choices.text: creation of the Underground Railroad
start of the Trail of Tears march to the Oklahoma Territory
assimilation of Native American Indians into mainstream American culture
expansion of the rights of Native American Indians

questions.explanation: The Indian Removal Act (1830), championed by President Andrew Jackson, authorized the forced relocation of the Five Civilized Tribes (Cherokee, Chickasaw, Choctaw, Creek, and Seminole) from their traditional homelands in the Southeast to 'Indian Territory' west of the Mississippi River. The Cherokee Nation challenged their removal in the Supreme Court (Worcester v. Georgia, 1832), winning a ruling that Georgia could not force them out, but Jackson reportedly defied the ruling. The resulting forced march to Oklahoma (1838–1839) became known as the 'Trail of Tears,' during which approximately 4,000 Cherokee died from disease, exposure, and starvation.
questions.sourceIssue: Stimulus image was reused by a different released Regents source; hidden until re-imported from the original PDF.

questions.subject: U.S. History
questions.stem: Which conclusion is most clearly supported by information on the map?

choices.text: United States military action in Latin America supported European colonies.
The United States rarely used its armed forces in Latin America before World War II.
United States military action was used to protect American interests.
The United States respected the sovereignty of Latin American nations.

questions.explanation: The map showing U.S. military interventions in the Caribbean and Central America (1898–1934) demonstrates that American military force was consistently deployed to protect U.S. economic investments (fruit companies, sugar plantations, canal rights) and prevent European intervention in the region. Cuba, Haiti, Nicaragua, the Dominican Republic, and Panama all experienced direct U.S. military occupations during this period. The Regents tests students' ability to read such maps and identify the foreign policy principle (Roosevelt Corollary) that justified these interventions.
questions.sourceIssue: Stimulus image was reused by a different released Regents source; hidden until re-imported from the original PDF.

questions.subject: U.S. History
questions.stem: This amendment was adopted in 1868 primarily to

choices.text: protect the rights of formerly enslaved persons
make it easier for immigrants to become citizens
extend suffrage to settlers on the Great Plains
require the federal government to pay the costs of Reconstruction

questions.explanation: The 14th Amendment (July 1868) was ratified primarily to grant citizenship and equal protection of the laws to the four million formerly enslaved people freed by the 13th Amendment. Section 1 declared all persons born or naturalized in the U.S. to be citizens and prohibited states from denying any person equal protection or due process—directly overturning the Dred Scott decision (1857), which had declared Black Americans ineligible for citizenship. The 14th Amendment's Equal Protection Clause has since been used in landmark cases including Brown v. Board (1954), Loving v. Virginia (1967), and many others.

questions.subject: U.S. History
questions.stem: This cartoon is portraying a situation that resulted from the

choices.text: public rejection of the New Deal
economic downturn of the late 1920s
failure of the Civilian Conservation Corps
opposition to the Treaty of Versailles

questions.explanation: The Great Depression (1929–1941) produced the most devastating economic collapse in American history: unemployment reached 25%, banks failed by the thousands, farm prices collapsed, and millions lost their homes and savings. Cartoons depicting people in breadlines, Hoovervilles (shantytowns), and bank runs visually capture the human suffering of the era. The Regents regularly uses Depression-era imagery to test students' understanding of the economic crisis's causes, impacts, and the political response that produced the New Deal.

questions.subject: U.S. History
questions.stem: Which statement best expresses the cartoonist's point of view?

choices.text: Older Americans reject tax-supported government assistance.
Citizens sometimes fail to appreciate the tax-funded benefits provided by government.
More social benefits programs are needed for older Americans.

The Tea Party movement lacks public support. 47 "Attorney General Palmer Orders Raids on Reported Communists" "Senator McCarthy Launches Investigation of Accused Traitors in State Department" "Attorney General Ashcroft Authorizes Use of Wiretaps on Suspected Terrorists"

questions.explanation: The cartoon showing a citizen complaining about taxes while using public services illustrates a fundamental tension in democratic politics: people benefit from government programs (roads, schools, Social Security, national defense) while objecting to the taxes that fund them. This disconnect between public support for government spending and opposition to taxes is a recurring theme in U.S. political history, from anti-tax movements of the colonial era to the Reagan tax cuts to modern debates over deficits. The Regents uses this cartoon to test students' critical analysis of the relationship between citizens and their government.

questions.subject: U.S. History
questions.stem: Which statement is accurate about the information contained in the map?

choices.text: Suffrage was not sought by American women until 1920.
Western states lagged behind other states in granting women voting rights.
Before 1920, southern states allowed women full voting rights.
Before 1920, many states allowed women some voting privileges.

questions.explanation: The 19th Amendment (1920), which granted women the right to vote, was preceded by decades of state-level suffrage campaigns: Wyoming granted women full voting rights in 1869, Colorado in 1893, and by 1920 fifteen states had granted full suffrage and many others allowed women to vote in school board, municipal, or presidential elections. The map showing pre-1920 women's voting rights illustrates that suffrage was incrementally achieved at the state level before the federal amendment, demonstrating the multi-track nature of American constitutional reform.

questions.subject: U.S. History
questions.stem: Based on this excerpt, President Ronald Reagan's economic policy called for

choices.text: reducing taxes to increase investment by private businesses
increasing government spending on social welfare programs
limiting military spending to balance the federal budget
raising taxes to decrease inflation

questions.explanation: Reaganomics (supply-side economics), based on Reagan's 1981 Economic Recovery Tax Act, cut the top marginal income tax rate from 70% to 28% over eight years, arguing that lower taxes would unleash private investment, create jobs, and generate enough economic growth to offset the revenue loss. Reagan drew on the Laffer Curve theory, which suggested that tax cuts could be 'self-financing' through economic stimulus. Critics argued that the cuts primarily benefited the wealthy and led to large federal budget deficits.
questions.sourceIssue: Stimulus image was reused by a different released Regents source; hidden until re-imported from the original PDF.

questions.subject: U.S. History
questions.stem: President Franklin D. Roosevelt addressed the situation described in this speech by

choices.text: reducing the influence of labor unions
supporting programs to aid the poor and unemployed
promoting the interests of big business
adopting the trickle-down economic theory

questions.explanation: When FDR took office in March 1933, one-quarter of Americans were unemployed, banks were failing across the country, and farm prices had collapsed. Roosevelt's First Hundred Days produced an extraordinary burst of legislation: the Emergency Banking Act, the CCC, the AAA, and the FERA (Federal Emergency Relief Administration), which provided direct federal aid to the poor and unemployed for the first time in U.S. history. FDR's support for direct relief represented a sharp departure from Hoover's belief that federal aid would undermine self-reliance.

questions.subject: U.S. History
questions.stem: President Kennedy responded to warnings like this by

choices.text: promising to explore Mars and other nearby planets
asking Congress to fund the Hubble telescope
pledging to put a man on the Moon within a decade
joining the Soviet Union in building a space station

questions.explanation: After the Soviet Union launched Sputnik (October 4, 1957), the first artificial satellite, Americans feared the USSR had achieved technological and military superiority that threatened U.S. national security. President Kennedy channeled this anxiety into an ambitious space program, announcing in May 1961 that the U.S. would put a man on the Moon 'before this decade is out.' The Apollo program fulfilled this pledge on July 20, 1969, when Neil Armstrong became the first human to walk on the Moon—a triumph in the Cold War space race.

questions.subject: U.S. History
questions.stem: What reason does Mercy Otis Warren give for the position she stated concerning the executive and legislative branches?

choices.text: Federal courts were a threat to individual liberty.
The thirteen states could never agree on important issues.
The duties of the president and of Congress were not clearly separated.
The United States Constitution would benefit only a wealthy few.

questions.explanation: Mercy Otis Warren, in her 1788 pamphlet 'Observations on the New Constitution,' argued as an Antifederalist that the Constitution's structure failed to clearly separate the powers of the president and Congress, creating dangerous ambiguity that could lead to tyranny. She was particularly concerned about the Senate's role in executive appointments and treaties, which she saw as blurring the line between legislative and executive power. Warren's critique, along with other Antifederalist arguments, pushed the Federalists to promise the Bill of Rights as the price of ratification.

questions.subject: U.S. History
questions.stem: Based on these statements by Senator Long, one reason for the suffering caused by the Great Depression was the

choices.text: failure of President Franklin D. Roosevelt to sign relief legislation
failure of labor unions to accept shorter work days
overproduction of military equipment
uneven distribution of income that favored the rich

questions.explanation: Senator Huey Long's 'Share Our Wealth' program identified extreme income inequality as the fundamental cause of the Great Depression: too much money was concentrated in the hands of a few wealthy individuals, leaving ordinary consumers without enough purchasing power to sustain the economy. Long's populist critique resonated with millions of Americans who felt the New Deal's benefits were too modest and too slow to reach ordinary working people. This question tests students' understanding of Depression-era inequality arguments as both cause analysis and political challenge to FDR.

questions.subject: U.S. History
questions.stem: Anthony's statement is part of her campaign to gain

choices.text: woman's suffrage
a progressive income tax
consumer safety regulations
the abolition of slavery

questions.explanation: Susan B. Anthony (1820–1906) spent over 50 years campaigning for women's suffrage—the right of women to vote—arguing that the 14th Amendment's guarantee of equal citizenship rights should logically include the right to vote. Anthony was arrested and tried in 1872 for illegally voting in a presidential election in Rochester, New York, turning her trial into a national platform for the suffrage cause. Her lifelong advocacy contributed to the eventual passage of the 19th Amendment in 1920, fourteen years after her death.

questions.subject: U.S. History
questions.stem: Which three individuals would historians most likely study in conjunction with this passage?

choices.text: Ida Tarbell, Upton Sinclair, and Jacob Riis
Terence Powderly, Samuel Gompers, and Eugene Debs
John D. Rockefeller, Andrew Carnegie, and Henry Ford
Sojourner Truth, William Lloyd Garrison, and Harriet Tubman

questions.explanation: The abolitionist movement drew together Black and white activists: Sojourner Truth (formerly enslaved, powerful orator for abolition and women's rights), William Lloyd Garrison (founder of The Liberator newspaper and American Anti-Slavery Society), and Harriet Tubman (Underground Railroad conductor who personally freed hundreds of enslaved people). All three reflect the passage's themes of moral opposition to slavery and active resistance, connecting individual biography to the broader movement that built political pressure for emancipation. The Regents uses this type of question to test students' ability to identify individuals associated with specific historical movements.
questions.sourceIssue: Stimulus image was reused by a different released Regents source; hidden until re-imported from the original PDF.

questions.subject: U.S. History
questions.stem: Which presidential role is being demonstrated in the photograph?

choices.text: commander in chief of the armed forces
granting reprieves and pardons for federal offenses
treaty-making powers with foreign nations
chief executive approving congressional bills

questions.explanation: The photograph showing a president signing legislation into law illustrates the chief executive's role in the constitutional system: Congress passes bills, but only the president's signature (or a congressional override of his veto) makes them law, per Article I, Section 7. This is a fundamental expression of the separation of powers and checks and balances—the president exercises the veto power as a check on Congress, while Congress can override with a two-thirds majority. Regents questions on presidential roles test students' knowledge of the constitutional framework for legislation.
questions.sourceIssue: Stimulus image was reused by a different released Regents source; hidden until re-imported from the original PDF.

questions.subject: U.S. History
questions.stem: What was one method that was used to deny African Americans the voting rights mentioned in this speech?

choices.text: literacy tests and poll taxes
bus boycotts and sit-ins
student protests
deployment of federal troops

questions.explanation: Despite the 15th Amendment's guarantee of voting rights (1870), Southern states systematically disenfranchised Black voters through literacy tests (requiring prospective voters to interpret obscure constitutional passages to a white registrar's satisfaction), poll taxes (making voting too expensive for poor sharecroppers), and grandfather clauses (allowing whites to vote without tests if their grandfathers had voted, while excluding most Black men). These Jim Crow voter suppression tactics kept Black Southerners effectively disenfranchised until the Voting Rights Act of 1965 outlawed them.

questions.subject: U.S. History
questions.stem: Which action did President Eisenhower take to address the issue raised in this interview?

choices.text: sending federal troops to protect African American students in Arkansas
signing the Voting Rights Act passed by Congress
forcing the resignation of Arkansas Governor Orval Faubus
desegregating all southern schools

questions.explanation: After the Supreme Court's Brown v. Board of Education decision (1954–1955) ordered school desegregation 'with all deliberate speed,' Arkansas Governor Orval Faubus ordered the National Guard to block nine Black students from entering Little Rock Central High School in September 1957. President Eisenhower, who had been reluctant to use federal power on civil rights, federalized the Arkansas National Guard and deployed 101st Airborne Division paratroopers to escort the 'Little Rock Nine' into school, enforcing the federal court order. The crisis demonstrated that the executive branch would use force to uphold federal judicial authority against state resistance.

questions.subject: U.S. History
questions.stem: What conclusion can be drawn from President Johnson's statement?

choices.text: President Johnson favored policies to deregulate industry.
American consumption of poultry was declining.
The federal government should take ownership of meatpacking plants.
President Johnson supported the expansion of consumer protection.

questions.explanation: President Lyndon Johnson's Consumer Credit Protection Act and related Great Society legislation reflected his conviction that the federal government had an obligation to protect ordinary Americans from exploitation by powerful corporations and financial institutions. Johnson's Great Society (1964–1968) encompassed consumer protection, environmental regulation, education funding, and anti-poverty programs, expanding the federal government's role as protector of individual rights against corporate power. The Regents links Johnson's domestic legislative record to the broader tradition of progressive federal intervention in the economy.

questions.subject: U.S. History
questions.stem: What was the primary reason President Andrew Jackson supported the removal of Native Americans from their tribal lands?

choices.text: it provided fertile farmland to Native Americans
it gave political support to Native Americans
it opened Native American land to white settlement
it granted citizenship to Native Americans

questions.explanation: President Andrew Jackson championed the Indian Removal Act (1830) by arguing that moving Native Americans west of the Mississippi would protect them from the corrupting influence of white civilization and give them space to develop at their own pace—while conveniently opening millions of fertile acres in Georgia, Alabama, Mississippi, and Tennessee to white settlement. The Supreme Court ruled in Worcester v. Georgia (1832) that Georgia had no authority over the Cherokee, but Jackson and Georgia state authorities proceeded with removal anyway.
