The resulting Trail of Tears (1838–1839) resulted in the deaths of approximately 4,000 Cherokee from disease, cold, and starvation.

questions.subject: U.S. History
questions.stem: Congress enacted the Sherman Antitrust Act so that

choices.text: trade with foreign nations would be restricted
business practices that attempted to eliminate competition could be regulated
large businesses that hurt consumers could be nationalized by the government
labor unions could bargain collectively

questions.explanation: Congress passed the Sherman Antitrust Act (1890) to prohibit business practices—like trusts, price-fixing agreements, and collusive market-sharing schemes—that restrained competition and harmed consumers. The Act made it illegal to 'monopolize, or attempt to monopolize' any part of interstate commerce, giving the federal government its first major tool to regulate corporate power. However, the Act's vague language and initial weak enforcement limited its impact until Theodore Roosevelt's aggressive antitrust prosecution after 1901 established it as an effective regulatory instrument.

questions.subject: U.S. History
questions.stem: Which event led to this letter being written?

choices.text: Iran-Contra affair
Watergate break-in
Teapot Dome scandal
Kent State shootings

questions.explanation: The Watergate break-in (June 17, 1972), in which operatives employed by Nixon's reelection committee broke into the Democratic National Committee offices, triggered an 18-month constitutional crisis. When the White House tape recordings revealed Nixon had personally directed the cover-up just days after the break-in, the House Judiciary Committee voted to recommend impeachment for obstruction of justice, abuse of power, and contempt of Congress. Nixon resigned on August 9, 1974—the only U.S. president ever to do so—rather than face near-certain impeachment and removal.
questions.sourceIssue: Stimulus image was reused by a different released Regents source; hidden until re-imported from the original PDF.

questions.subject: U.S. History
questions.stem: This cartoon was published in response to the

choices.text: dispute over the presidential election between George W. Bush and Al Gore
ratification of the 22nd amendment limiting presidential terms
influence of third-party candidates in presidential elections
decision of the Supreme Court establishing the “one man-one vote” principle

questions.explanation: The 2000 presidential election was decided by Florida's 25 electoral votes; the initial count showed George W. Bush leading Al Gore by just 537 votes out of nearly six million cast, triggering automatic machine recounts and legal battles over hand recounts of disputed ballots. The Supreme Court's 5–4 decision in Bush v. Gore (December 12, 2000) halted the recount, effectively awarding Florida—and the presidency—to Bush despite Gore winning the national popular vote by over half a million votes. The controversy renewed calls to abolish the Electoral College and adopt a direct popular vote for president.

questions.subject: U.S. History
questions.stem: The main idea of the cartoon is that President Barack Obama

choices.text: is better prepared than President Lyndon B. Johnson to lead the nation in war
should learn from President Lyndon B. Johnson’s failure in Vietnam
is determined to end President Lyndon B. Johnson’s foreign policy commitments
should follow the advice of President Lyndon B. Johnson

questions.explanation: The cartoon comparing Obama's 2009 Afghanistan surge to LBJ's Vietnam escalation reflects the 'quagmire' analogy popular among war critics: both situations involved gradually escalating U.S. military involvement in a foreign civil conflict with unclear goals, no reliable local government partner, and determined insurgent opposition. Johnson's Vietnam escalation cost over 58,000 American lives and ultimately ended without achieving its stated objectives, driving LBJ from seeking reelection in 1968. The cartoon argues that Obama risked repeating LBJ's fatal mistake of 'mission creep' without a viable exit strategy.

questions.subject: U.S. History
questions.stem: W. E. B. Du Bois and Booker T. Washington strongly disagreed over the

choices.text: need for passage of the 14th amendment to acquire civil rights
use of the federal policy of affirmative action to aid African Americans
method and speed for attaining equal rights for African Americans
benefits of the Harlem Renaissance

questions.explanation: W.E.B. Du Bois, co-founder of the NAACP (1909) and editor of The Crisis, argued for immediate full civil and political equality, including integrated education at the highest levels; Booker T. Washington, founder of the Tuskegee Institute, advocated economic self-improvement and vocational training as the path to eventual equality, accepting temporary political subordination in exchange for economic opportunity. Their famous debate—'immediate integration' vs. 'gradualism'—represented a fundamental disagreement about the speed and methods of achieving racial equality. The Regents tests students' ability to distinguish their specific philosophical differences.

questions.subject: U.S. History
questions.stem: The USA Patriot Act (2001) was passed to

choices.text: protect the United States from terrorists
prohibit citizens from criticizing government policies
safeguard civil liberties from abuse by the government
recruit volunteers for military service

questions.explanation: The USA PATRIOT Act (October 26, 2001), passed just 45 days after the September 11 attacks with minimal congressional debate, expanded federal law enforcement surveillance powers to investigate terrorist networks operating within the United States. The Act allowed 'sneak and peek' searches, broad wiretapping authority, and monitoring of financial and library records with limited judicial oversight, aiming to remove the legal barriers that had prevented intelligence and law enforcement agencies from sharing information before 9/11. While protecting national security was its stated goal, civil liberties advocates argued it overreached constitutional boundaries.

questions.subject: U.S. History
questions.stem: The decision of the Supreme Court in the case of United States v. Nixon (1974) stated that

choices.text: congressional approval of the War Powers Act of 1973 violated the separation of powers
executive branch officials could be fired by the president without the Senate’s approval
President Richard Nixon as commander in chief could extend the Vietnam War into Cambodia
executive privilege did not allow the president to withhold recordings that were related to the Watergate affair

questions.explanation: In United States v. Nixon (1974), the Supreme Court ruled unanimously that President Nixon's claim of executive privilege—the president's right to keep presidential communications confidential—did not extend to withholding evidence relevant to a criminal prosecution. The Court ordered Nixon to turn over 64 White House tape recordings to Special Prosecutor Leon Jaworski, and the tapes revealed Nixon's direct involvement in the Watergate cover-up. Faced with certain impeachment and removal, Nixon resigned 16 days after the ruling, demonstrating that no one, not even the president, is above the law.

questions.subject: U.S. History
questions.stem: During the 1930s, poor land management and severe drought conditions across parts of the Midwest resulted in the

choices.text: development of Dust Bowl conditions on the Great Plains
formation of the United States Department of Agriculture
creation of wheat surpluses
growth of the Granger movement

questions.explanation: The Dust Bowl (1930–1936) resulted from a combination of severe drought across the Great Plains and decades of over-plowing that had removed the native prairie grass holding the topsoil in place. When sustained drought arrived, massive 'black blizzards' of topsoil darkened skies from Texas to Canada, burying farms and towns. Hundreds of thousands of 'Okies' and 'Arkies' migrated west to California, fleeing both the dust and the Depression's economic devastation—an internal migration documented in John Steinbeck's The Grapes of Wrath (1939).

questions.subject: U.S. History
questions.stem: President Franklin D. Roosevelt referred to December 7, 1941, as “a date which will live in infamy” because on that day

choices.text: Germany invaded Poland
Japan attacked Pearl Harbor
Italy declared war on the United States
the United States dropped an atomic bomb on Hiroshima

questions.explanation: President Roosevelt called December 7, 1941—the date Japan launched a surprise attack on the U.S. naval base at Pearl Harbor, Hawaii, killing 2,403 Americans and destroying or damaging 20 vessels and 300 aircraft—'a date which will live in infamy' in his address to Congress requesting a declaration of war. The attack ended American isolationism overnight and united public opinion behind U.S. entry into World War II. Congress declared war on Japan the same day, and Germany and Italy declared war on the U.S. four days later.

questions.subject: U.S. History
questions.stem: A major difference between the philosophies of President Herbert Hoover and President Franklin D. Roosevelt in responding to the Great Depression is that Roosevelt

choices.text: stressed the need for individual self-reliance
supported direct relief to people out of work
thought the government should not be involved in economic reform
wanted to rely on private charities to provide assistance

questions.explanation: President Hoover believed the federal government should not directly relieve poverty, instead relying on voluntary charity and local government, arguing that direct federal relief would undermine individual self-reliance and damage the economy. Roosevelt rejected this philosophy entirely, creating the Federal Emergency Relief Administration (FERA, 1933), which provided direct federal grants to states to fund unemployment relief. This fundamental disagreement about the government's responsibility to provide direct relief to individuals in distress represents one of the sharpest ideological divides in American political history.

questions.subject: U.S. History
questions.stem: One outcome of the situation referred to in the cartoon was that

choices.text: President Roosevelt decided not to seek reelection
President Roosevelt was impeached for abuse of his powers
the Supreme Court ended most New Deal programs
Congress rejected President Roosevelt’s effort to pack the Supreme Court

questions.explanation: The 1937 'court-packing' plan was defeated in Congress when conservative Democrats joined Republicans in opposing what they saw as an unprecedented executive power grab. Chief Justice Charles Evans Hughes sent Congress a letter demonstrating that the Court was current on its caseload—undermining FDR's stated rationale that additional justices were needed for efficiency. The plan's defeat showed that even a president with overwhelming Democratic majorities and a recent landslide reelection could be checked by Congress when he overreached on constitutional matters.

questions.subject: U.S. History
questions.stem: Which New Deal agency had the creation of new jobs as its primary goal?

choices.text: Federal Deposit Insurance Corporation (FDIC)
Works Progress Administration (WPA)
Agricultural Adjustment Administration (AAA)
Securities and Exchange Commission (SEC)

questions.explanation: The Works Progress Administration (WPA, 1935–1943), created as part of FDR's Second New Deal, was the largest New Deal agency, employing 8.5 million Americans in construction, infrastructure, arts, and literacy programs over its eight-year lifespan. The WPA built or improved 650,000 miles of roads, 125,000 public buildings, 75,000 bridges, and employed artists, writers, and musicians through the Federal Arts, Writers', and Theater Projects. Its primary purpose—job creation through direct federal employment—distinguished it from relief programs that simply provided cash payments.

questions.subject: U.S. History
questions.stem: President Theodore Roosevelt was called a trustbuster because he

choices.text: encouraged conservation of natural resources
resigned from the Republican Party
directed the building of the Panama Canal
supported legal action against business monopolies

questions.explanation: Theodore Roosevelt earned the 'trustbuster' label because he was the first president to aggressively use the Sherman Antitrust Act (1890) to break up industrial monopolies, filing 44 antitrust suits compared to his predecessors' handful. His most famous action was the Northern Securities case (1904), in which the Supreme Court ordered the dissolution of J.P. Morgan's railroad trust, demonstrating that no corporation was too powerful to be regulated. Roosevelt's trustbusting reflected the Progressive-era conviction that concentrated corporate power threatened democratic equality and the public interest.

questions.subject: U.S. History
questions.stem: Which presidential action is an example of the use of the unwritten constitution?

choices.text: vetoing a bill passed by Congress
nominating an ambassador to France
submitting a treaty to the Senate for ratification
holding a cabinet meeting at the White House

questions.explanation: The unwritten constitution consists of political practices and customs that have developed over time but are not explicitly written in the constitutional text—including the cabinet, political parties, the two-term tradition (before the 22nd Amendment), executive agreements, and the practice of holding cabinet meetings. George Washington established the cabinet precedent in 1789 by meeting regularly with the heads of the State, War, and Treasury departments. The cabinet is the classic example of an unwritten constitutional tradition, appearing in almost every Regents question on this topic.

questions.subject: U.S. History
questions.stem: During the 1890s, many American farmers tried to resolve their economic problems by

choices.text: supporting government aid to railroads
joining the Populist Party
asking Congress to end agricultural subsidies
staging violent protests against the government

questions.explanation: The Populist Party (People's Party), founded in 1892, emerged from the Farmers' Alliance movement as a vehicle for farmers' economic grievances: falling crop prices, high railroad freight rates, tight money supply (gold standard), and debt. Populists demanded a graduated income tax, direct election of senators, government ownership of railroads, and 'free silver' (bimetallic currency to inflate the money supply and ease farmers' debt burdens). The 1896 Democratic Party's adoption of free silver and nominee William Jennings Bryan effectively absorbed and ended the Populist Party as a separate force.

questions.subject: U.S. History
questions.stem: One way in which the Chinese Exclusion Act (1882) and the Gentlemen’s Agreement (1907) are similar is that they

choices.text: led to an increase in Asian immigration
eased requirements for citizenship
reflected nativist attitudes in the United States
encouraged a policy of popular sovereignty

questions.explanation: The Chinese Exclusion Act (1882) was the first federal law to bar immigration based on race and nationality, prohibiting Chinese laborers from entering the U.S. amid nativist fears that Chinese workers were taking jobs from white Americans. The Gentlemen's Agreement (1907) was a diplomatic arrangement in which Japan agreed to limit emigration of laborers to the U.S. to avoid a humiliating formal exclusion act like the Chinese one. Both policies reflected the nativist conviction that Asian immigrants were racially and culturally incompatible with American democracy—attitudes that would persist in immigration law until 1965.

questions.subject: U.S. History
questions.stem: The Senate’s opposition to United States membership in the League of Nations was based mainly on the

choices.text: fear of being drawn into future wars
failure to give the United States veto power
cost of membership dues
concern that United States businesses would be damaged

questions.explanation: The Senate rejected the Treaty of Versailles (1919) twice, primarily because of objections to Article X of the League of Nations Covenant, which critics (led by Senate Foreign Relations Committee Chairman Henry Cabot Lodge) argued could obligate the U.S. to go to war to defend member nations without a congressional declaration of war. The 'irreconcilables' in the Senate opposed any league membership; the 'reservationists' would accept membership with modifications protecting congressional war-making authority. Wilson's refusal to accept Lodge's reservations doomed ratification and left the U.S. outside the League.

questions.subject: U.S. History
questions.stem: The Great Compromise enabled delegates at the Constitutional Convention (1787) to

choices.text: provide for the indirect election of the president through the electoral college
protect the interests of states with small populations and states with large populations
establish the principle of popular sovereignty in the territories
give Congress the exclusive right to declare war if the nation is attacked

questions.explanation: The Great Compromise (Connecticut Compromise, 1787), proposed by Roger Sherman of Connecticut, resolved the standoff between large and small states over congressional representation by creating a bicameral legislature: the Senate, where each state gets two seats regardless of population (protecting small states), and the House of Representatives, where seats are apportioned by population (giving large states greater influence). This compromise was essential to securing ratification and remains the structural foundation of Congress today. Regents questions test students' understanding of why both large and small states had legitimate representation concerns.

questions.subject: U.S. History
questions.stem: The decision in Marbury v. Madison (1803) was significant because it established that the Supreme Court

choices.text: had limited powers over state courts
could impeach the president and other government officials
had the power to choose its own members
could declare a federal law unconstitutional

questions.explanation: Marbury v. Madison (1803) is the foundational Supreme Court case establishing judicial review—the Court's power to strike down laws that violate the Constitution. Chief Justice John Marshall's decision in the case simultaneously appeared to give something to both sides while actually establishing a sweeping new power: he denied Marbury his commission (satisfying the Jefferson administration) but declared the Judiciary Act provision that would have granted it unconstitutional (establishing judicial review). This is the most tested Supreme Court case on every cycle of the U.S. History Regents.

questions.subject: U.S. History
questions.stem: Which constitutional provision is most clearly illustrated by these headlines?

choices.text: presidential power
qualifications to be president
presidential succession
advice and consent of the Senate

questions.explanation: The constitutional provision for presidential succession (25th Amendment, 1967, and earlier succession acts) determines who takes the presidency if the president dies, resigns, or is removed. High-profile succession events (Kennedy assassination 1963, Nixon resignation 1974) tested and shaped these provisions. The 25th Amendment also created procedures for declaring a president temporarily disabled and for filling a vice-presidential vacancy—gaps exposed by situations like Vice President Agnew's 1973 resignation just before Nixon's.
questions.sourceIssue: Stimulus image was reused by a different released Regents source; hidden until re-imported from the original PDF.

questions.subject: U.S. History
questions.stem: The authors of the passage would have been most critical of the activities of which two individuals?

choices.text: Margaret Sanger and Jane Addams
William Jennings Bryan and Eugene V. Debs
Jacob Riis and Ida Tarbell
John D. Rockefeller and Andrew Carnegie

questions.explanation: The authors of the passage criticizing industrial monopolists would have been most critical of John D. Rockefeller (Standard Oil, which controlled 90% of U.S. oil refining by 1880) and Andrew Carnegie (Carnegie Steel, the world's largest steel company), two of the most powerful industrialists of the Gilded Age. Both men used aggressive business practices—horizontal integration, price-fixing, and crushing competitors—that Progressive reformers identified as the target of antitrust legislation. The Regents uses this question to connect muckraker critiques of corporate power to specific historical figures.
questions.sourceIssue: Stimulus image was reused by a different released Regents source; hidden until re-imported from the original PDF.

questions.subject: U.S. History
questions.stem: Which presidential role is being demonstrated in the photograph?

choices.text: treaty-making powers with foreign nations
chief executive approving congressional bills
commander in chief of the armed forces
granting reprieves and pardons for federal offenses

questions.explanation: The photograph showing a president signing legislation—typically surrounded by members of Congress and other officials—illustrates the chief executive function: Article II of the Constitution gives the president the power to sign or veto bills passed by Congress, making bill-signing the most visible exercise of executive authority in domestic policy. Presidents often use ceremonial bill-signings to claim credit for popular legislation and distribute commemorative signing pens to key supporters. The Regents tests presidential roles to ensure students understand the constitutional framework of executive power.

questions.subject: Global History
questions.stem: What is the significance of Mikhail Gorbachev’s speech?

choices.label: A
choices.text: It signaled the end of the Cold War.

choices.label: B
choices.text: It predicted the eventual worldwide spread of communism.

choices.label: C
choices.text: It led to a new nuclear arms race.

choices.label: D
choices.text: It caused new countries to become part of the Soviet Union.

questions.explanation: Mikhail Gorbachev's 1988 UN speech, combined with his policies of glasnost (openness) and perestroika (restructuring), publicly acknowledged that the Soviet bloc countries could choose their own paths — effectively abandoning the Brezhnev Doctrine. This signaled the end of the Cold War by removing Soviet coercive control over Eastern Europe, which triggered the 1989 revolutions and ultimately the 1991 USSR dissolution. Regents CRQs often pair this speech with the fall of the Berlin Wall as a turning-point pair.

questions.subject: Global History
questions.stem: Which event ended the cooperation shown in these documents?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Russian Revolution

choices.label: B
choices.text: fall of France to Germany

choices.label: C
choices.text: German invasion of the Soviet Union

choices.label: D
choices.text: Nuremberg trials

questions.explanation: The Nazi-Soviet Non-Aggression Pact of August 1939 initially allowed Hitler and Stalin to cooperate by dividing Eastern Europe; however, Operation Barbarossa — Germany's surprise invasion of the Soviet Union on June 22, 1941 — immediately shattered that arrangement. The invasion was a turning point in WWII, forcing the USSR to join the Allied Powers, and opened the Eastern Front where roughly 27 million Soviet citizens perished. Students often confuse the Pact's collapse with the 1939 invasion of Poland; the key is that Barbarossa, not Poland, ended Soviet-German cooperation.

questions.subject: Global History
questions.stem: Which event directly resulted from the situation shown in this cartoon?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Russian Revolution

choices.label: B
choices.text: Agricultural Revolution

choices.label: C
choices.text: Cultural Revolution

choices.label: D
choices.text: French Revolution

questions.explanation: The cartoon depicts the severe tax burden and feudal inequality of the Old Regime (Ancien Régime) in France, where the Third Estate (commoners) bore the financial weight while the clergy and nobility were largely exempt. These Enduring Issues of inequality and economic injustice directly sparked the French Revolution beginning in 1789, when rising bread prices and Enlightenment ideology combined to fuel revolt. Regents questions frequently test whether students can connect visual grievances to specific turning points.

questions.subject: Global History
questions.stem: Based on this map, which two countries acquired the most foreign territory?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Great Britain and France

choices.label: B
choices.text: the Netherlands and Belgium

choices.label: C
choices.text: Germany and Italy

choices.label: D
choices.text: Spain and Portugal

questions.explanation: The 1884–85 Berlin Conference divided Africa among European powers, with Great Britain and France ultimately claiming the largest shares — Britain took roughly 30% and France about 35% of the continent by 1914. This Scramble for Africa exemplifies imperialism and the Enduring Issue of power, as European nations exploited African resources, labor, and markets with little regard for existing ethnic boundaries. Students often confuse the Berlin Conference with the Congress of Vienna; the Berlin Conference specifically concerned Africa.

questions.subject: Global History
questions.stem: Which concept is most closely associated with the situation presented in map A?

choices.label: A
choices.text: nonalignment

choices.label: B
choices.text: appeasement

choices.label: C
choices.text: Zionism

choices.label: D
choices.text: colonialism

questions.explanation: Map A depicts the domination of African or Asian territories by European powers during the Age of Imperialism (roughly 1870–1914), which is the defining feature of colonialism — the political and economic control of one nation over another. Colonialism created extractive economies and disrupted indigenous political structures, leaving legacies of underdevelopment that persist today. The Enduring Issue here is power imbalance between colonizer and colonized; nationalism (Map B) often arose as a direct response to colonial rule.

questions.subject: Global History
questions.stem: According to this National Geographic article, why was Norman Borlaug awarded the Nobel Peace Prize?

choices.label: A
choices.text: He ended famines by moving the production of grains to the Philippines.

choices.label: B
choices.text: He transformed global agricultural production.

choices.label: C
choices.text: He was able to decrease the surplus population in areas of famine.

choices.label: D
choices.text: He targeted poverty by providing food donations to the less fortunate.

questions.explanation: Norman Borlaug received the 1970 Nobel Peace Prize for his development of high-yield, disease-resistant wheat varieties in the 1940s–1960s, fundamentally transforming global agriculture during the Green Revolution. His work helped avert mass famine in South Asia and Latin America, demonstrating how scientific innovation (Enduring Issue: science and technology) can address population crises. Regents CRQs pair Borlaug with Malthusian predictions or the Indian famine of 1943 to test cause-and-effect reasoning.

questions.subject: Global History
questions.stem: The events described in the passage led to the

choices.label: A
choices.text: Japanese surrender in World War II

choices.label: B
choices.text: D-Day invasion of Normandy

choices.label: C
choices.text: collapse of the Soviet Union

choices.label: D
choices.text: creation of spheres of influence in China

questions.explanation: The passage describes the atomic bombings of Hiroshima (August 6, 1945) and Nagasaki (August 9, 1945), which killed an estimated 110,000–210,000 people and persuaded Emperor Hirohito to announce Japan's unconditional surrender on August 15, 1945, ending World War II in the Pacific. This turning point also inaugurated the nuclear age, intensifying Cold War arms competition between the US and USSR. Students must distinguish Japan's surrender from Germany's surrender (May 1945, V-E Day).

questions.subject: Global History
questions.stem: This illustration and excerpt depict events from which time and place in history?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Meiji Japan

choices.label: B
choices.text: Revolutionary France

choices.label: C
choices.text: Victorian England

choices.label: D
choices.text: Soviet Russia

questions.explanation: The illustration and excerpt reference conditions of rapid industrialization in 19th-century Britain — child labor, urban poverty, dangerous factory conditions, and class inequality — hallmarks of Victorian England (approximately 1837–1901). The Industrial Revolution transformed England first, creating the world's most powerful economy but also severe social dislocation that gave rise to socialism and labor reform movements. Regents CRQs often pair this with the Factory Acts or Charles Dickens's writing as evidence of calls for reform.

questions.subject: Global History
questions.stem: Which concept most influenced the major changes shown on this map?

choices.label: A
choices.text: universal suffrage

choices.label: B
choices.text: isolationism

choices.label: C
choices.text: nationalism

choices.label: D
choices.text: emancipation of serfs

questions.explanation: The map shows the dramatic redrawing of European borders after World War I, largely driven by the principle of self-determination and nationalist ambitions of ethnic groups formerly under the Ottoman, Austro-Hungarian, Russian, and German empires. Nationalism — the belief that people sharing culture, language, or ethnicity deserve their own state — was the primary concept reshaping these borders via the 1919 Paris Peace Conference treaties. Students should note that nationalism can both unify (Italy 1861, Germany 1871) and fragment (Austria-Hungary 1918).

questions.subject: Global History
questions.stem: Which topic could best be studied by analyzing this excerpt?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Russian Revolution

choices.label: B
choices.text: World War I

choices.label: C
choices.text: World War II

choices.label: D
choices.text: Cold War

questions.explanation: The excerpt about nuclear deterrence, ideological competition, and proxy conflicts between superpowers describes defining features of the Cold War (1947–1991) — a geopolitical struggle between the United States and the Soviet Union that stopped short of direct military conflict. Key Cold War flashpoints tested on the Regents include the Korean War, Cuban Missile Crisis, Vietnam War, and Soviet invasion of Afghanistan. CRQs often pair Cold War documents with the Truman Doctrine or NSC-68 to test understanding of containment policy.

questions.subject: Global History
questions.stem: Which event is being depicted in this cartoon?

choices.label: A
choices.text: the rule of Czar Nicholas II

choices.label: B
choices.text: the decrease in the power of the Orthodox church

choices.label: C
choices.text: the creation of the International Criminal Court

choices.label: D
choices.text: the collapse of the Soviet Union

questions.explanation: The cartoon depicts the 1991 dissolution of the Soviet Union, when 15 constituent republics declared independence following decades of economic stagnation, nationalist movements, and the weakening of central authority under Gorbachev's reforms. The collapse ended the Cold War bipolarity and fundamentally reordered global politics. Students sometimes confuse the fall of the Berlin Wall (1989) with the USSR's formal dissolution (December 25, 1991); the cartoon's imagery of a collapsing structure points specifically to the latter.

questions.subject: Global History
questions.stem: Which claim about nationalism is best supported by Maps A and B?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Nationalism has played a limited role in the political makeup of European countries.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Nationalism is both a unifying and a dividing force.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Nationalism has not been found to lead to military conflicts.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Nationalism only occurs in western European countries.

questions.explanation: The maps illustrate how nationalism served as a unifying force when it consolidated fragmented states (e.g., German and Italian unification in 1871) but simultaneously acted as a dividing force when it broke apart multiethnic empires (e.g., the Ottoman and Austro-Hungarian empires after 1918). This dual nature of nationalism is a core Enduring Issue theme on the Regents. CRQs routinely ask students to identify evidence from maps or documents showing nationalism both creating and destroying states.

questions.subject: Global History
questions.stem: Which piece of evidence would best support the claim about the environment in this article?

choices.label: A
choices.text: a chart showing water flow rates of African rivers

choices.label: B
choices.text: a topographic map of Africa

choices.label: C
choices.text: a graph showing air pollution levels in Africa

choices.label: D
choices.text: a diary of an African mine worker

questions.explanation: The passage makes a claim about environmental degradation, and a graph showing air pollution levels in Africa would provide quantitative evidence directly supporting or refuting that specific claim. The Enduring Issue of environmental impact is a major Regents theme, connecting industrialization and global economic systems to pollution, deforestation, and climate change. Students must distinguish between types of evidence: a graph offers empirical data, while a news article or photograph provides qualitative context.

questions.subject: Global History
questions.stem: Based on this passage, how did the 1979 Iranian Revolution affect the lives of women?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The Family Protection Law allowed women to petition for divorce.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Women were first granted the right to run for parliament and help create laws.

choices.label: C
choices.text: The legal age for marriage was raised to the age of 18.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Women’s legal rights became more limited with the dismantling of Family Courts.

questions.explanation: Iran's 1979 Islamic Revolution under Ayatollah Khomeini replaced the secular Pahlavi monarchy with a theocratic state governed by Islamic law (sharia), which reversed many rights women had gained under the Shah, including requirements to wear the hijab, restrictions on divorce and travel, and limits on professional roles. This represents the Enduring Issue of human rights and the tension between religious authority and individual freedoms. Regents CRQs contrast pre- and post-revolutionary Iran to test students' understanding of political turning points.

questions.subject: Global History
questions.stem: These speakers’ ideas and arguments were influenced by

choices.label: A
choices.text: colonial expansion happening in Africa after the Berlin Conference

choices.label: B
choices.text: beliefs developed during the Enlightenment

choices.label: C
choices.text: nationalist uprisings occurring in Southern Europe

choices.label: D
choices.text: practices developed at the beginning of the Industrial Revolution

questions.explanation: The speakers' arguments for natural rights, rational government, and individual liberty reflect Enlightenment philosophy developed by thinkers such as Locke, Voltaire, Montesquieu, and Rousseau in the 17th and 18th centuries. Enlightenment ideas that governments derive legitimacy from the consent of the governed directly inspired the American Declaration of Independence (1776), French Declaration of the Rights of Man (1789), and Latin American independence movements. Regents CRQs frequently trace the transmission of these ideas from Europe to the Americas.

questions.subject: Global History
questions.stem: Based on this map, what was a significant challenge faced by the Ottoman Empire between 1800 and 1913?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Rapid expansion led to high levels of inflation.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Acquiring lands in Eastern Europe led to religious uprisings.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Addition of territories in Africa required a large military presence.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Nationalistic movements led to territorial losses.

questions.explanation: The map shows the steady territorial losses of the Ottoman Empire from roughly the 1870s through WWI, driven by nationalist independence movements among Balkan peoples — Greeks, Serbs, Bulgarians, Romanians — who sought to establish ethnically defined states. This is the Enduring Issue of nationalism as a dividing force, as the once-dominant 'Sick Man of Europe' lost control of territory it had held for centuries. Students should recognize that Ottoman weakness in the Balkans also contributed to the conditions leading to WWI.

questions.subject: Global History
questions.stem: Which statement best explains the changes in borders shown between maps A and B?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Marxism has been a dominant movement in this region.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Nationalism has been a unifying and dividing force in this region.

choices.label: C
choices.text: National elections have been a determining factor in this region.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Invading neighbors have been a driving force in this region.

questions.explanation: The maps of the Middle East or the Balkans show border changes caused by ethnic nationalism — diverse multiethnic empires fragmenting into nation-states or new states emerging from post-WWI mandates. Nationalism's dual role as a unifying and dividing force explains why some territories gained independence while others experienced civil conflict or partition. This is a frequently tested Enduring Issue; CRQs pair these maps with documents from the Paris Peace Conference or Balfour Declaration.

questions.subject: Global History
questions.stem: Which historical event was a significant factor in bringing about the conditions shown on Map A?

choices.label: A
choices.text: establishment of the League of Nations

choices.label: B
choices.text: the Berlin Conference

choices.label: C
choices.text: formation of the World Trade Organization

choices.label: D
choices.text: the Great Leap Forward

questions.explanation: The 1884–85 Berlin Conference, convened by Bismarck, established the rules for European colonization of Africa and resulted in borders that divided African ethnic groups arbitrarily across European-controlled territories. These artificial colonial boundaries — which ignored pre-existing kingdoms, ethnic ties, and religious communities — created the conditions for civil wars and instability that persisted long after independence. Students should recognize the Berlin Conference as distinct from the Congress of Berlin (1878), which addressed the Balkans.

questions.subject: Global History
questions.stem: Which event most directly influenced the writing of this document?

choices.label: A
choices.text: the Holocaust

choices.label: B
choices.text: the Cold War

choices.label: C
choices.text: the Iranian Revolution

choices.label: D
choices.text: the Great Depression

questions.explanation: The document is the 1948 Universal Declaration of Human Rights, adopted by the UN General Assembly on December 10, 1948, in direct response to the Holocaust — the systematic genocide of approximately 6 million Jews and millions of others by Nazi Germany from 1941–1945. The horrors exposed at Nuremberg (1945–46) created international consensus that human rights needed codified global protection. Regents CRQs pair the UDHR with the Nuremberg Trials or the UN Charter as turning-point documents.

questions.subject: Global History
questions.stem: Based on these maps, which statement best describes Europe following World War I?

choices.label: A
choices.text: After World War I, the Austro-Hungarian Empire expanded.

choices.label: B
choices.text: World War I had little impact on Eastern Europe.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Russia retained all of its pre-war territory.

choices.label: D
choices.text: New countries were created out of old empires.

questions.explanation: The post-WWI settlement (1918–1919), guided by Woodrow Wilson's principle of self-determination and codified in the Treaties of Versailles, Saint-Germain, Trianon, and Sèvres, dismantled the German, Austro-Hungarian, Ottoman, and Russian empires and created new nations including Poland, Czechoslovakia, Yugoslavia, and the Baltic states. This represented the Enduring Issue of nationalism as a political organizing principle and a major turning point in European political geography. Many of these newly created borders sowed seeds of future conflict.

questions.subject: Global History
questions.stem: One reason for the situation shown in Map A was Austria’s

choices.label: A
choices.text: defeat by Germany in both World Wars

choices.label: B
choices.text: diverse ethnic composition

choices.label: C
choices.text: location along key trade routes

choices.label: D
choices.text: loss of power after fighting the Ottoman Empire

questions.explanation: Austria-Hungary was a multinational empire containing Germans, Hungarians, Czechs, Slovaks, Poles, Croats, Slovenes, Serbs, Romanians, and Italians, among others. This diverse ethnic composition created constant centrifugal pressure as nationalist movements within each group demanded self-rule. By 1914, pan-Slavic nationalism — backed by Russia — was directly threatening Austro-Hungarian territorial integrity, making Serbian nationalism the spark that ignited WWI. The empire's collapse in 1918 is a direct consequence of this ethnic diversity.

questions.subject: Global History
questions.stem: Which turning point is most closely associated with the geographic context described in this passage?

choices.label: A
choices.text: the Russian Revolution

choices.label: B
choices.text: World War I

choices.label: C
choices.text: the Industrial Revolution

choices.label: D
choices.text: the Great Depression

questions.explanation: The geographic context described — coal and iron deposits, river networks enabling transport, and urban factory centers — matches the conditions that made Britain the birthplace of the Industrial Revolution from roughly 1760–1840. Britain's combination of natural resources, colonial markets, capital investment, and technological innovation (steam engine, spinning jenny) transformed production methods globally. Students often need to distinguish Industrial Revolution geography (Britain, then Europe/US) from the later Second Industrial Revolution.

questions.subject: Global History
questions.stem: A historian would find this article most useful for

choices.label: A
choices.text: researching the geography of Turkey

choices.label: B
choices.text: identifying the cause of the incident

choices.label: C
choices.text: supporting the claim that a genocide took place

choices.label: D
choices.text: determining the number of people affected by the incident

questions.explanation: The article documenting mass killings, forced displacement, and systematic targeting of an ethnic group provides evidence for historical genocide — specifically useful for establishing intent, scale, and method, which are the legal and historical criteria distinguishing genocide from other atrocities. The Enduring Issues of human rights and genocide are central Regents themes, and students must use historical evidence to support claims rather than assert them without documentation. This type of document appears in CRQ scaffolds requiring claim-evidence reasoning.

questions.subject: Global History
questions.stem: During which historical period did Atatürk make these remarks?

choices.label: A
choices.text: at the time of Israel’s creation

choices.label: B
choices.text: between the World Wars

choices.label: C
choices.text: at the beginning of the Iranian Revolution

choices.label: D
choices.text: during the Persian Gulf War

questions.explanation: Mustafa Kemal Atatürk led Turkey between 1923 and 1938 — the interwar period between WWI and WWII — implementing sweeping secularization reforms including abolishing the Ottoman sultanate and caliphate, adopting a Latin alphabet, granting women suffrage (1934), and separating religion from government. This modernization program mirrored Japan's Meiji-era Westernization and reflects the Enduring Issue of cultural change imposed by a strong central leader. Regents questions frequently connect Atatürk to the decline of the Ottoman Empire.

questions.subject: Global History
questions.stem: What was a cause of the event shown in this cartoon?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Japan’s loss in the Sino-Japanese War

choices.label: B
choices.text: Japan’s defeat in World War II

choices.label: C
choices.text: the attempted invasion of Japan by Russia

choices.label: D
choices.text: the success of Japan’s Meiji Restoration

questions.explanation: The cartoon depicts Japan's military expansion following the success of the Meiji Restoration (1868–1912), which modernized Japan's military and economy so effectively that Japan defeated China in 1895 and Russia in 1905 — proving an Asian nation could compete with Western imperial powers. Japan's victory demonstrated the transformative power of industrialization and nationalism, and emboldened further imperial expansion into Korea, Manchuria, and China. Students should link Meiji success directly to Japan's emergence as an imperial power.

questions.subject: Global History
questions.stem: Which political leader other than Mao Zedong utilized this type of poster?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Joseph Stalin

choices.label: B
choices.text: Nelson Mandela

choices.label: C
choices.text: Otto Von Bismarck

choices.label: D
choices.text: Emperor Meiji

questions.explanation: Both Mao Zedong (China) and Joseph Stalin (USSR) used propaganda posters featuring heroic workers, idealized leaders, and socialist imagery to mobilize populations, reinforce loyalty, and promote communist ideology. Stalin's socialist realism aesthetic in Soviet posters from the 1930s–1950s closely paralleled Maoist poster campaigns in China. Regents CRQs on totalitarianism often use these posters to illustrate how authoritarian regimes manipulate mass culture; students should not confuse propaganda with democratic political advertising.

questions.subject: Global History
questions.stem: Which statement is best supported by the information shown on this map?

choices.label: A
choices.text: United States clothing imports come primarily from Asia.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Many African nations manufacture large quantities of clothing for export.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Many Asian nations lack the technology necessary for industrialization.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Central America imports the majority of its clothing from India.

questions.explanation: The map showing US clothing imports overwhelmingly sourced from Asian countries (China, Bangladesh, Vietnam, India) reflects globalization and the shift of manufacturing to lower-wage economies in the late 20th and 21st centuries. This pattern is driven by comparative economic advantage, lower labor costs, and free-trade agreements. The Enduring Issue here is economic interdependence and its social consequences — job displacement in the US, labor conditions in Asia, and environmental impact of long supply chains.

questions.subject: Global History
questions.stem: This passage best describes which development in Japanese history?

choices.label: A
choices.text: the Meiji Restoration

choices.label: B
choices.text: the rule of the Tokugawa Shogunate

choices.label: C
choices.text: the annexation of Korea

choices.label: D
choices.text: the arrival of Commodore Matthew Perry

questions.explanation: The Tokugawa Shogunate (1603–1868) governed Japan through a strict feudal hierarchy with the shogun as military ruler, implemented the sakoku (closed country) policy isolating Japan from foreign trade and Christianity, and froze social mobility through rigid caste structures. This era of deliberate isolation ended when Commodore Matthew Perry's 'Black Ships' forced Japan open in 1853–54, directly triggering the Meiji Restoration. Students confuse the shogunate (military government) with the emperor, who was ceremonially present but politically powerless.

questions.subject: Global History
questions.stem: The situation shown on this map played a direct role in the

choices.label: A
choices.text: series of Arab-Israeli wars

choices.label: B
choices.text: admission of Turkey to the North Atlantic Treaty Organization (NATO)

choices.label: C
choices.text: invasion of Afghanistan

choices.label: D
choices.text: building of the Suez Canal

questions.explanation: The 1948 UN partition plan created the State of Israel in former British Mandate Palestine, displacing approximately 700,000 Palestinian Arabs in what Palestinians call the Nakba ('catastrophe'). Arab states rejected the partition, and five Arab nations immediately attacked Israel upon its declaration of independence, beginning the first of the Arab-Israeli Wars. The Enduring Issue is competing nationalisms — Zionist and Palestinian — both claiming the same territory, a conflict the Regents tests through maps and primary source documents.

questions.subject: Global History
questions.stem: The shift in membership after 2000 can best be explained as a response to the

choices.label: A
choices.text: collapse of the Soviet Union

choices.label: B
choices.text: independence movements in Africa

choices.label: C
choices.text: formation of the United Nations

choices.label: D
choices.text: construction of the Berlin Wall

questions.explanation: After the Soviet Union's collapse in 1991, formerly communist Eastern European nations — Poland, Hungary, Czech Republic, the Baltic states — pivoted toward Western democratic and economic institutions, seeking protection against potential Russian resurgence. NATO expanded from 16 members in 1991 to over 30 by the early 2000s to accommodate these new democracies. This shift illustrates the Enduring Issue of political power reorganization after a major turning point; students should recognize NATO expansion as a direct consequence of Cold War's end.

questions.subject: Global History
questions.stem: Which type of social scientist is most likely to use the information shown on this map?

choices.label: A
choices.text: historian

choices.label: B
choices.text: anthropologist

choices.label: C
choices.text: sociologist

choices.label: D
choices.text: economist

questions.explanation: The map displaying data about trade flows, GDP, per capita income, or economic output patterns is primarily useful to an economist — a social scientist who studies production, distribution, and consumption of goods and services. Geographers analyze spatial patterns, historians analyze change over time, and political scientists analyze power structures; economists focus specifically on economic systems and market behavior. Regents questions testing social science methodology require students to match data types with appropriate disciplinary frameworks.

questions.subject: Global History
questions.stem: What was a major result of Germany’s decision to use the Schlieffen Plan in 1914?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Serbia and Austria-Hungary attacked Germany and France.

choices.label: B
choices.text: French and Germans engaged in ongoing fighting across trenches.

choices.label: C
choices.text: British submarines came to the aid of Germans to defeat France.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Russia applied diplomatic pressure to achieve a peace settlement between Germany and France.

questions.explanation: Germany's Schlieffen Plan (developed 1905, executed 1914) called for a rapid two-front war — a fast knock-out blow against France through Belgium, then turning to defeat Russia — but failed when Belgian resistance and the Battle of the Marne stalled the German advance in September 1914. The failure transformed WWI into a devastating war of attrition fought from static trenches stretching 400 miles across France and Belgium, with millions of casualties at Verdun, the Somme, and Ypres. The Enduring Issue is unintended consequences of military strategy.

questions.subject: Global History
questions.stem: The division of the mandates into these states caused future conflicts because

choices.label: A
choices.text: none of the territories were turned over to German control as they had been promised

choices.label: B
choices.text: the Ottoman Empire thought it could easily conquer the divided territories

choices.label: C
choices.text: Britain received a larger territorial mandate than France did

choices.label: D
choices.text: the boundaries failed to fully recognize competing religious and ethnic interests

questions.explanation: After WWI, the League of Nations awarded Britain and France 'mandates' to administer former Ottoman Arab territories, drawing borders in the Middle East (Iraq, Syria, Lebanon, Palestine, Transjordan) that combined rival Sunni, Shia, and Kurdish populations or separated related ethnic groups. These artificial boundaries, which prioritized European strategic interests over local ethnic and religious realities, have fueled conflicts including the Arab-Israeli conflict, Lebanese civil war, Iraqi sectarian violence, and Kurdish separatist movements. Regents CRQs frequently use Middle East mandate maps to test students on imperialism's long-term consequences.

questions.subject: Global History
questions.stem: According to Thomas Friedman, why is it surprising that Mexico is being replaced by China as the number two exporter to the United States?

choices.label: A
choices.text: China’s industry lacks low-cost manufacturing ability.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Mexico is geographically close to the United States.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Mexico has the world’s strongest economy.

choices.label: D
choices.text: China’s relationship with the United States has been damaged.

questions.explanation: Thomas Friedman's argument highlights that despite Mexico's geographic advantage — sharing a 2,000-mile border with the world's largest consumer market — Chinese manufacturing has displaced Mexican exports to the US. This is surprising because proximity should minimize shipping costs and time, normally a decisive economic advantage. The Enduring Issue is globalization and economic competition, where labor cost differentials can override geographic advantages. Friedman uses this example to illustrate how interconnected the global economy became in the early 21st century.

questions.subject: Global History
questions.stem: A historian would find this passage most useful for

choices.label: A
choices.text: analyzing the ethnic makeup of Simón Bolívar’s forces

choices.label: B
choices.text: determining the economic variables that affected Simón Bolívar’s troops

choices.label: C
choices.text: evaluating the weapons used in the struggle for independence in South America

choices.label: D
choices.text: understanding the impact of geographic factors on the fight for Colombia’s independence

questions.explanation: The passage describes how geographic features — mountain ranges, dense jungles, monsoon seasons, or river systems — created tactical obstacles that shaped the nature and duration of a military conflict. Geographic factors as a historical force is a core Enduring Issue framework on the Regents; examples include the Himalayas protecting India, the Alps shaping Napoleon's campaigns, or Vietnam's jungle terrain complicating US counterinsurgency. Historians use geographic analysis to explain why certain military strategies succeeded or failed.

questions.subject: Global History
questions.stem: What is a valid conclusion based on the information shown on this map?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Most European trading stations and empires were located along the coast.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Russia had the largest number of trading stations in Asia.

choices.label: C
choices.text: France controlled more ports in India than Britain did.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Each European power represented had possessions in the East Indies.

questions.explanation: The map showing European trading posts concentrated along African and Asian coastlines reflects the 15th–17th century pattern of maritime imperialism, when Portuguese, Dutch, British, and French merchants established coastal footholds for trade in spices, textiles, and enslaved people without penetrating interiors. This coastal concentration was due to disease barriers (malaria), African political resistance, and the practical limitations of early modern navigation. The Enduring Issue is geographic access shaping the scope and form of imperial contact.

questions.subject: Global History
questions.stem: Which event played a major role in the expansion shown on this map?

choices.label: A
choices.text: World War I

choices.label: B
choices.text: French Revolution

choices.label: C
choices.text: Industrial Revolution

choices.label: D
choices.text: Russo-Japanese War

questions.explanation: The Industrial Revolution (1760–1840 in Britain, then spreading globally) generated surplus capital, advanced weapons technology, and the need for raw materials and overseas markets that drove the late 19th-century expansion of European empires — especially in Africa and Asia. Steam-powered ships and railways, quinine for malaria prevention, and repeating firearms gave European powers decisive military-technological advantages over colonized peoples. Students should trace the direct causal link between industrialization and the accelerated imperialism shown on the expansion map.

questions.subject: Global History
questions.stem: Which event influenced the writing of this poem?

choices.label: A
choices.text: the Russian Revolution

choices.label: B
choices.text: World War II

choices.label: C
choices.text: the Korean War

choices.label: D
choices.text: World War I

questions.explanation: The poem reflects the disillusionment, mass casualties, and futility of WWI (1914–1918), a conflict that killed approximately 17 million people through industrialized warfare — poison gas, machine guns, artillery barrages, and trench warfare. WWI shattered European optimism about progress and civilization, producing a 'Lost Generation' of writers and artists (Wilfred Owen, Siegfried Sassoon, Erich Maria Remarque) who documented its horror. Regents CRQs contrast pre-war patriotic rhetoric with wartime disillusionment poetry.

questions.subject: Global History
questions.stem: Which major political event was partially caused by the Soviet war in Afghanistan?

choices.label: A
choices.text: building of the Berlin Wall

choices.label: B
choices.text: collapse of the Soviet Union

choices.label: C
choices.text: placement of nuclear missiles in Cuba

choices.label: D
choices.text: Soviet occupation of Hungary

questions.explanation: The Soviet Union's costly and unwinnable war in Afghanistan (1979–1989), often called 'the Soviet Vietnam,' drained the USSR economically, demoralized the military, and exposed the system's brittleness to domestic audiences. Combined with economic stagnation, Gorbachev's reforms, and rising nationalist movements in Soviet republics, the Afghan failure accelerated the political crisis that led to the Soviet Union's dissolution in December 1991. Students sometimes attribute the collapse solely to Gorbachev's reforms, overlooking Afghanistan's role.

questions.subject: Global History
questions.stem: Which event brought about similar agricultural changes to those described in this passage?

choices.label: A
choices.text: desertification of the Sahara/Sahel

choices.label: B
choices.text: Ukrainian Holodomor

choices.label: C
choices.text: construction of the Aswan Dam

choices.label: D
choices.text: Green Revolution

questions.explanation: The Green Revolution (1940s–1970s) introduced high-yield wheat and rice varieties, chemical fertilizers, pesticides, and irrigation systems to developing countries — especially India, Mexico, and the Philippines — dramatically increasing food production and averting predicted famines. Norman Borlaug's wheat varieties doubled and tripled yields, transforming subsistence agricultural economies. The Enduring Issue is how scientific and technological innovation (like the earlier Agricultural Revolution) reshapes societies and demographic patterns.

questions.subject: Global History
questions.stem: Which historical event most directly influenced the development of the 1947 plan shown on Map A?

choices.label: A
choices.text: the fall of the Austro-Hungarian Empire

choices.label: B
choices.text: Russian pogroms

choices.label: C
choices.text: Paris Peace Conference

choices.label: D
choices.text: the Holocaust

questions.explanation: The Holocaust (1941–1945), in which Nazi Germany systematically murdered approximately 6 million Jews and 5–6 million others in a state-organized genocide, generated overwhelming international support for establishing a Jewish homeland as both moral restitution and security. The 1947 UN Partition Plan for Palestine (Resolution 181), which proposed dividing British Mandate Palestine into Jewish and Arab states, was directly shaped by post-Holocaust political consensus in the United Nations. Students must connect Holocaust documentation to post-WWII geopolitical decisions.

questions.subject: Global History
questions.stem: The events shown on this map and in this cartoon were related to the outbreak of which conflict?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Cold War

choices.label: B
choices.text: Napoleonic Wars

choices.label: C
choices.text: World War II

choices.label: D
choices.text: Russo-Japanese War

questions.explanation: The map and cartoon together illustrate German and Japanese territorial expansion in the late 1930s — Germany's annexation of Austria (1938), the Sudetenland (1938), and Czechoslovakia (1939), combined with Japan's invasion of China from 1937 — the aggressive actions of the Axis powers that triggered WWII. The policy of appeasement by Britain and France, culminating at Munich (1938), failed to stop this expansion. The Enduring Issue is how unchecked aggression and failed diplomacy lead to large-scale conflict.

questions.subject: Global History
questions.stem: The overall change desired by Atatürk is similar to the change enacted in

choices.label: A
choices.text: India under nonalignment

choices.label: B
choices.text: Japan under the Meiji

choices.label: C
choices.text: South Africa during the period of apartheid

choices.label: D
choices.text: the Soviet Union during the period of détente

questions.explanation: Both Atatürk's Turkish modernization (1923–1938) and Japan's Meiji Restoration (1868–1912) involved top-down, state-directed modernization: adopting Western technology, legal codes, military organization, and educational systems while deliberately discarding traditional political and religious structures. Both aimed to achieve national sovereignty and equality with Western powers through rapid industrialization and institutional reform. Regents CRQs frequently ask students to compare these two modernization movements as parallel turning points.

questions.subject: Global History
questions.stem: This cartoon can be used to demonstrate the

choices.label: A
choices.text: threat posed by Japan to other territories of Asia

choices.label: B
choices.text: geographic barriers that protected China from foreign influence

choices.label: C
choices.text: military technologies Japan possessed

choices.label: D
choices.text: alliances that were formed in East Asia

questions.explanation: The cartoon shows Japan as an aggressive predator — likely an octopus or tiger — threatening neighboring Asian territories in the 1930s–1940s, illustrating Imperial Japan's expansion into Manchuria (1931), China (1937), Southeast Asia (1940–41), and the Pacific. Japan's Greater East Asia Co-Prosperity Sphere ideology justified conquest as 'Asian liberation from Western colonialism,' but in practice imposed brutal occupation. The Enduring Issue is imperial aggression and propaganda; students should recognize Japan's expansion as a parallel to Nazi Germany's.
questions.sourceIssue: Stimulus image was reused by a different released Regents source; hidden until re-imported from the original PDF.

questions.subject: Global History
questions.stem: Which claim about the French Revolution is best supported by the information included in these headlines?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The revolutionary spirit grew out of frustration with the Old Regime.

choices.label: B
choices.text: King Louis XVI was killed because of his belief in consent of the governed.

choices.label: C
choices.text: The revolution was unexpected as the Estates General met regularly.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Nobility and clergy were the only victims of the Terror.

questions.explanation: The French Revolution's origins lie in the frustrations of the Third Estate — the 97% of France who bore crushing tax burdens while the privileged nobility and clergy (First and Second Estates) were largely exempt under the Old Regime. Enlightenment ideas about natural rights, combined with financial crisis from France's involvement in the American Revolution, pushed the Third Estate to form the National Assembly in June 1789. Regents CRQs often pair the Declaration of the Rights of Man with the Estates-General breakdown to test cause-and-effect reasoning.
questions.sourceIssue: Stimulus image was reused by a different released Regents source; hidden until re-imported from the original PDF.

questions.subject: Global History
questions.stem: Which event led most directly to the situation described in this document?

choices.label: A
choices.text: the Truman Doctrine

choices.label: B
choices.text: the Berlin Conference

choices.label: C
choices.text: the French Revolution

choices.label: D
choices.text: the Treaty of Versailles

questions.explanation: The 1884–85 Berlin Conference, initiated by Chancellor Bismarck, established rules for European partition of Africa — particularly the principle of 'effective occupation' — and accelerated the Scramble for Africa, resulting in colonial systems that extracted resources, exploited labor, and dismantled existing political structures. The document likely describes an African leader or community responding to colonial imposition. The Enduring Issue is the human cost of imperialism on colonized peoples, a frequent anchor for Regents CRQ document sets.

questions.subject: Global History
questions.stem: Which policy is most closely associated with the event featured on this map?

choices.label: A
choices.text: détente

choices.label: B
choices.text: containment

choices.label: C
choices.text: nonalignment

choices.label: D
choices.text: perestroika

questions.explanation: The map depicting the division of Korea (or Germany, or Vietnam) into communist and non-communist zones illustrates the policy of containment — the US strategy articulated by diplomat George Kennan and formalized in the Truman Doctrine (1947) to prevent the further spread of Soviet-backed communism. Containment drove US involvement in the Korean War (1950–53), the Vietnam War (1955–75), and proxy conflicts across Asia, Africa, and Latin America. Students sometimes confuse containment (stopping spread) with rollback (reversing communism).

questions.subject: Global History
questions.stem: What best explains the differences in national boundaries shown on the two maps?

choices.label: A
choices.text: new patterns of urbanization

choices.label: B
choices.text: the spread of nationalist movements

choices.label: C
choices.text: opposition to modernization

choices.label: D
choices.text: the end of the Cold War

questions.explanation: The maps show Europe before and after WWI (or a similar colonial/post-colonial comparison), with the spread of nationalist movements driving border changes as ethnic minorities within large empires — Poles, Czechs, South Slavs, Finns — successfully demanded independent states. Woodrow Wilson's Fourteen Points (1918) endorsed the principle of national self-determination, legitimizing these movements in the post-war settlement. The Enduring Issue is nationalism as the primary force reshaping political geography in the 20th century.

questions.subject: Global History
questions.stem: Which statement best describes the cartoonist’s purpose in creating this cartoon?

choices.label: A
choices.text: to draw attention to United Nations peacekeeping efforts

choices.label: B
choices.text: to highlight the dangers of mutual assured destruction

choices.label: C
choices.text: to build support for the Soviet Union’s desire to spread communism

choices.label: D
choices.text: to assess the ways proxy wars were fought

questions.explanation: The cartoon depicts the doctrine of Mutually Assured Destruction (MAD), the Cold War deterrence logic in which both the US and USSR maintained nuclear arsenals large enough to survive a first strike and still annihilate the attacker, making a nuclear exchange suicidal for both sides. MAD stabilized the superpower relationship by making direct war irrational, but it also meant humanity lived under constant existential threat, a tension the Regents frames as both an achievement of deterrence and a humanitarian crisis. CRQs often connect MAD to the Cuban Missile Crisis (1962) as its most dramatic near-failure.

questions.subject: Global History
questions.stem: Which historical document most influenced the government of Venezuela as described in this passage?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Mary Wollstonecraft’s “A Vindication of the Rights of Woman”

choices.label: B
choices.text: The Napoleonic Code

choices.label: C
choices.text: Adam Smith’s The Wealth of Nations

choices.label: D
choices.text: Locke’s Two Treatises of Government

questions.explanation: John Locke's Two Treatises of Government (1689) argued that governments derive their just authority from the consent of the governed and that individuals possess natural rights to life, liberty, and property — rights no government may lawfully strip. Venezuelan independence leader Simón Bolívar explicitly drew on Locke and Montesquieu when framing republican constitutions for liberated South American states in the 1810s–1820s. Regents CRQs frequently test the transmission of Enlightenment ideas from European theory to American revolutionary practice.

questions.subject: Global History
questions.stem: Which concept is depicted in this cartoon?

choices.label: A
choices.text: pacifism

choices.label: B
choices.text: imperialism

choices.label: C
choices.text: isolationism

choices.label: D
choices.text: socialism

questions.explanation: The cartoon shows a large powerful nation — typically depicted as a European country or the United States — dominating a smaller, weaker one, visually illustrating imperialism: the domination of one country's political, economic, and cultural life by a stronger foreign power. Imperialism is a recurring Enduring Issue on the Regents, and students should distinguish economic imperialism (controlling trade, resources) from political imperialism (direct colonial administration). Common wrong choices include nationalism and colonialism, which are related but distinct concepts.

questions.subject: Global History
questions.stem: Which statement is best supported by the evidence included in these documents?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Dictatorships lead to rebellions and nationalist struggles.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Media and propaganda can be used to promote national goals.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Citizens should have freedom to travel and trade with anybody.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Government-supported agricultural programs are necessary.

questions.explanation: The documents likely include wartime propaganda posters, state-controlled newspapers, or radio broadcasts demonstrating how governments — from Nazi Germany to Stalinist USSR to Imperial Japan — used mass media to shape public opinion, justify military campaigns, and suppress dissent. Media manipulation is a key tool of totalitarian and nationalist states, reflecting the Enduring Issue of power and its extension through information control. Regents CRQs ask students to identify the purpose, audience, and bias of such documents.
questions.sourceIssue: Stimulus image was reused by a different released Regents source; hidden until re-imported from the original PDF.

questions.subject: Global History
questions.stem: Based on this cartoon, the views of the International Monetary Fund (IMF) are most closely associated with which economic system?

choices.label: A
choices.text: command

choices.label: B
choices.text: mixed

choices.label: C
choices.text: traditional

choices.label: D
choices.text: capitalism

questions.explanation: The International Monetary Fund (IMF), established at Bretton Woods in 1944, promotes free-market capitalism by requiring borrowing nations to implement austerity measures, privatization, and trade liberalization as conditions for loans — policies critics depict as imposing Western economic ideology on developing nations. The cartoon likely critiques IMF 'structural adjustment' programs applied to African or Latin American economies in the 1980s–90s. The Enduring Issue is economic systems and the global power imbalance between wealthy creditor nations and indebted developing countries.
questions.sourceIssue: Stimulus image was reused by a different released Regents source; hidden until re-imported from the original PDF.

questions.subject: Global History
questions.stem: Which of Deng Xiaoping’s actions contributed most directly to the changes experienced by Li Xiaohua as described in this passage?

choices.label: A
choices.text: restricting the ability of foreign banks to make investments in China

choices.label: B
choices.text: introducing elements of free market capitalism into China’s economy

choices.label: C
choices.text: implementing the one-child policy to limit family size

choices.label: D
choices.text: directing that science education be prioritized in schools throughout China

questions.explanation: Deng Xiaoping's 'Reform and Opening Up' policy (1978–1992) introduced Special Economic Zones, foreign direct investment, private enterprise, and market-pricing mechanisms into China's command economy, producing double-digit annual growth rates and lifting hundreds of millions out of poverty over the following decades. Deng famously declared 'it doesn't matter whether a cat is black or white, as long as it catches mice,' pragmatically accepting capitalist tools while maintaining Communist Party political control. The Regents tests this as an example of selective modernization without political liberalization.

questions.subject: Global History
questions.stem: The situation shown in this cartoon is most closely associated with the

choices.label: A
choices.text: Cold War

choices.label: B
choices.text: Holocaust

choices.label: C
choices.text: rise of fascism

choices.label: D
choices.text: end of World War I

questions.explanation: The cartoon's imagery — typically two superpowers armed and circling each other, or a globe under nuclear shadow — represents the Cold War (1947–1991), the ideological and geopolitical competition between the US-led capitalist West and the USSR-led communist East. The Cold War was characterized by arms races, proxy conflicts, propaganda campaigns, and nuclear brinkmanship rather than direct military confrontation. Students should recognize Cold War symbolism (iron curtain, Berlin Wall, missiles) as distinct from WWI or WWII imagery.

questions.subject: Global History
questions.stem: Which claim is best supported by the evidence in this passage?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Louis XIV supported religious toleration.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Louis XIV’s excessive spending had negative consequences.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Court visits led to the spread of disease in Paris.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Absolutism in France promoted equality among the classes.

questions.explanation: Louis XIV's extravagant court at Versailles (built 1661–1710 at enormous cost), his wars of expansion (War of Spanish Succession, Nine Years' War), and his revocation of the Edict of Nantes (1685) driving out productive Huguenot craftsmen collectively drained France's treasury and undermined the economy. His excessive spending is a textbook example of absolutism's costs — the Enduring Issue of power unchecked by accountability. The Regents often uses Louis XIV to contrast effective and destructive exercises of absolute monarchical authority.

questions.subject: Global History
questions.stem: Based on this passage, which political idea evolved during the Tokugawa shogunate?

choices.label: A
choices.text: absolutism

choices.label: B
choices.text: socialism

choices.label: C
choices.text: oligarchy

choices.label: D
choices.text: democracy

questions.explanation: Under the Tokugawa Shogunate (1603–1868), the shogun exercised absolute, hereditary military rule over Japan with no meaningful checks on executive authority — a form of political absolutism that enforced rigid hierarchy, suppressed Buddhism and Christianity as political threats, and maintained social order through the bushido code and strict estate divisions. This parallel to European absolutism (Louis XIV, Peter the Great) is frequently tested; students should note that the Tokugawa emperor was a figurehead while the shogun held real power.

questions.subject: Global History
questions.stem: Which claim is best supported by the evidence depicted in this cartoon?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Ho Chi Minh wanted to keep North Vietnam separated from South Vietnam.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Efforts by the United States to free South Vietnam from China were successful.

choices.label: C
choices.text: The United States used air strikes to stop the spread of communism into South Vietnam.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Ho Chi Minh invited the United States to use air strikes to end French colonization of Vietnam.

questions.explanation: The cartoon depicts US air strikes — most likely during the Korean War (1950–53) or Vietnam War (1955–75) — carried out under the Cold War policy of containment, the US strategy to prevent further expansion of Soviet-backed communism articulated by George Kennan and enacted through the Truman Doctrine (1947) and Marshall Plan (1948). US military intervention in Asia was framed publicly as defending freedom, but the Regents tests whether students recognize containment as the ideological justification. CRQs pair this with NSC-68 or Kennan's 'Long Telegram.'

questions.subject: Global History
questions.stem: Based on this cartoon, which concept influenced Kemal Atatürk’s development of New Turkey?

choices.label: A
choices.text: zionism

choices.label: B
choices.text: communism

choices.label: C
choices.text: modernization

choices.label: D
choices.text: laissez-faire
