questions.explanation: The cartoon shows Atatürk replacing Ottoman symbols with Western ones — European clothing, the Latin alphabet, secular law codes — reflecting his deliberate modernization campaign to transform Turkey from a religious Ottoman empire into a modern, secular, European-style republic after 1923. Atatürk's modernization parallels the Meiji Restoration in Japan, and both are standard Regents examples of top-down, state-directed Westernization. Students should distinguish modernization (adopting new technology/institutions) from Westernization (specifically adopting Western cultural norms).

questions.subject: Global History
questions.stem: Which conclusion about China can best be drawn from this passage?

choices.label: A
choices.text: It uses a communist economy based on Maoist principles.

choices.label: B
choices.text: It wants the economic benefits of capitalism without free speech and democracy.

choices.label: C
choices.text: It wants to completely isolate itself from contact with the rest of the world.

choices.label: D
choices.text: It rejects capitalism as an economic system but welcomes democratic ideals.

questions.explanation: China under Deng Xiaoping and his successors implemented market-based economic reforms — Special Economic Zones, foreign investment, private enterprise — to generate growth while maintaining one-party Communist Party political control and censoring political dissent. This 'socialist market economy' model suppresses free speech, controls internet access, and violently crushed pro-democracy movements like Tiananmen Square (1989), demonstrating the CCP's goal to separate economic liberalization from political pluralism. The Enduring Issue is the tension between economic freedom and political repression.

questions.subject: Global History
questions.stem: What is the intended purpose of this document?

choices.label: A
choices.text: promoting isolationism

choices.label: B
choices.text: establishment of alliances

choices.label: C
choices.text: containing communism

choices.label: D
choices.text: preventing future atrocities

questions.explanation: The document is the 1948 Universal Declaration of Human Rights (UDHR), adopted by the UN General Assembly on December 10, 1948, with the primary purpose of establishing universal legal standards to prevent the atrocities of the Holocaust and WWII from recurring. Its 30 articles codified rights to life, freedom from torture, freedom of expression, and equality before the law as obligations of all UN member states. Regents CRQs use the UDHR as a turning point document: students must connect it causally to the Holocaust and Nuremberg Trials.

questions.subject: Global History
questions.stem: The design and use of this poster suggests its purpose was to

choices.label: A
choices.text: advertise advancements in Chinese healthcare

choices.label: B
choices.text: build support for China’s leader among the people

choices.label: C
choices.text: improve the literacy rates of children and adults throughout China

choices.label: D
choices.text: warn the Chinese people about the dangers of capitalism

questions.explanation: The Maoist propaganda poster — featuring heroic workers, peasants, and soldiers alongside Mao's idealized portrait beneath red banners and slogans — was designed using socialist realist aesthetics to cultivate a personality cult, mobilize mass support for Communist Party campaigns, and suppress critical thinking about government policies. Mao Zedong's use of propaganda parallels Stalin's in the USSR, both reflecting how totalitarian regimes deploy visual culture as a control mechanism. The Enduring Issue is how governments use information and imagery to manipulate populations.

questions.subject: Global History
questions.stem: What impact did Speakers C and D have on world history?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Their arguments led to the creation of labor unions and changes in working conditions.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Their arguments caused an immediate shift from absolutist rule to democratic rule.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Their ideas helped establish the foundation of the suffrage and anti-slavery movements.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Their ideas eased transitions from mercan- tilism to capitalism.

questions.explanation: The passage likely references early feminist thinkers — Mary Wollstonecraft (A Vindication of the Rights of Woman, 1792) or Olympe de Gouges (Declaration of the Rights of Woman, 1791) — whose arguments that Enlightenment principles of natural rights applied equally to women laid the philosophical foundation for the 19th–20th century suffrage movement. Their ideas directly influenced the Seneca Falls Convention (1848) in the US and European suffragist organizations. Regents CRQs trace the lineage from Enlightenment gender philosophy to women's political rights.

questions.subject: Global History
questions.stem: Which historical writing most influenced the circumstances described in this document?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Adam Smith’s Wealth of Nations

choices.label: B
choices.text: Olympe de Gouges’ Declaration of the Rights of Woman

choices.label: C
choices.text: Karl Marx’s Communist Manifesto

choices.label: D
choices.text: John Locke’s Two Treatises of Government

questions.explanation: John Locke's Two Treatises (1689) established the social contract theory: governments exist to protect natural rights (life, liberty, property), and citizens may revolt against governments that violate those rights. This directly influenced the American Declaration of Independence (1776), the French Declaration of the Rights of Man (1789), and Simón Bolívar's revolutionary manifestos. The Regents consistently tests the transmission of Enlightenment ideas from Europe to Atlantic revolutions as a cause-and-effect chain.

questions.subject: Global History
questions.stem: This photo demonstrates a clash between

choices.label: A
choices.text: ethnic diversity and cultural homogenization

choices.label: B
choices.text: economic inequality and socialism

choices.label: C
choices.text: localized trade networks and globalization

choices.label: D
choices.text: traditional cultural values and secularization

questions.explanation: The photo likely depicts a tension between traditional religious dress (e.g., the hijab in Turkey or Iran) and modern secular clothing — reflecting the clash between traditional religious values and secularization, a defining tension in 20th-century Islamic societies. This tension became acute in Turkey under Atatürk's secular reforms, in Iran after the 1979 revolution's reimposition of religious dress codes, and in ongoing debates about personal freedoms versus religious law. The Enduring Issue is cultural change and the politics of identity and religion.

questions.subject: Global History
questions.stem: Which issue is the focus of this cartoon?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Soviet support for anticolonial nations in Africa and Southeast Asia

choices.label: B
choices.text: the spread of communism to parts of Europe and the Middle East

choices.label: C
choices.text: development of nuclear weapons by South Asian and East Asian countries

choices.label: D
choices.text: proxy wars being fought in South America and the Caribbean

questions.explanation: The cartoon likely depicts the Truman Doctrine (1947) or a related Cold War crisis showing communist movements threatening Greece, Turkey, Iran, or Western Europe, reflecting US fears of a Soviet-backed 'domino effect.' The Truman Doctrine committed the US to supporting free peoples resisting communist subjugation and marked the formal beginning of containment policy. Students should distinguish the Truman Doctrine (military/financial aid) from the Marshall Plan (economic reconstruction of Western Europe).

questions.subject: Global History
questions.stem: Which historical development in China is being described in this passage?

choices.label: A
choices.text: the implementation of Cultural Revolution policies

choices.label: B
choices.text: the creation of the People’s Republic of China and Taiwan

choices.label: C
choices.text: reactions to the signing of the Treaty of Nanjing

choices.label: D
choices.text: dislike for the Four Modernizations policy

questions.explanation: Mao Zedong's Cultural Revolution (1966–1976) mobilized teenage Red Guards to purge 'counter-revolutionary' elements — intellectuals, teachers, artists, party officials — destroying cultural heritage, closing universities, and sending millions to 'reeducation' labor camps. An estimated 500,000 to 2 million people were killed and millions more persecuted. The passage describes the revolutionary chaos, book burnings, or public humiliations characteristic of this period. The Enduring Issue is how totalitarian leaders use cultural revolution as a tool of political terror and mass control.

questions.subject: Global History
questions.stem: Based on these documents, Secretary McNamara believed U.S. involvement in this region would prevent

choices.label: A
choices.text: civil wars in recently liberated nations

choices.label: B
choices.text: genocides and human rights abuses

choices.label: C
choices.text: the spread of communism

choices.label: D
choices.text: rebellions against colonial rule

questions.explanation: Secretary of Defense Robert McNamara's documents — likely including the Pentagon Papers (declassified 1971) — reveal that US officials believed intervention in Vietnam was necessary to prevent communist North Vietnam from controlling the South and triggering a wider domino effect across Southeast Asia. This reflects containment doctrine applied to Vietnam. The Pentagon Papers specifically showed McNamara privately doubted the war was winnable, creating a credibility gap. Regents CRQs use McNamara's writings to test students' understanding of Cold War decision-making and propaganda versus reality.

questions.subject: Global History
questions.stem: What inference about Atatürk is best supported by this passage?

choices.label: A
choices.text: He based his ideas on key concepts of Sharia law.

choices.label: B
choices.text: He was a strict believer of Adam Smith’s ideas.

choices.label: C
choices.text: He opposed secularization.

choices.label: D
choices.text: He supported westernization.

questions.explanation: Atatürk's passage promotes secularism, Westernization, and modernization — replacing Arabic script with the Latin alphabet, adopting European legal codes, abolishing the caliphate, and emancipating women. These actions confirm his support for Western-style modernization as the path to building a strong, independent Turkey able to resist European imperialism. Students should note that Atatürk's modernization was authoritarian and top-down: he imposed changes rather than building democratic consensus.

questions.subject: Global History
questions.stem: Which concept is most closely associated with the ideas expressed in this passage?

choices.label: A
choices.text: theocracy

choices.label: B
choices.text: socialism

choices.label: C
choices.text: nonalignment

choices.label: D
choices.text: westernization

questions.explanation: Westernization — the adoption of Western European or American cultural, institutional, technological, and political models — is the concept most closely associated with deliberate reforms like Atatürk's Turkey, Meiji Japan, or Peter the Great's Russia. These leaders sought to modernize by importing Western legal systems, educational structures, military organization, and dress codes. Students should distinguish westernization from modernization (broader technological development) and secularization (specifically removing religious authority from government).

questions.subject: Global History
questions.stem: The ideals contained in this document are most closely associated with the ideals developed by

choices.label: A
choices.text: Enlightenment thinkers

choices.label: B
choices.text: industrialists

choices.label: C
choices.text: supporters of imperialism

choices.label: D
choices.text: dictators

questions.explanation: The document — likely the French Declaration of the Rights of Man and Citizen (1789) or the American Declaration of Independence (1776) — reflects Enlightenment ideals of natural rights, social contract, popular sovereignty, and rational governance articulated by Locke, Montesquieu, Voltaire, and Rousseau. These 18th-century philosophers argued that governments derive authority from the people and that reason, not tradition or divine right, should guide political organization. Regents CRQs frequently trace Enlightenment ideas to Atlantic revolutions.

questions.subject: Global History
questions.stem: Which claim is best supported by this cartoon?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Competition for natural resources led to a rejection of dictatorships.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Increasing nationalism fueled imperialism throughout the first half of the 20th century.

choices.label: C
choices.text: The removal of imperial control led to a rise in militarism in many nations.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Nationalism led to an increase in independence movements throughout the world.

questions.explanation: The cartoon depicts how rising nationalism — the belief in national superiority and the drive for national glory — fueled competition for colonies, markets, and territory among European powers in the late 19th and early 20th centuries, directly contributing to imperialism and ultimately WWI. This MAIN framework (Militarism, Alliances, Imperialism, Nationalism) is the standard Regents analytical lens for WWI causes. Students must distinguish nationalism as a colonial motivator (pride, prestige) from nationalism as a liberation force in colonized territories.

questions.subject: Global History
questions.stem: The wall referred to in this excerpt was used to

choices.label: A
choices.text: promote imperialism

choices.label: B
choices.text: prevent military cooperation

choices.label: C
choices.text: limit the movement of people

choices.label: D
choices.text: encourage the spread of capitalism

questions.explanation: The Berlin Wall (1961–1989) was constructed by East Germany to stop the mass emigration of skilled workers and professionals fleeing communist East Germany for the prosperous West. It physically divided Berlin for 28 years, becoming the most powerful symbol of the Iron Curtain. Its fall on November 9, 1989 — prompted by an East German official's accidental announcement that crossings were open — symbolized the collapse of communist control in Eastern Europe. Students confuse building the Wall (1961, to limit movement) with its fall (1989, an act of liberation).

questions.subject: Global History
questions.stem: Simón Bolívar’s actions were most likely influenced by the ideas of

choices.label: A
choices.text: church of cials

choices.label: B
choices.text: Marxist followers

choices.label: C
choices.text: Enlightenment thinkers

choices.label: D
choices.text: laissez-faire economists

questions.explanation: Simón Bolívar (1783–1830), who led independence movements across Venezuela, Colombia, Ecuador, Peru, and Bolivia, was directly inspired by Enlightenment thinkers — particularly Locke's natural rights, Montesquieu's separation of powers, and Rousseau's social contract — as well as by the practical examples of the American and French revolutions. Bolívar read Enlightenment texts extensively and explicitly invoked them in his revolutionary writings. Regents CRQs test the direct intellectual lineage from European Enlightenment to Latin American independence.

questions.subject: Global History
questions.stem: Which action did Marx and Engels predict would change the conditions described in this passage?

choices.label: A
choices.text: use of passive resistance

choices.label: B
choices.text: adoption of laissez-faire policies

choices.label: C
choices.text: implementation of government reforms

choices.label: D
choices.text: revolts by the working class

questions.explanation: Karl Marx and Friedrich Engels, in The Communist Manifesto (1848) and Das Kapital (1867), argued that industrial capitalism created an exploited proletariat (working class) that, through growing class consciousness, would inevitably revolt against the bourgeoisie (capitalist class) to establish a communist society. They predicted that workers' revolutions — not gradual reform — were the only path to ending the exploitation of industrial capitalism. Students often confuse the prediction (revolution) with the prescription (socialism/communism as a system).

questions.subject: Global History
questions.stem: Which statement identifies a long-term effect of the conflict described in this passage?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The Qing Dynasty was cut off from further trade with European countries.

choices.label: B
choices.text: The Congress of Vienna led to the redrawing of political boundary lines in Europe.

choices.label: C
choices.text: A series of unequal treaties granted Britain spheres of influence in China.

choices.label: D
choices.text: The British implemented a policy of mercantilism in their American colonies.

questions.explanation: The Opium Wars (First, 1839–42; Second, 1856–60) ended with China's military defeat and the imposition of 'unequal treaties' — the Treaty of Nanjing (1842) and Treaty of Tianjin (1858) — that granted Britain and other Western powers extraterritoriality, treaty ports, most-favored-nation trading status, and control of Hong Kong. These treaties represented a catastrophic loss of Chinese sovereignty and are the Enduring Issue of imperialism's long-term effect on China. Students should link Opium War defeat to the later Boxer Rebellion and the 1911 revolution.

questions.subject: Global History
questions.stem: Whose ideas most influenced the creation of these documents?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Maximilien Robespierre

choices.label: B
choices.text: Adam Smith

choices.label: C
choices.text: Catherine the Great

choices.label: D
choices.text: John Locke

questions.explanation: John Locke's political philosophy — natural rights to life, liberty, and property, government by consent of the governed, and the right to revolution against tyranny — is the direct intellectual foundation for the American Declaration of Independence (1776) and the French Declaration of the Rights of Man (1789), the documents most likely referenced. Thomas Jefferson explicitly adapted Locke's language in the Declaration. Regents CRQs routinely ask students to identify Locke as the primary Enlightenment influence on revolutionary-era political documents.

questions.subject: Global History
questions.stem: What was one effect of the historical development shown in this illustration?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The king became an Enlightened despot and a totalitarian government was created.

choices.label: B
choices.text: A directory was formed and the king maintained his power.

choices.label: C
choices.text: The king was overthrown and communism was introduced.

choices.label: D
choices.text: A republic was established and the king was beheaded.

questions.explanation: The French Revolution's most dramatic event was the Reign of Terror (1793–94), during which the radical Jacobin faction under Robespierre executed King Louis XVI by guillotine in January 1793, abolished the monarchy, and established a republic — but also executed roughly 17,000 people in political purges. The revolution's transformation from constitutional monarchy to republic to terror to Napoleon's dictatorship is a frequently tested turning point sequence on the Regents. Students should recognize that the king's execution was a direct consequence of revolutionary radicalization.
questions.sourceIssue: Stimulus image was reused by a different released Regents source; hidden until re-imported from the original PDF.

questions.subject: Global History
questions.stem: What is a common characteristic of the political systems represented in these headlines?

choices.label: A
choices.text: humanitarianism

choices.label: B
choices.text: capitalism

choices.label: C
choices.text: totalitarianism

choices.label: D
choices.text: multinationalism

questions.explanation: The headlines from Nazi Germany, Fascist Italy, Stalinist USSR, or Maoist China all share the feature of totalitarianism — a political system in which a single party and leader control all aspects of public and private life, suppress opposition, use propaganda and terror, and reject democratic institutions. Totalitarianism distinguishes itself from ordinary authoritarianism by the scope of state control. Students often confuse totalitarianism with dictatorship; all totalitarian states are dictatorships but not all dictatorships are fully totalitarian.
questions.sourceIssue: Stimulus image was reused by a different released Regents source; hidden until re-imported from the original PDF.

questions.subject: Global History
questions.stem: Which claim is best supported by the evidence in this speech?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Under French control, the Vietnamese economy had grown.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Ho Chi Minh gained Western support for a democratic republic in Vietnam.

choices.label: C
choices.text: The French had established education systems throughout Indochina.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Ho Chi Minh intended to reclaim what the French had taken from his people.

questions.explanation: Ho Chi Minh's 1945 Declaration of Vietnamese Independence — which explicitly borrowed the language of the American Declaration of Independence — declared that France had violated Vietnam's natural rights through colonial exploitation and demanded that the Allied powers recognize Vietnamese sovereignty as promised in Wilson's Fourteen Points. Ho sought to reclaim what France had taken since the 1880s and argued that the Allies' own principles demanded support for Vietnamese independence. Regents CRQs use this document to test irony: Ho cited American ideals while the US later opposed his movement.

questions.subject: Global History
questions.stem: The economic theory described in this passage is most related to the ideas found in

choices.label: A
choices.text: The Spirit of the Laws by Baron de Montesquieu

choices.label: B
choices.text: Two Treatises of Government by John Locke

choices.label: C
choices.text: The Wealth of Nations by Adam Smith

choices.label: D
choices.text: A Vindication of the Rights of Woman by Mary Wollstonecraft

questions.explanation: The passage describes laissez-faire capitalism — the economic theory that markets function best with minimal government interference — associated with Adam Smith's The Wealth of Nations (1776), which argued that the 'invisible hand' of self-interest and competition allocates resources more efficiently than state direction. Smith's ideas underpinned 19th-century industrial capitalism and later informed free-market policies. Students should contrast The Wealth of Nations (capitalism, market freedom) with The Communist Manifesto (socialism, collective ownership).

questions.subject: Global History
questions.stem: Which issue are the Mothers of the Plaza de Mayo addressing?

choices.label: A
choices.text: the need for education reform

choices.label: B
choices.text: oppressive rule by a military dictatorship

choices.label: C
choices.text: a policy of ethnic cleansing

choices.label: D
choices.text: lack of job opportunities

questions.explanation: The Mothers of the Plaza de Mayo began their weekly silent protest in Buenos Aires in 1977 to demand information about their children who had 'disappeared' under Argentina's military junta (1976–1983), which kidnapped, tortured, and killed an estimated 9,000–30,000 political opponents during the 'Dirty War.' Their white headscarves (representing babies' diapers) became an iconic symbol of peaceful resistance to state terror. The Enduring Issue is human rights and the courage of civil society to confront authoritarian oppression.

questions.subject: Global History
questions.stem: What was an immediate result of the actions shown in these photographs?

choices.label: A
choices.text: the creation of a one-party political system

choices.label: B
choices.text: the formation of North Atlantic Treaty Organization [NATO]

choices.label: C
choices.text: the rise of fascist dictators

choices.label: D
choices.text: an easing of tensions between Eastern and Western Europe

questions.explanation: The photographs show the fall of the Berlin Wall on November 9–10, 1989, when East Germans breached and began dismantling the Wall after an East German official accidentally announced unrestricted border crossing. This immediately eased Cold War tensions, symbolized the end of communist repression in Eastern Europe, and directly preceded German reunification (October 3, 1990) and the Soviet Union's dissolution (1991). The Regents treats the Wall's fall as one of the defining turning points of the late 20th century.

questions.subject: Global History
questions.stem: The opinion expressed in this statement was most likely influenced by

choices.label: A
choices.text: containment concerns

choices.label: B
choices.text: appeasement policies

choices.label: C
choices.text: nationalistic feelings

choices.label: D
choices.text: fascist goals

questions.explanation: The statement expresses nationalist pride, ethnic superiority, or the belief that a particular nation has a unique historical mission — sentiments that fueled both constructive (Italian and German unification) and destructive (WWI alliance commitments, Balkan violence) outcomes in the 19th–20th centuries. Nationalist ideology is the Enduring Issue connecting the French Revolutionary 'nation in arms' through WWI's MAIN causes to 20th-century anti-colonial independence movements. Students must recognize nationalist language even when it is not labeled as such.

questions.subject: Global History
questions.stem: Which evidence best supports the claim made by Ho Chi Minh in this passage when he said “It was patriotism, not communism, that inspired” him?

choices.label: A
choices.text: He consistently favored Japan as an ally.

choices.label: B
choices.text: He reached out to the U.S. president for support for Vietnamese independence.

choices.label: C
choices.text: He sought to form alliances exclusively with countries under communist rule.

choices.label: D
choices.text: He regularly compromised with French officials to ease the transition of power.

questions.explanation: Ho Chi Minh wrote to President Truman in 1945–46, citing the Atlantic Charter and US anti-colonial rhetoric to appeal for American support of Vietnamese independence from France. This letter demonstrates that Ho initially sought US backing and that American failure to respond reinforced the communist path for his movement. The evidence supports his claim because it shows concrete diplomatic outreach before the US aligned with France. CRQs use this document to test understanding of Cold War priorities overriding anti-colonial principles.

questions.subject: Global History
questions.stem: Which problem is identified in this passage?

choices.label: A
choices.text: the opening of ports to foreign merchants

choices.label: B
choices.text: the low prices of agricultural products

choices.label: C
choices.text: the waste caused by grain overproduction

choices.label: D
choices.text: the exporting of much-needed food

questions.explanation: The passage likely describes the Irish Famine (1845–1852), when potato blight destroyed the main food source of Irish peasants while British landlords continued exporting grain from Ireland — a policy that transformed a natural disaster into a catastrophic famine killing approximately 1 million people and forcing 1–2 million to emigrate. The 'problem' is the exporting of food from a starving country, reflecting British laissez-faire economic ideology and colonial indifference. The Enduring Issue is the human consequences of economic systems applied without regard for human welfare.

questions.subject: Global History
questions.stem: After his victory, which course of action was recommended by the leader depicted in this illustration?

choices.label: A
choices.text: implementing programs to assist the widespread refugee crisis throughout the nation

choices.label: B
choices.text: promoting communism through state collectivization of lands and the use of propaganda

choices.label: C
choices.text: establishing a Truth and Reconciliation Commission to overcome a history of discrimination

choices.label: D
choices.text: having the African National Congress (ANC) plan peaceful marches, demonstrations, and boycotts

questions.explanation: The leader is Nelson Mandela, who became South Africa's first Black president in 1994 after apartheid's end, and his recommended course of action was the Truth and Reconciliation Commission (TRC), chaired by Archbishop Desmond Tutu. The TRC allowed perpetrators of apartheid-era violence to confess publicly in exchange for amnesty, aiming to heal national divisions through restorative rather than retributive justice. This approach is a landmark model for post-conflict reconciliation and the Enduring Issue of overcoming systemic racial oppression.

questions.subject: Global History
questions.stem: An economist would find this passage most useful for

choices.label: A
choices.text: supporting laissez-faire policies

choices.label: B
choices.text: arguing for fewer environmental restrictions

choices.label: C
choices.text: organizing protests against labor abuses

choices.label: D
choices.text: justifying protective tariffs

questions.explanation: The passage advocates for laissez-faire economics — minimal government intervention in markets, free trade, and allowing prices and wages to be set by supply and demand — associated with Adam Smith's The Wealth of Nations (1776) and 19th-century liberal economic thought. An economist would cite this passage to argue against tariffs, price controls, or welfare state interventions. Students should contrast laissez-faire with Keynesian economics (government spending to stimulate economies) and with Marxist critique of capitalism.

questions.subject: Global History
questions.stem: Which historical development is most closely associated with the situation described in this passage?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Cold War

choices.label: B
choices.text: Nuremberg Trials

choices.label: C
choices.text: Armenian Massacre

choices.label: D
choices.text: Berlin Conference

questions.explanation: The situation — likely depicting nuclear standoff, proxy war, or the division of a country into communist and non-communist zones — is characteristic of the Cold War (1947–1991), the global ideological competition between US-led capitalism and Soviet-backed communism. Cold War developments tested on the Regents include the Berlin Blockade (1948), Korean War (1950–53), Hungarian Revolution (1956), Cuban Missile Crisis (1962), Vietnam War, and Soviet-Afghan War (1979–89). Students should be able to identify Cold War contexts from maps, cartoons, and documents.

questions.subject: Global History
questions.stem: Which response is being taken to address the problem discussed in this article?

choices.label: A
choices.text: utilizing international cooperation

choices.label: B
choices.text: developing new technologies to meet local needs

choices.label: C
choices.text: relying on traditional methods of assessing the problem

choices.label: D
choices.text: increasing access to medication

questions.explanation: The article likely describes technological innovations tailored to developing-world conditions — solar microgrids, mobile banking systems, drought-resistant GMO crops, or low-cost water purification — created to address local needs in ways that expensive Western technologies cannot. This reflects the Enduring Issue of science and technology as solutions to human problems, particularly the persistent challenge of global inequality. Regents questions on globalization frequently test whether students can distinguish technology transfer from locally developed adaptive solutions.
questions.sourceIssue: Stimulus image was reused by a different released Regents source; hidden until re-imported from the original PDF.

questions.subject: Global History
questions.stem: The main purpose of this speech was to

choices.label: A
choices.text: allow for the peaceful transition of power in Vietnam from France to the Allies

choices.label: B
choices.text: appeal to Western nations to support Vietnamese independence

choices.label: C
choices.text: prevent the people of Vietnam from fighting for their independence

choices.label: D
choices.text: rally support among the Vietnamese people to join forces with the French against the Allies

questions.explanation: Ho Chi Minh's 1945–46 speeches and declarations appealed specifically to Western democratic nations — especially the United States — by invoking the language of the Atlantic Charter, Wilson's Fourteen Points, and the American Declaration of Independence to argue that Vietnamese people deserved the same self-determination Allied powers had promised to colonized peoples during WWII. His main purpose was to gain recognition and support for Vietnamese independence from French recolonization. CRQs test students on the irony that the US ultimately backed France instead.
questions.sourceIssue: Stimulus image was reused by a different released Regents source; hidden until re-imported from the original PDF.

questions.subject: Global History
questions.stem: Which viewpoint is expressed in this 1970 political cartoon?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Cost and instability resulted in arms control talks.

choices.label: B
choices.text: The Soviet Union had fewer nuclear weapons than the United States.

choices.label: C
choices.text: The United States paid more for their nuclear weapons.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Both Soviet and American citizens desired production of more weapons.

questions.explanation: The 1970 cartoon depicts the Strategic Arms Limitation Talks (SALT I, signed 1972) as a response to the enormous financial drain and political instability caused by the nuclear arms race between the US and USSR. Both superpowers recognized that unlimited arms competition was economically unsustainable and strategically dangerous, motivating détente-era diplomacy under Nixon and Brezhnev. The Enduring Issue is how economic cost and the threat of mutual annihilation forced adversaries to negotiate even without resolving ideological differences.

questions.subject: Global History
questions.stem: Which conclusion about these passages is valid?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Irish peasants refused to accept government assistance during the famine.

choices.label: B
choices.text: British officials disagreed about an appropriate response to economic problems.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Agricultural productivity increased as a result of British involvement.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Britain’s long-term objective was to support Irish nationalism.

questions.explanation: The British parliamentary debate over how to respond to a colonial situation — likely India's independence movement, the Irish famine, or African resistance — reveals that colonial administrators and politicians held sharply differing views on imperial policy, from harsh suppression to negotiated reform. This internal disagreement among British officials reflects the Enduring Issue of power and its limits: even dominant empires face challenges of governance, legitimacy, and the costs of maintaining control. Regents CRQs use such documents to show imperial perspectives were not monolithic.

questions.subject: Global History
questions.stem: What is a correct inference based on the data presented in this chart?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Global migration trends reveal people increasingly moving to urban centers and living in overcrowded environments.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Post-independence movements led some African nations to experience ethnic conflict, genocide, and an increase in refugee populations.

choices.label: C
choices.text: European imperialism has left many African nations with inadequate health care, a lack of education, and poverty.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Modern societies continue to negatively impact the environment by polluting air and water in addition to depleting natural resources.

questions.explanation: The chart likely shows that many African nations — particularly those with histories of European colonial rule — have inadequate infrastructure, low GDP per capita, or limited institutional capacity, reflecting how colonial exploitation deliberately underdeveloped African economies by extracting resources, suppressing local industries, and creating educational systems serving colonial rather than African interests. This Enduring Issue of imperialism's long-term consequences is central to Regents Global History; students should be able to distinguish colonial causes from post-independence political failures.

questions.subject: Global History
questions.stem: Marx and Engels are reacting to changes resulting from

choices.label: A
choices.text: industrialization

choices.label: B
choices.text: democratization

choices.label: C
choices.text: nationalism

choices.label: D
choices.text: imperialism

questions.explanation: Marx and Engels wrote The Communist Manifesto in 1848 in direct response to the brutal conditions of industrial capitalism — 14-hour workdays, child labor, dangerous factories, urban squalor, and complete lack of worker protections — transforming the British and European working class into an exploited proletariat. Industrialization created the economic contradictions Marx argued would inevitably generate class consciousness and revolutionary action. Students should distinguish the historical context (industrialization causing suffering) from Marx's prescription (communism as the solution).

questions.subject: Global History
questions.stem: Based on this passage, which claim is most plausible?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The Ottomans ruled through intimidation and fear.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Traditional Islamic society was undermined by the Ottomans’ advances in technology.

choices.label: C
choices.text: The Ottomans maintained political and economic stability over a vast region.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Europeans had no awareness of the Ottomans’ strengths and weaknesses.

questions.explanation: The Ottoman Empire at its height (16th–17th centuries) maintained remarkable political stability across a vast territory spanning three continents by implementing the millet system — allowing religious minorities (Christians, Jews) to self-govern under their own laws — and by using the devshirme system to recruit talented administrators from conquered peoples. This managed diversity enabled centuries of relative stability and economic integration of diverse trade routes. Students should distinguish the Ottoman system's pragmatic tolerance from later 19th-century decline as nationalism eroded the millet framework.

questions.subject: Global History
questions.stem: The Japanese social and economic changes of the late 19th century described in the passage were a result of

choices.label: A
choices.text: isolationist policies

choices.label: B
choices.text: the Russo-Japanese War

choices.label: C
choices.text: Marxist labor reforms

choices.label: D
choices.text: rapid industrialization

questions.explanation: The Meiji Restoration's 'Fukoku Kyōhei' (Rich Country, Strong Military) policy transformed Japan from a feudal agrarian society into an industrialized nation within one generation: building railroads and steamships, establishing steel mills and textile factories, adopting Western legal codes, and creating a conscript army — all between 1868 and 1900. Japan's rapid industrialization eliminated centuries of technological lag with the West and enabled military victories over China (1895) and Russia (1905). This is the Regents' primary example of successful non-Western modernization.

questions.subject: Global History
questions.stem: Which event most directly contributed to the action shown in this photo?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Iran’s decision to become a member of OPEC

choices.label: B
choices.text: founding of the Mothers of the Plaza de Mayo

choices.label: C
choices.text: passage of the Universal Declaration of Human Rights

choices.label: D
choices.text: the Ayatollah Khomeini’s rise to power

questions.explanation: The 1979 Iranian Revolution led by Ayatollah Ruhollah Khomeini overthrew Shah Mohammad Reza Pahlavi and established an Islamic Republic governed by sharia law and clerical authority. The revolution directly caused the US Embassy hostage crisis (November 4, 1979–January 20, 1981), in which Iranian students seized 52 American diplomats for 444 days, permanently transforming US-Iran relations and reflecting the revolution's rejection of Western influence. Regents CRQs pair the Iranian Revolution with the Shah's secular modernization to test cause-and-effect reasoning.

questions.subject: Global History
questions.stem: The international organization shown on this map was originally established to improve its

choices.label: A
choices.text: access to development loans by the International Monetary Fund

choices.label: B
choices.text: defensive capabilities during the Cold War

choices.label: C
choices.text: participation in the economic benefits of globalization

choices.label: D
choices.text: ability to counter the harmful effects of pollution and climate change

questions.explanation: NATO (North Atlantic Treaty Organization), founded April 4, 1949, was established by the US and Western European democracies as a collective defense alliance under the principle that an attack on one member constitutes an attack on all — directly responding to Soviet expansion in Eastern Europe and the 1948 Berlin Blockade. NATO was the institutional embodiment of containment policy and is the primary example of collective security during the Cold War. Students should distinguish NATO (Western, founded 1949) from the Warsaw Pact (Soviet bloc, founded 1955).

questions.subject: Global History
questions.stem: Mandela’s support for universal suffrage in South Africa led to

choices.label: A
choices.text: mass emigration

choices.label: B
choices.text: membership in the United Nations (UN)

choices.label: C
choices.text: an end to white minority control

choices.label: D
choices.text: confiscation of private farms

questions.explanation: Nelson Mandela advocated for universal suffrage — one person, one vote regardless of race — which directly ended apartheid's system of white minority rule when South Africa held its first fully democratic elections on April 27, 1994, and Mandela was elected president with 62.6% of the vote. Apartheid (1948–1994) had legally segregated South Africa along racial lines, denying Black South Africans voting rights, freedom of movement, and equal access to education and healthcare. Regents CRQs treat the 1994 elections as a turning point in human rights history.

questions.subject: Global History
questions.stem: The conditions shown in this illustration played a major role in bringing about the

choices.label: A
choices.text: unification of Italy

choices.label: B
choices.text: Potsdam Conference

choices.label: C
choices.text: start of World War I

choices.label: D
choices.text: signing of the Warsaw Pact

questions.explanation: The illustration depicts the MAIN causes of WWI — Militarism (arms races), Alliance systems (Triple Alliance vs. Triple Entente), Imperialism (colonial rivalry), and Nationalism (especially in the Balkans) — creating a powder keg that exploded when Archduke Franz Ferdinand was assassinated in Sarajevo on June 28, 1914. The alliance system's automatic mobilization commitments rapidly escalated a regional conflict into a global war within five weeks. Students must distinguish the spark (assassination) from the underlying conditions that made war likely.

questions.subject: Global History
questions.stem: Which action was a direct result of the event depicted in this cartoon?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Limits were placed on the number of missiles in the United States and the Soviet Union.

choices.label: B
choices.text: The power of the United Nations security council increased.

choices.label: C
choices.text: The Soviet Union sold nuclear weapons to India and Pakistan.

choices.label: D
choices.text: China obtained additional nuclear weapons under Mao Zedong.

questions.explanation: The Cuban Missile Crisis of October 1962 — thirteen days of nuclear brinkmanship when the US discovered Soviet nuclear missiles being installed in Cuba — ended with the Kennedy-Khrushchev agreement: the Soviets removed their missiles from Cuba, the US pledged not to invade Cuba and secretly agreed to remove US Jupiter missiles from Turkey. This near-catastrophe demonstrated the dangers of unchecked nuclear competition and directly motivated the Limited Nuclear Test Ban Treaty (1963) and SALT I negotiations (1969–72) that placed the first limits on US and Soviet missile stockpiles.

questions.subject: Global History
questions.stem: Which event most influenced the situation being described in this passage?

choices.label: A
choices.text: the Berlin Conference

choices.label: B
choices.text: World War II

choices.label: C
choices.text: the Treaty of Versailles

choices.label: D
choices.text: the Congress of Vienna

questions.explanation: The 1884–85 Berlin Conference established the rules for European partition of Africa, creating colonial boundaries that grouped rival ethnic and religious communities together and split cohesive groups across different European colonies. These artificial borders created structural conditions for post-independence ethnic conflicts that persist into the 21st century in Nigeria, Rwanda, Somalia, and the Democratic Republic of Congo. The passage likely describes how a specific African country's colonial-era boundaries produced ethnic tensions or resource conflicts.

questions.subject: Global History
questions.stem: Which claim is best supported by the evidence in this passage?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The Khmer Rouge only arrested artists and authors.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Many Cambodians resisted arrest by fleeing the country.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Sisowath Doung Chanto questioned his father’s patriotism.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Millions of Cambodians were executed during the civil war.

questions.explanation: The Khmer Rouge regime under Pol Pot (1975–1979) carried out a genocidal campaign in Cambodia, executing an estimated 1.5–2 million people — roughly 25% of Cambodia's population — through forced agricultural collectivization, mass executions at 'killing fields,' starvation, and forced labor. The regime targeted ethnic minorities, educated professionals, Buddhist monks, and anyone perceived as a threat to its 'Year Zero' utopian ideology. This is a key 20th-century genocide case study tested on the Regents alongside the Holocaust, Armenian Genocide, and Rwandan Genocide.
questions.sourceIssue: Stimulus image was reused by a different released Regents source; hidden until re-imported from the original PDF.

questions.subject: Global History
questions.stem: In this quotation, Nelson Mandela is referring to the end of which policy?

choices.label: A
choices.text: apartheid

choices.label: B
choices.text: forced migration

choices.label: C
choices.text: self-determination

choices.label: D
choices.text: urbanization

questions.explanation: Nelson Mandela's 1994 inaugural address invoked the end of apartheid — the system of institutionalized racial segregation and white minority rule that had governed South Africa since 1948, classifying South Africans by race, enforcing separate amenities, restricting movement and employment, and denying the Black majority political rights. Mandela, who had served 27 years in prison as a political prisoner, led the ANC's non-violent and later armed resistance. The Regents treats the end of apartheid as a landmark human rights turning point of the 20th century.
questions.sourceIssue: Stimulus image was reused by a different released Regents source; hidden until re-imported from the original PDF.

questions.subject: Global History
questions.stem: Which other document presented a message similar to this letter?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Germany’s Nuremberg Laws

choices.label: B
choices.text: Mandela’s Rivonia Trial Speech

choices.label: C
choices.text: Mao’s Little Red Book

choices.label: D
choices.text: France’s Law of Suspects

questions.explanation: Mandela's 1964 Rivonia Trial speech — 'I Am Prepared to Die' — like the anti-apartheid letter referenced, expressed the willingness to sacrifice personal freedom for the principle of equal rights and democratic governance in South Africa. Both documents share the central message that apartheid's injustice demanded resistance and that the goal was a non-racial democratic South Africa. Regents CRQs frequently pair Mandela documents across time periods to test students' ability to identify consistent themes of justice, sacrifice, and democratic aspiration.

questions.subject: Global History
questions.stem: Based on this passage, one can infer that popular radio programming

choices.label: A
choices.text: was a propaganda tool that led to the Tutsis being seen as inferior

choices.label: B
choices.text: gave hope to the Tutsis during the genocide

choices.label: C
choices.text: was used to help end the genocide

choices.label: D
choices.text: was not well known in Rwanda

questions.explanation: Radio Mille Collines ('Radio Machete') broadcast propaganda demonizing Tutsis as 'cockroaches' and inciting Hutu listeners to kill their Tutsi neighbors in the lead-up to and during the 1994 Rwandan Genocide, in which approximately 800,000 Tutsi and moderate Hutu were murdered in just 100 days. The radio broadcasts demonstrate how media and propaganda can transform ethnic prejudice — originally intensified by Belgian colonial classification systems — into mass murder. This is a key case study for the Enduring Issue of human rights violation and the role of dehumanizing language.

questions.subject: Global History
questions.stem: What was a consequence of the situation shown in this chart?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Britain and France were bankrupted by the high cost of governing these mandates.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Germany regained substantial power in sub-Saharan Africa.

choices.label: C
choices.text: More mandates were created as nations struggled to govern themselves after World War II.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Nationalist movements developed in areas under European control.

questions.explanation: The chart showing economic exploitation of colonized territories — resource extraction, unfavorable trade terms, suppression of local industries — caused rising nationalist movements in Asia, Africa, and the Middle East as educated colonial subjects recognized the disparity between colonial rhetoric about 'civilizing missions' and the reality of economic and political oppression. Nationalist leaders like Gandhi (India), Nkrumah (Ghana), and Sukarno (Indonesia) drew on both indigenous traditions and Western Enlightenment concepts to demand independence. The Enduring Issue is imperialism generating its own resistance.

questions.subject: Global History
questions.stem: What would be the best use for this pair of maps?

choices.label: A
choices.text: to explain why European powers used the mandate system

choices.label: B
choices.text: to examine the relationship between fresh water and Arab settlement patterns

choices.label: C
choices.text: to illustrate the advantages Palestinian Arabs have over Arabs living in Egypt

choices.label: D
choices.text: to understand a reason used to establish boundaries for partition

questions.explanation: The pair of maps likely shows Jewish population distribution in Europe before and after the Holocaust alongside the proposed UN partition of Palestine, providing geographic context for understanding why the 1947 Partition Plan proposed establishing Israel in British Mandate Palestine. The Holocaust's decimation of European Jewry and the displacement of Jewish survivors created urgent pressure for a Jewish homeland. Regents CRQs use this type of map pairing to test students' ability to connect the Holocaust's human geography to the political geography of the Middle East.

questions.subject: Global History
questions.stem: Which situation was a contributing factor in the decision to partition British Palestine as shown on the 1947 map?

choices.label: A
choices.text: decolonization from French rule

choices.label: B
choices.text: formation of the Warsaw Pact

choices.label: C
choices.text: capture of the Suez Canal

choices.label: D
choices.text: mass migrations following the Holocaust

questions.explanation: The Holocaust's murder of approximately 6 million European Jews and the displacement of 250,000 Jewish survivors — living in displaced persons camps with no safe home to return to — created overwhelming international moral pressure and political will to support establishing a Jewish homeland. Mass migration of Holocaust survivors toward British Mandate Palestine, combined with Zionist political lobbying, directly shaped the 1947 UN Partition Plan (Resolution 181) and the subsequent creation of Israel on May 14, 1948. Students must connect Holocaust geography to post-WWII Middle East politics.

questions.subject: Global History
questions.stem: During which conflict did the events depicted in this passage occur?

choices.label: A
choices.text: World War II

choices.label: B
choices.text: Cold War

choices.label: C
choices.text: World War I

choices.label: D
choices.text: Russo-Japanese War

questions.explanation: The passage describes a Cold War confrontation — possibly the Berlin Blockade (1948–49), Korean War (1950–53), Berlin Crisis (1961), or Cuban Missile Crisis (1962) — in which the US and USSR engaged in tense standoffs mediated by nuclear deterrence and proxy conflict rather than direct combat. These events reflect the defining logic of the Cold War: superpower competition conducted through client states, economic pressure, and ideological competition rather than direct war. Students should be able to identify Cold War contexts from specific leaders, locations, and policy language.

questions.subject: Global History
questions.stem: Based on Denys’ account, which situation developed during the partition of India?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Religious and ethnic conflict increased along the borders.

choices.label: B
choices.text: British troops seized control of trains.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Pakistan installed a communist regime.

choices.label: D
choices.text: India became a mandate of Great Britain.

questions.explanation: The 1947 Partition of British India into the independent nations of India and Pakistan triggered one of history's largest mass migrations — approximately 14–17 million people crossing new borders in both directions — accompanied by communal violence between Hindus, Sikhs, and Muslims that killed an estimated 200,000–2 million people. Denys's account captures the chaos, displacement, and sectarian violence along the Punjab border. The Enduring Issue is how political borders created to separate religious communities can produce humanitarian catastrophe.

questions.subject: Global History
questions.stem: The event described in this passage resulted in

choices.label: A
choices.text: the expansion of World War II into Asia

choices.label: B
choices.text: an international agreement banning chemical weapons

choices.label: C
choices.text: the development of a new type of arms race

choices.label: D
choices.text: Hiroshima’s emergence as an important center for trade

questions.explanation: The passage describes the Soviet launch of Sputnik I on October 4, 1957 — the world's first artificial satellite — which shocked the US and initiated the Space Race, a new dimension of Cold War competition. Sputnik demonstrated Soviet ballistic missile capability, raising fears that the USSR could deliver nuclear warheads to US cities. This prompted the US to create NASA (1958), expand science education (National Defense Education Act), and accelerate ICBM development, transforming the arms race into an arms-and-space race. Students should link Sputnik to both the Space Race and ICBM acceleration.

questions.subject: Global History
questions.stem: The significance of the events shown on this time line is that they represent

choices.label: A
choices.text: attempts to force empires to decolonize

choices.label: B
choices.text: underlying causes of World War II

choices.label: C
choices.text: the expansion of communist forces into Europe and Asia

choices.label: D
choices.text: efforts by Manchuria and Czechoslovakia to maintain the balance of power

questions.explanation: The timeline of events leading to WWII — Hitler's appointment as Chancellor (1933), German rearmament (1935), Rhineland remilitarization (1936), Anschluss with Austria (1938), Sudetenland annexation via Munich Agreement (1938), invasion of Czechoslovakia (1939), and invasion of Poland (September 1, 1939) — represents the step-by-step failure of Allied appeasement policy that enabled German aggression to escalate into world war. The Enduring Issue is how unchecked aggression and failed diplomacy lead to large-scale conflict. Students must distinguish appeasement (giving concessions to avoid war) from deterrence.
questions.sourceIssue: Stimulus image was reused by a different released Regents source; hidden until re-imported from the original PDF.

questions.subject: Global History
questions.stem: Which claim about the need to create Pakistan is best supported by this passage?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Concerns about the loss of shared Muslim and Hindu traditions need to be considered.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Local residents should determine whether Kashmir is controlled by Muslims or Hindus.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Muslims and Hindus could not live in harmony without colonial assistance.

choices.label: D
choices.text: The cultural identity of Muslims is unique and separate from that of Hindus.

questions.explanation: The All-India Muslim League under Muhammad Ali Jinnah argued that India's Muslim minority constituted a separate nation with distinct culture, history, and religion that could not be protected within a Hindu-majority independent India — the 'Two-Nation Theory' that provided the ideological justification for Pakistan's creation in 1947. The passage reflects the claim that Muslim cultural identity was incompatible with a unified post-colonial Indian state. Regents CRQs test this as an example of nationalism and religious identity driving political partition.
questions.sourceIssue: Stimulus image was reused by a different released Regents source; hidden until re-imported from the original PDF.

questions.subject: Global History
questions.stem: Which historical turning point was directly influenced by the events described in this document?

choices.label: A
choices.text: British Parliament began to pass legislation to address concerns over working conditions.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Japan shifted from a period of isolation to a more modernized state.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Germany unified and industrialized, becoming a dominant world power by World War I.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Europeans lost profitable colonies in the Americas and sought new imperial holdings.

questions.explanation: The passage likely describes the Spanish and Portuguese conquest of the Americas in the late 15th–16th centuries, which depleted indigenous populations by an estimated 90% through disease, forced labor, and warfare, and eventually led to the decline of Caribbean plantation labor. This created the economic demand that drove the transatlantic slave trade, as European colonizers began importing enslaved Africans to replace indigenous labor — transforming Africa, the Americas, and the Atlantic world. The Regents treats this as a turning point connecting the Columbian Exchange, colonialism, and the slave trade.

questions.subject: Global History
questions.stem: Gandhi’s activity in this photograph is a reaction to

choices.label: A
choices.text: South Asian famines

choices.label: B
choices.text: India’s nonalignment during the Cold War

choices.label: C
choices.text: British imperialism of India

choices.label: D
choices.text: Russia’s invasion of South Asia

questions.explanation: Gandhi's 1930 Salt March was a direct act of nonviolent civil disobedience against British imperialism — specifically the British salt monopoly that taxed a basic necessity while preventing Indians from harvesting their own salt. Gandhi led 240 miles from Ahmedabad to the Arabian Sea at Dandi, where he illegally collected salt, inspiring mass civil disobedience across India. This protest demonstrated satyagraha (truth-force), Gandhi's philosophy of nonviolent resistance that later influenced Martin Luther King Jr. and other civil rights movements worldwide.

questions.subject: Global History
questions.stem: What is the primary focus of these documents?

choices.label: A
choices.text: the importance of the Cold War in history

choices.label: B
choices.text: the role of American culture in globalization

choices.label: C
choices.text: the value of immigration to German regions

choices.label: D
choices.text: the need for cultural exchanges in Europe

questions.explanation: The documents examining McDonald's, Hollywood films, American music, and English language dominance in global markets focus on cultural globalization and the spread of American popular culture worldwide — sometimes called 'Americanization' or cultural imperialism. The Enduring Issue is whether globalization homogenizes cultures or whether local communities adapt and resist foreign cultural influences. Regents CRQs ask students to distinguish cultural globalization from economic globalization and to evaluate different perspectives on this process.

questions.subject: Global History
questions.stem: What claim is best supported by the evidence included in this article?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The mujahedeen supported the pro- communist government in Afghanistan.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Cold War conflicts in Afghanistan contributed to the rise of the Taliban.

choices.label: C
choices.text: The United States attempted to eliminate the mujahedeen in the 1980s.

choices.label: D
choices.text: The invasion of Afghanistan secured Soviet influence in the region.

questions.explanation: The CIA-backed mujahideen resistance during the Soviet-Afghan War (1979–1989) provided weapons, training, and funding to Afghan fighters — including Arab volunteers organized by figures including Osama bin Laden — who later formed the core of al-Qaeda and the Taliban after the Soviets withdrew in 1989. The article demonstrates that Cold War proxy strategy in Afghanistan directly contributed to the rise of Islamist extremism and the September 11, 2001 attacks. Students should trace the causal chain from Cold War intervention to post-Cold War terrorism.

questions.subject: Global History
questions.stem: This document was drafted largely in response to the

choices.label: A
choices.text: Rwandan Genocide

choices.label: B
choices.text: Meiji Restoration

choices.label: C
choices.text: Holocaust

choices.label: D
choices.text: Irish Potato Famine

questions.explanation: The Universal Declaration of Human Rights (UDHR, 1948) was drafted primarily as a global response to the Holocaust — the Nazi regime's systematic murder of approximately 6 million Jews and millions of others that demonstrated the catastrophic consequences of state-sponsored dehumanization without legal protection. Eleanor Roosevelt chaired the drafting committee, and the document was explicitly motivated by the need to prevent future genocides. Regents CRQs link the UDHR to Nuremberg trials as parallel post-WWII institutions building international human rights law.

questions.subject: Global History
questions.stem: Which leader agreed with the philosophy in this passage?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Maximilien Robespierre

choices.label: B
choices.text: Jiang Jieshi [Chiang Kai-shek]

choices.label: C
choices.text: Fidel Castro

choices.label: D
choices.text: Augusto Pinochet

questions.explanation: Fidel Castro, who led Cuba's communist revolution in 1959 and ruled until 2008, embraced Marxist-Leninist ideology — the belief that capitalist exploitation of the working class must be overthrown by proletarian revolution and replaced with a one-party communist state controlling the means of production. Castro's alignment with the Soviet Union made Cuba a Cold War flashpoint, particularly during the 1962 Cuban Missile Crisis. Students should connect Castro's ideology to Marx's philosophy but recognize Cuba's revolution emerged from Cuban nationalist grievances against US-backed dictator Batista.

questions.subject: Global History
questions.stem: What was an immediate effect of the actions depicted in this cartoon?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Nazi leaders were tried for crimes against humanity at Nuremberg.

choices.label: B
choices.text: The United States entered World War II.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Wilson’s Fourteen Points allowed Germany to rebuild its military.

choices.label: D
choices.text: The Nazis proceeded to take over new territory.

questions.explanation: The cartoon depicts the Munich Agreement of September 30, 1938, where British Prime Minister Neville Chamberlain and French Premier Édouard Daladier allowed Hitler to annex the Sudetenland (German-speaking Czechoslovakia) in exchange for his pledge of no further territorial demands — the policy of appeasement. Rather than satisfying Hitler, Munich encouraged further aggression: within six months, Germany occupied all of Czechoslovakia, and by September 1939, invaded Poland, starting WWII. The Regents uses Munich as the defining example of appeasement's failure.

questions.subject: Global History
questions.stem: During which war did the conference described in this passage take place?

choices.label: A
choices.text: World War I

choices.label: B
choices.text: World War II

choices.label: C
choices.text: the Korean War

choices.label: D
choices.text: the Vietnam War

questions.explanation: The passage describes the Yalta Conference (February 4–11, 1945), where Churchill, Roosevelt, and Stalin met to plan post-WWII Europe's reorganization — agreeing to Germany's division into occupation zones, free elections in liberated Eastern Europe (a promise Stalin violated), and Soviet entry into the Pacific War. Yalta's decisions effectively established the post-war balance of power that defined the early Cold War. Students confuse Yalta (1945, war still ongoing) with Potsdam (July 1945, after Germany's defeat) — both set post-war terms but with different participants and outcomes.

questions.subject: Global History
questions.stem: The situation described in this passage was part of which historical development?

choices.label: A
choices.text: regional conflicts during the Cold War

choices.label: B
choices.text: rise of the Organization of Petroleum Exporting Countries (OPEC) in the Middle East

choices.label: C
choices.text: growth of nationalism in Southeast Asia

choices.label: D
choices.text: decolonization in Africa

questions.explanation: The passage likely describes a Cold War proxy conflict — Korea (1950–53), Vietnam (1955–75), Angola (1975–2002), or Nicaragua (1979–90) — in which the US and USSR supported opposing sides without direct military confrontation between the superpowers. Regional proxy wars were how the Cold War's ideological competition was actually fought, costing millions of lives in developing nations while protecting superpower cities from nuclear destruction. The Enduring Issue is how great-power competition imposes devastating costs on smaller nations.

questions.subject: Global History
questions.stem: Which situation is most responsible for the events described in this passage?

choices.label: A
choices.text: the failure of the Great Leap Forward

choices.label: B
choices.text: the creation of Western spheres of influence

choices.label: C
choices.text: the end of Japanese occupation after World War II

choices.label: D
choices.text: the death of Deng Xiaoping

questions.explanation: Mao's Great Leap Forward (1958–1962) attempted to rapidly industrialize and collectivize China's agricultural economy but caused one of history's worst famines, killing an estimated 15–55 million people through starvation caused by grain quotas enforced at gunpoint even as harvests failed. The catastrophic failure discredited Mao's authority and prompted the Cultural Revolution (1966–76) as his response to critics. The passage about the Cultural Revolution's purges of those who questioned government policies was Mao's direct political response to the Great Leap Forward's exposure of his failures.

questions.subject: Global History
questions.stem: The conflict referred to in the documents is most closely associated with

choices.label: A
choices.text: the Chinese Civil War

choices.label: B
choices.text: the Cold War

choices.label: C
choices.text: the Cultural Revolution

choices.label: D
choices.text: global terrorism

questions.explanation: The documents reference nuclear deterrence, ideological competition, and proxy conflicts between capitalist and communist blocs — the defining characteristics of the Cold War (1947–1991). The Cold War's global reach included crises in Europe (Berlin), Asia (Korea, Vietnam), Latin America (Cuba), Africa (Angola, Ethiopia), and the Middle East (proxy backing for Israel and Arab states). Students should be able to identify Cold War context from references to 'containment,' 'domino theory,' or the US-Soviet superpower rivalry.

questions.subject: Global History
questions.stem: This cartoon suggests that the Chinese people and their government

choices.label: A
choices.text: disagreed on whether to adopt democratic reforms

choices.label: B
choices.text: wanted to continue Mao’s economic programs

choices.label: C
choices.text: had no interest in the principles of the Enlightenment

choices.label: D
choices.text: saw the need to increase agricultural imports

questions.explanation: The cartoon likely depicts the June 1989 Tiananmen Square massacre, when student pro-democracy protesters gathered in Beijing were violently suppressed by Chinese military forces, killing hundreds to thousands. The image of a lone protestor blocking a column of tanks became a global symbol of individual resistance against authoritarian power. The Chinese government's brutal response demonstrated that the Communist Party would accept capitalist economic reforms but never democratic political challenges — the Enduring Issue of political repression versus demands for human rights.

questions.subject: Global History
questions.stem: Which historical development led Nehru to promote the policy of unaligned areas?

choices.label: A
choices.text: partitioning of India and Pakistan at independence

choices.label: B
choices.text: internal friction between various Indian ethnic groups

choices.label: C
choices.text: political pressure from his Parliament to pursue isolationism

choices.label: D
choices.text: expansion of Cold War blocs

questions.explanation: Indian Prime Minister Jawaharlal Nehru promoted the Non-Aligned Movement (NAM), founded at the Bandung Conference in 1955, as a 'Third Way' for newly independent nations that rejected alignment with either the US-led NATO bloc or the Soviet-led Warsaw Pact. Nehru argued that Cold War superpower competition threatened to reduce newly independent nations to pawns and that genuine independence required avoiding entanglement in both blocs. The expansion of Cold War into Asia and Africa made this position strategically appealing to dozens of newly decolonized states.

questions.subject: Global History
questions.stem: Which group benefi tted the most from the changes shown on these maps?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Palestinian nationalists

choices.label: B
choices.text: the government of Jordan

choices.label: C
choices.text: the citizens of Lebanon

choices.label: D
choices.text: Zionists and Jewish immigrants

questions.explanation: The maps show the 1947 UN Partition Plan and the 1948 creation of Israel, both of which provided the territorial homeland for Zionists — Jews who had advocated since the late 19th-century Zionist movement led by Theodor Herzl for a Jewish state in historic Palestine. Jewish immigration to Palestine had grown throughout the British Mandate period (1920–1948), accelerating after the Holocaust. While Zionists and Jewish immigrants gained a state, Palestinian Arabs experienced the Nakba (catastrophe), losing their homes and homeland.

questions.subject: Global History
questions.stem: These photographs could best be used when studying the end of which conflict?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Persian Gulf War

choices.label: B
choices.text: Korean War

choices.label: C
choices.text: World War II

choices.label: D
choices.text: Cold War

questions.explanation: The photographs showing the fall of the Berlin Wall (November 9–10, 1989), German reunification (October 3, 1990), and Soviet dissolution (December 25, 1991) collectively document the end of the Cold War. These events unfolded as communist regimes across Eastern Europe collapsed in rapid succession in 1989–91 due to economic stagnation, Gorbachev's liberalizing reforms, and mass popular protest. The Regents treats these events as a cluster of related turning points rather than isolated incidents.

questions.subject: Global History
questions.stem: Which situation in Rwanda led to the events described in this passage?

choices.label: A
choices.text: a large influx of refugees

choices.label: B
choices.text: the establishment of a classless society

choices.label: C
choices.text: famine caused by environmental disaster

choices.label: D
choices.text: ethnic rivalries promoted by colonial favoritism

questions.explanation: Belgian colonial rule in Rwanda (1916–1962) institutionalized and hardened the distinction between Hutu and Tutsi, issuing identity cards classifying all Rwandans by ethnicity and favoring Tutsis for administrative positions — transforming a fluid social distinction into a rigid racial hierarchy. When Belgians shifted support to Hutu majority rule at independence, accumulated grievances and colonial-era ethnic hierarchies created the conditions for the 1994 genocide. The Enduring Issue is how colonial 'divide and rule' policies create lasting ethnic tensions that outlast colonial rule.

questions.subject: Global History
questions.stem: The passage was written to justify

choices.label: A
choices.text: the Great Famine

choices.label: B
choices.text: Irish protests against British trade policies

choices.label: C
choices.text: the importation of foreign food through English ports

choices.label: D
choices.text: the export of Irish grain

questions.explanation: The passage justifies Irish resistance to British mercantilist trade policies that prohibited Ireland from exporting wool, glass, and other manufactured goods to protect English industries — policies that impoverished Irish workers and manufacturers. This is most likely Jonathan Swift's 'A Modest Proposal' (1729) or a similar Patriot pamphlet, arguing that British economic exploitation violated natural rights and common humanity. The Enduring Issue is colonial economic systems designed to enrich the metropole at the colonized periphery's expense.

questions.subject: Global History
questions.stem: Based on these documents, which method is being used by Mandela and de Klerk to address the conflict in South Africa?

choices.label: A
choices.text: international economic sanctions

choices.label: B
choices.text: a violent revolution

choices.label: C
choices.text: outside military intervention

choices.label: D
choices.text: political compromise

questions.explanation: Nelson Mandela (ANC) and F.W. de Klerk (National Party, white minority government) ended apartheid through negotiated political compromise: de Klerk released Mandela in February 1990, unbanned the ANC, and agreed to multiracial elections, while Mandela agreed to a peaceful transition rather than demanding immediate revolutionary change. Their shared 1993 Nobel Peace Prize recognized this achievement. This model of political compromise — avoiding civil war through negotiation — is a key Regents example of peaceful conflict resolution.

questions.subject: Global History
questions.stem: Which historical development is most similar to the situation described in this passage?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Nazi actions in Germany in the 1930s and 1940s

choices.label: B
choices.text: Argentinian mothers protesting in the Plaza de Mayo in the 1980s and 1990s

choices.label: C
choices.text: Ayatollah Khomeini’s rise to power in Iran in the late 1970s

choices.label: D
choices.text: Nelson Mandela’s campaigns against apartheid in the early 1960s

questions.explanation: The Khmer Rouge's systematic targeting of ethnic minorities, educated professionals, and religious groups in Cambodia (1975–1979) closely parallels Nazi actions in Germany and occupied territories (1933–1945), both employing mass deportation, forced labor, dehumanizing propaganda, and systematic mass murder to eliminate perceived enemies. Both regimes are studied as 20th-century genocides, though Cambodia's targeted class enemies and perceived collaborators alongside ethnic minorities, while the Holocaust primarily targeted Jews and other specific groups. The Enduring Issue is the pattern of genocide repeating across different contexts.

questions.subject: Global History
questions.stem: Which turning point led to the 1967 war that inspired these letters?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Palestinian Intifada

choices.label: B
choices.text: signing of the Camp David Accords

choices.label: C
choices.text: Persian Gulf War

choices.label: D
choices.text: creation of the State of Israel

questions.explanation: The 1948 creation of the State of Israel and the resulting Arab-Israeli War left Jordan controlling the West Bank and Egypt controlling Gaza until 1967, when Israel's Six-Day War (June 5–10, 1967) captured these territories along with the Sinai Peninsula and Golan Heights. The letters responding to the Six-Day War reflect the competing Palestinian and Israeli nationalist claims intensified by Israel's territorial expansion. The creation of Israel is the root turning point that produced all subsequent Arab-Israeli conflicts tested on the Regents.

questions.subject: Global History
questions.stem: Which long-standing policy influenced the actions and words expressed in these passages by Nelson Mandela and F. W. de Klerk?

choices.label: A
choices.text: tribal decision-making

choices.label: B
choices.text: international cooperation

choices.label: C
choices.text: apartheid

choices.label: D
choices.text: non-aggression
