questions.explanation: Apartheid (Afrikaans: 'separateness') was South Africa's official system of institutionalized racial segregation (1948–1994) that classified citizens by race, restricted Black South Africans to homelands (Bantustans), enforced pass laws limiting movement, and denied political rights. The documents — likely including ANC protest documents and apartheid-era laws — both reflect and resist this system. Regents CRQs pair apartheid documents with the UDHR and Mandela's writings to test the Enduring Issue of human rights and resistance to oppression.

questions.subject: Global History
questions.stem: The human rights violations discussed in the passage occurred during the

choices.label: A
choices.text: Rwandan Genocide

choices.label: B
choices.text: Holocaust

choices.label: C
choices.text: Armenian Genocide

choices.label: D
choices.text: Amritsar Massacre

questions.explanation: The Holocaust (Shoah) was the Nazi regime's systematic, state-sponsored persecution and murder of approximately 6 million Jews and 5–6 million others — including Roma, disabled people, Soviet POWs, and political prisoners — using an industrial bureaucracy of ghettos, deportation trains, concentration camps, and death camps (Auschwitz, Treblinka, Sobibor) primarily between 1941 and 1945. The Nuremberg Laws (1935) had previously stripped German Jews of citizenship. The Regents treats the Holocaust as the defining 20th-century human rights catastrophe.

questions.subject: Global History
questions.stem: Which provision of the United Nations Universal Declaration of Human Rights was violated by the actions described in the passage?

choices.label: A
choices.text: No one shall be held in slavery or servitude.

choices.label: B
choices.text: No one shall be subjected to arbitrary arrest, detention, or exile.

choices.label: C
choices.text: No one shall be arbitrarily deprived of his property.

choices.label: D
choices.text: No one shall be forced to belong to an association.

questions.explanation: The passage likely describes South Africa's apartheid-era Group Areas Act or pass laws that required Black South Africans to live in designated 'homelands' and prohibited them from owning property in white areas — directly violating the UDHR Article 17 principle that 'no one shall be arbitrarily deprived of his property.' This connection between a specific historical injustice and a specific UDHR provision is a standard Regents CRQ task. Students must be able to match historical violations to specific UDHR articles.

questions.subject: Global History
questions.stem: For which purpose could this passage best be used?

choices.label: A
choices.text: to provide a primary source of information to explain events during the Cold War

choices.label: B
choices.text: to give an example of resistance methods that can be modeled to other societies

choices.label: C
choices.text: to show the perspective of indigenous people during the time period

choices.label: D
choices.text: to develop policies of religious toleration and consideration of traditional cultures

questions.explanation: The passage provides a firsthand account from an indigenous person — likely a Native American, African, or Asian — describing the experience of European colonization: loss of land, forced religious conversion, cultural destruction, or exploitation. Primary source indigenous perspectives are essential for understanding colonialism's human cost beyond European accounts. The Enduring Issue is power and how it was exercised in colonial relationships; indigenous voices counter the 'civilizing mission' narrative Europeans used to justify imperialism.

questions.subject: Global History
questions.stem: This poem describes events related to which international conflict?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Vietnam War

choices.label: B
choices.text: Korean War

choices.label: C
choices.text: World War I

choices.label: D
choices.text: World War II

questions.explanation: The poem describes the suffering, futility, and disillusionment of trench warfare — likely referencing WWI battles such as the Somme (July–November 1916, 1 million casualties) or Verdun (February–December 1916, 700,000 casualties) — where soldiers endured mustard gas, machine guns, and artillery barrages in static trenches for minimal territorial gains. WWI poetry by Wilfred Owen, Rupert Brooke, and Siegfried Sassoon documents the gap between patriotic recruitment rhetoric and industrial-age warfare's brutal reality. The Enduring Issue is the human cost of technological warfare.
questions.sourceIssue: Stimulus image was reused by a different released Regents source; hidden until re-imported from the original PDF.

questions.subject: Global History
questions.stem: For which purpose could this passage best be used?

choices.label: A
choices.text: researching life in Phnom Penh before the arrival of the Khmer Rouge

choices.label: B
choices.text: learning about the reasons for the rise of the Khmer Rouge

choices.label: C
choices.text: understanding the impacts of Khmer Rouge policies on Cambodian society

choices.label: D
choices.text: explaining the contributions of the ethnic minorities within Cambodia

questions.explanation: The passage provides a firsthand account of the Khmer Rouge's brutal policies in Cambodia (1975–1979) — forced evacuation of cities, the killing of intellectuals and 'enemies of the revolution,' forced agricultural labor, and the destruction of currency and religion. Pol Pot's 'Year Zero' ideology rejected all pre-revolutionary society and killed approximately 25% of Cambodia's population. This passage would be most useful for understanding the scope and nature of Khmer Rouge policies and their impact on Cambodian society, a case study in totalitarian genocide.
questions.sourceIssue: Stimulus image was reused by a different released Regents source; hidden until re-imported from the original PDF.

questions.subject: Global History
questions.stem: Which claim is best supported by the information provided in this illustration?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Violence in South Africa led to injury and death, contributing to a revolution.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Economic sanctions by international powers led to political and economic hardships for many South Africans.

choices.label: C
choices.text: The opening of South African schools to Black children created an increase in national literacy rates.

choices.label: D
choices.text: The end of apartheid allowed Black South Africans to participate in national elections for the first time.

questions.explanation: The 1994 South African elections — the first in which all South Africans regardless of race could vote — resulted in Nelson Mandela's election as president with 62.6% of the vote, allowing Black South Africans for the first time to participate equally in their country's political process after 46 years of apartheid disenfranchisement. The illustration likely shows multiracial voting lines or Mandela's inauguration. The Enduring Issue is how democratic participation is the foundation of human rights; the 1994 elections are a key turning point in South African and global human rights history.

questions.subject: Global History
questions.stem: This speech is most closely associated with the end of which international conflict?

choices.label: A
choices.text: the Cold War

choices.label: B
choices.text: the Korean War

choices.label: C
choices.text: the Vietnam War

choices.label: D
choices.text: the Persian Gulf War

questions.explanation: The speech — likely Gorbachev's 1988 UN address, the Malta Summit communiqué (1989), or Reagan's 'tear down this wall' speech (1987) — signaled the approaching end of the Cold War by acknowledging that the ideological confrontation between capitalism and communism no longer required military standoff or ideological purity from aligned states. The Cold War ended not with a bang but through a series of diplomatic statements, falling walls, and peaceful revolutions in Eastern Europe. The Regents treats Cold War's end as a turning point requiring students to identify primary sources from 1987–1991.

questions.subject: Global History
questions.stem: Which claim about Latin America is best supported by the information on this map?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Most independence movements occurred in the 20th century.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Most countries gained independence before the Haitian Revolution.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Nationalism became a growing force during the 19th century.

choices.label: D
choices.text: European countries had control over most of the region until World War I.

questions.explanation: The map shows Latin American countries gaining independence from Spain and Portugal in the early 19th century (1810–1830), directly inspired by the American Revolution (1776), French Revolution (1789), and Enlightenment ideology promoting national self-determination. Simón Bolívar, José de San Martín, and Father Hidalgo led nationalist liberation movements across the continent. The Regents treats Latin American independence as a wave of nationalist movements driven by Creole elites using Enlightenment justifications for revolution against colonial rule.

questions.subject: Global History
questions.stem: As shown in Document B, Mandela and de Klerk are being honored for agreeing to establish a political system that

choices.label: A
choices.text: provided universal suffrage

choices.label: B
choices.text: nationalized all the major industries

choices.label: C
choices.text: required complete separation of the races

choices.label: D
choices.text: allowed only a single political party

questions.explanation: Nelson Mandela and F.W. de Klerk jointly received the 1993 Nobel Peace Prize for negotiating South Africa's peaceful transition from apartheid to democracy — specifically for agreeing to end white minority rule through universal suffrage rather than civil war. The new constitution (1994) provided one person, one vote to all South Africans regardless of race, making the 1994 election the first true democratic election in South African history. The Regents treats Mandela-de Klerk negotiations as a model for peaceful conflict resolution and institutional democratization.

questions.subject: Global History
questions.stem: Which generalization about the Cold War is best supported by these headlines?

choices.label: A
choices.text: It impacted various countries and regions throughout the world.

choices.label: B
choices.text: It led to direct military conflict between the U.S. and the Soviet Union.

choices.label: C
choices.text: It prevented outbreaks of violence.

choices.label: D
choices.text: It required intervention by the United Nations.

questions.explanation: The Cold War (1947–1991) was a truly global conflict: nuclear standoff in Europe (Berlin Blockade, NATO vs. Warsaw Pact), proxy wars in Asia (Korea, Vietnam, Afghanistan), Latin America (Cuba, Nicaragua, Chile), Africa (Angola, Ethiopia), and the Middle East (Arab-Israeli conflict). The headlines from multiple continents confirm that no region was untouched by superpower competition, whether through direct intervention, arms sales, economic pressure, or ideological influence. The Enduring Issue is how great-power rivalry shapes outcomes in smaller, weaker nations worldwide.

questions.subject: Global History
questions.stem: Which statement best represents a response taken by the Third Estate to the situation shown in the cartoon?

choices.label: A
choices.text: They appointed Napoleon as head of the government.

choices.label: B
choices.text: They signed the Versailles Treaty.

choices.label: C
choices.text: They declared themselves the National Assembly.

choices.label: D
choices.text: They ended the Reign of Terror.

questions.explanation: In June 1789, frustrated by deadlock in the Estates-General where the First and Second Estates blocked voting by head (which would give the Third Estate majority power), the Third Estate's deputies declared themselves the National Assembly — claiming they represented the French nation and had the right to write a constitution. This act directly triggered the French Revolution's constitutional phase and culminated in the Tennis Court Oath (June 20, 1789). The Enduring Issue is how political exclusion and Enlightenment ideas about popular sovereignty produce revolutionary action.

questions.subject: Global History
questions.stem: Which situation was the result of the situation depicted in this cartoon?

choices.label: A
choices.text: King Louis XVI was restored as absolute monarch.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Napoleon rose to power and reduced chaos.

choices.label: C
choices.text: The Catholic Church became more powerful.

choices.label: D
choices.text: France’s Estates system was reinstated.

questions.explanation: The French Revolution's radical Reign of Terror (1793–94) created political chaos, mass executions, and governmental instability that Napoleon Bonaparte exploited — using his military reputation from Italian and Egyptian campaigns — to seize power in the coup of 18 Brumaire (November 9, 1799). Napoleon ended revolutionary disorder by establishing the Consulate and later the Empire (1804), providing stable authoritarian government while preserving some revolutionary gains (Napoleonic Code, religious tolerance). Students should note that Napoleon both embodied and betrayed the Revolution's ideals.

questions.subject: Global History
questions.stem: The actions taken by Gandhi and his followers, as described in this excerpt, are examples of

choices.label: A
choices.text: nonviolent resistance

choices.label: B
choices.text: economic terrorism

choices.label: C
choices.text: collective bargaining

choices.label: D
choices.text: political espionage

questions.explanation: Gandhi's nonviolent resistance (satyagraha — truth-force or soul-force) involved deliberate, peaceful violation of unjust laws, public demonstrations, hunger strikes, and mass civil disobedience to expose British imperialism's moral contradictions and generate political pressure for Indian independence. The Salt March (1930), the Quit India Movement (1942), and strategic fasting campaigns exemplify this philosophy. Gandhi's methods directly influenced the American civil rights movement (Martin Luther King Jr.), South African anti-apartheid activists, and the global human rights movement.

questions.subject: Global History
questions.stem: What was a major result of the situation depicted in this cartoon?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The French Whites in Haiti formed a new government that abolished slavery.

choices.label: B
choices.text: The French monarchy granted rights to Haiti without any blood being shed.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Napoleon formed an alliance with Toussaint L’Ouverture to jointly rule Haiti.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Toussaint L’Ouverture led a revolution to win independence for the people of Haiti.

questions.explanation: The cartoon depicts Haitian slaves under French colonial rule being brutalized, which directly led to the Haitian Revolution (1791–1804) — the only successful slave revolt in history, led by Toussaint L'Ouverture and later Jean-Jacques Dessalines. Haiti declared independence on January 1, 1804, becoming the first Black republic and the second independent nation in the Western Hemisphere. The Haitian Revolution is the Regents' most tested example of how colonial oppression generates revolutionary resistance, and its success influenced enslaved people throughout the Americas.

questions.subject: Global History
questions.stem: The policies described by Mikhail Gorbachev in this speech refer to

choices.label: A
choices.text: glasnost

choices.label: B
choices.text: nuclear proliferation

choices.label: C
choices.text: détente

choices.label: D
choices.text: nonalignment

questions.explanation: Mikhail Gorbachev's glasnost ('openness') policy, announced in 1986 and implemented from 1987, allowed unprecedented press freedom, public criticism of the Soviet government, rehabilitation of political prisoners, and release of long-suppressed information about Stalin's crimes. Glasnost was paired with perestroika ('restructuring') for economic reform. Together, these policies opened political space that Gorbachev could not control, enabling the nationalist movements and democratic demands that ultimately dissolved the Soviet Union. Students confuse glasnost (political openness) with perestroika (economic restructuring).

questions.subject: Global History
questions.stem: Which issue is most closely associated with the concerns raised in this passage?

choices.label: A
choices.text: migration

choices.label: B
choices.text: drought

choices.label: C
choices.text: widespread famine

choices.label: D
choices.text: climate change

questions.explanation: Climate change — driven by greenhouse gas emissions from fossil fuel combustion, deforestation, and industrial agriculture — raises global temperatures, causing more frequent extreme weather events, rising sea levels, ocean acidification, glacier retreat, and biodiversity loss. The passage likely cites scientific consensus data about rising CO2 levels or specific climate impacts. The Enduring Issue is technology and the environment: industrial development that lifted billions from poverty is simultaneously threatening the ecological systems that support human civilization.

questions.subject: Global History
questions.stem: The purpose of Gandhi’s letter was to

choices.label: A
choices.text: advocate for British control over salt production

choices.label: B
choices.text: inform the British viceroy that his home is considered private property

choices.label: C
choices.text: explain how religion has influenced his fight for independence against Britain

choices.label: D
choices.text: notify the British government of his plan to continue his nationalist efforts

questions.explanation: Gandhi wrote to Viceroy Lord Irwin in March 1930 notifying him of his planned Salt March, explaining his specific grievances against the salt tax and warning of civil disobedience — giving the British government formal notice before taking action, consistent with satyagraha's principle of transparency and willingness to accept legal consequences. The letter exemplifies Gandhi's strategy of moral confrontation: exposing imperial injustice publicly and forcing the British to choose between negotiation and violent suppression of peaceful protesters. The Regents tests this as a primary source for understanding Gandhi's independence strategy.

questions.subject: Global History
questions.stem: Which historical situation is most similar to the one described in this passage?

choices.label: A
choices.text: the implementation of an alternative attendance system in Tokugawa Japan

choices.label: B
choices.text: the emancipation of serfs in Russia under Catherine the Great

choices.label: C
choices.text: the mobilization of the Red Guard to remove corrupt offi cials under Mao Zedong

choices.label: D
choices.text: the rebellions in Cuba led by Fidel Castro

questions.explanation: The passage describes Japan's alternative attendance system (sankin-kōtai), the Tokugawa Shogunate's requirement that feudal lords (daimyo) alternate residence between their domains and Edo (Tokyo), effectively keeping their families as hostages in the capital. This controlled political decentralization by ensuring daimyo spent half their wealth on travel and could not accumulate resources to challenge the shogun. Students draw connections between this control mechanism and other systems of political loyalty management, such as European absolutist courts requiring nobles to reside at Versailles.

questions.subject: Global History
questions.stem: According to Elizabeth Robles Ortega, what is the basic cause of the problems described?

choices.label: A
choices.text: NAFTA’s demand for increased spending on health care

choices.label: B
choices.text: the Mexican government’s focus on obtaining foreign capital

choices.label: C
choices.text: NAFTA’s tight regulations on the environment

choices.label: D
choices.text: the Mexican government’s desire to centralize power

questions.explanation: Elizabeth Robles Ortega argues that Mexico's economic problems stem from government prioritization of attracting foreign capital investment over addressing domestic inequality, unemployment, and the interests of Mexican workers and farmers — a critique of neoliberal globalization policies that opened Mexico's economy through NAFTA (1994) while weakening labor protections and displacing small farmers. The Enduring Issue is economic systems and whether free-market globalization serves national development or primarily benefits multinational corporations and elites.

questions.subject: Global History
questions.stem: Based on this passage, which statement best describes the author’s point of view?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Climate change leads to numerous problems.

choices.label: B
choices.text: There is no need to track people who have moved as a result of climate change.

choices.label: C
choices.text: The number of climate refugees is rapidly decreasing.

choices.label: D
choices.text: International organizations have suffi ciently addressed changes to the climate.

questions.explanation: Climate change, driven by accelerating greenhouse gas emissions from industrial activity since the mid-20th century, causes coral reef bleaching, permafrost melting, intensified hurricanes, prolonged droughts, flooding of coastal cities, and mass species extinction. The author's point of view is that these interconnected problems constitute a global crisis requiring immediate collective action. The Enduring Issue is humanity's relationship to the natural environment, a theme connecting from the Columbian Exchange's ecological disruption to today's climate emergency.

questions.subject: Global History
questions.stem: What was a short-term effect of the uprising described in this passage?

choices.label: A
choices.text: creation of the independent nation of Haiti

choices.label: B
choices.text: introduction of women’s suffrage across Haiti

choices.label: C
choices.text: emergence of socialist labor reform move- ments in Latin America

choices.label: D
choices.text: election of Napoleon Bonaparte as First Consul in France

questions.explanation: The Haitian Revolution (1791–1804), sparked by Boukman's Bois Caïman ceremony in August 1791 and culminating under Jean-Jacques Dessalines, resulted in the creation of Haiti on January 1, 1804 — the Western Hemisphere's first Black republic and the world's only successful slave revolt. The short-term effect was immediate independence from France after 13 years of brutal warfare. The Regents treats the Haitian Revolution as a turning point demonstrating that enslaved people could successfully resist colonial power, though Haiti's independence was followed by punishing isolation and reparations demands from France.

questions.subject: Global History
questions.stem: What was one result of this speech?

choices.label: A
choices.text: the annexation of Laos and Cambodia

choices.label: B
choices.text: a period of isolation

choices.label: C
choices.text: decades of conflict with foreign powers

choices.label: D
choices.text: attacks by North Korea

questions.explanation: The speech — likely Mao Zedong's 1949 proclamation of the People's Republic of China or Khomeini's 1979 declaration of the Islamic Republic — announced a revolutionary political transformation that other states (US, Western powers, regional neighbors) refused to accept, leading to decades of diplomatic isolation, military confrontation (Korea, Iraq-Iran War), economic sanctions, and proxy conflicts. Both communist China and theocratic Iran faced sustained foreign pressure that shaped their internal politics. Regents CRQs test how revolutionary states' relations with the international community unfold over time.

questions.subject: Global History
questions.stem: Based on this illustration, which claim about the formation of the German Empire is most accurate?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Bismarck had a minor role in German unification.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Conflict played a key role in unifying Germanic territories.

choices.label: C
choices.text: The German Empire was created through a series of public referendums.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Bismarck followed a strict policy of pacifism in international relations.

questions.explanation: The illustration depicts the three Wars of German Unification: the Danish War (1864), Austro-Prussian War (1866), and Franco-Prussian War (1870–71), orchestrated by Prussian Chancellor Otto von Bismarck using 'blood and iron' realpolitik to unite the 39 German states under Prussian leadership. Bismarck deliberately provoked these conflicts to create nationalist fervor and eliminate rival powers from German affairs. German unification in January 1871 (proclaimed at Versailles) created the most powerful state in continental Europe and shifted the balance of power that led to WWI.

questions.subject: Global History
questions.stem: The situation illustrated by this map was resolved when

choices.label: A
choices.text: Winston Churchill delivered the “Iron Curtain” speech

choices.label: B
choices.text: the United Nations negotiated on behalf of East Germany

choices.label: C
choices.text: Joseph Stalin ordered the end of the blockade

choices.label: D
choices.text: the North Atlantic Treaty Organization (NATO) threatened to use atomic weapons

questions.explanation: The map depicts the Berlin Blockade (June 1948–May 1949), when Stalin cut off all land and water access to West Berlin to force the Western Allies to abandon the city. The US and Britain responded with the Berlin Airlift, flying over 200,000 flights supplying West Berlin with food, fuel, and supplies. Stalin ended the blockade on May 12, 1949 after 11 months without achieving his goal — a key early Cold War US victory demonstrating resolve to defend Western Europe. Students must distinguish who imposed (Stalin/USSR) and who ended (Stalin's order) the blockade.

questions.subject: Global History
questions.stem: Based on this passage, what issue was debated but not changed during the reign of Catherine the Great?

choices.label: A
choices.text: emancipating the serfs

choices.label: B
choices.text: withholding pay from the nobility

choices.label: C
choices.text: abolishing noble titles

choices.label: D
choices.text: increasing royal authority

questions.explanation: The passage refers to debates about serf emancipation during the reign of Tsar Nicholas I (1825–1855) or Alexander II, who ultimately emancipated Russia's approximately 23 million serfs in 1861. Nicholas I acknowledged the problem but resisted reform, fearing serf uprisings if freedoms were granted too quickly or noble resistance if property was redistributed. This reflects the Enduring Issue of political systems resisting necessary reform until forced by crisis — Russia's embarrassing defeat in the Crimean War (1853–56) finally compelled Alexander II to act.

questions.subject: Global History
questions.stem: Which leader used similar methods as those described in this passage?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Ho Chi Minh

choices.label: B
choices.text: Mao Zedong

choices.label: C
choices.text: Mohandas Gandhi

choices.label: D
choices.text: Ayatollah Khomeini

questions.explanation: The passage likely describes Nelson Mandela's or a civil rights leader's nonviolent strategy — peaceful protest, legal challenge, economic boycott, and moral persuasion — methods directly paralleling Gandhi's satyagraha. Gandhi's influence on anti-colonial and civil rights movements worldwide is a standard Regents connection; students are expected to identify shared methodologies across different geographic and historical contexts. Martin Luther King Jr., César Chávez, and Mandela all explicitly credited Gandhi's philosophy as their inspiration.

questions.subject: Global History
questions.stem: This chart would be most useful to a person or group advocating for

choices.label: A
choices.text: free trade laws

choices.label: B
choices.text: policies to slow climate change

choices.label: C
choices.text: collective security agreements

choices.label: D
choices.text: health and education funding

questions.explanation: The chart likely compares military spending versus social spending (health, education) in developing countries, demonstrating that nations investing heavily in military budgets while underfunding education and healthcare experience worse human development outcomes. Advocates for health and education funding would use this chart to argue that redirecting resources from military to social programs improves long-term national welfare and economic productivity. The Enduring Issue is economic systems and allocation of resources in developing countries, particularly those with histories of colonial underdevelopment.

questions.subject: Global History
questions.stem: What was one way the international community responded to the changes described in this passage?

choices.label: A
choices.text: promoting the use of fossil fuels

choices.label: B
choices.text: signing agreements such as the Kyoto Protocol

choices.label: C
choices.text: discouraging the use of renewable energy resources

choices.label: D
choices.text: dismantling of the World Trade Organization (WTO)

questions.explanation: The Kyoto Protocol (1997), adopted under the UN Framework Convention on Climate Change, was the first legally binding international agreement requiring industrialized nations to reduce greenhouse gas emissions by specified targets. While the US signed but did not ratify, over 190 nations participated, establishing the framework for subsequent climate agreements including the Paris Agreement (2015). The Enduring Issue is how nations cooperate to address global environmental challenges that transcend national borders — a test of international governance and collective action.

questions.subject: Global History
questions.stem: Based on this passage, UNICEF would most likely support which course of action in the developing world?

choices.label: A
choices.text: decreasing the role of non-governmental organizations in providing economic assistance to countries in need

choices.label: B
choices.text: establishing alternative forms of energy to combat global warming

choices.label: C
choices.text: encouraging communities to promote traditional values and beliefs

choices.label: D
choices.text: providing education programs for women

questions.explanation: UNICEF (United Nations Children's Fund) research demonstrates that educating women and girls produces some of the highest returns in development: educated mothers have lower child mortality rates, smaller families, higher household incomes, and better-nourished children. The passage argues that female education in developing countries reduces poverty, improves public health, and promotes economic growth. The Enduring Issue is how social investment in education — particularly for historically marginalized groups — addresses cycles of poverty and underdevelopment.

questions.subject: Global History
questions.stem: The ideas expressed in this passage are most similar to the ideas of

choices.label: A
choices.text: Deng Xiaoping

choices.label: B
choices.text: Simón Bolívar

choices.label: C
choices.text: Mary Wollstonecraft

choices.label: D
choices.text: Winston Churchill

questions.explanation: The passage likely expresses anti-colonial nationalist arguments for independence from European rule — citing natural rights, economic exploitation, and cultural self-determination — mirroring Simón Bolívar's Angostura Address (1819) and his Carta de Jamaica (1815), which argued that Spanish colonial rule violated the natural rights of American-born creoles and that Latin Americans were capable of self-governance. Bolívar drew explicitly on Enlightenment philosophy and the practical examples of the American and French revolutions to justify liberation.

questions.subject: Global History
questions.stem: The behavior criticized in this cartoon is most similar to which concept?

choices.label: A
choices.text: alliances

choices.label: B
choices.text: nuclear proliferation

choices.label: C
choices.text: appeasement

choices.label: D
choices.text: nonalignment

questions.explanation: The cartoon depicts appeasement — the policy of granting concessions to an aggressive power to avoid war — most famously exemplified by the 1938 Munich Agreement, where Britain's Neville Chamberlain and France's Daladier allowed Hitler to annex the Sudetenland in exchange for his pledge of 'peace in our time.' Appeasement failed catastrophically: rather than satisfying Hitler, Munich convinced him the Allies would not fight, enabling the invasion of Czechoslovakia (March 1939) and Poland (September 1939). Students should recognize appeasement cartoons from their 'feeding the crocodile' imagery.

questions.subject: Global History
questions.stem: Which statement best describes this cartoonist’s point of view?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Population rates in Sudan will begin to increase.

choices.label: B
choices.text: United Nations relief efforts in Sudan have been ineffective.

choices.label: C
choices.text: International agreements positively impacted the Sudan.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Sustainable agriculture has been achieved in Sudan.

questions.explanation: The cartoon criticizing UN relief efforts in Sudan (Darfur) argues that international humanitarian intervention through food aid and refugee assistance is ineffective — or even counterproductive — because it fails to address the root causes: the Sudanese government's support for Janjaweed militia attacks on Black African Darfurians in a conflict that killed an estimated 200,000–400,000 people after 2003. The Enduring Issue is the limits of international intervention in humanitarian crises when a nation-state's government is itself perpetrating the violence.

questions.subject: Global History
questions.stem: Which event would later reverse many of the changes described in this passage?

choices.label: A
choices.text: September 11th terrorist attacks

choices.label: B
choices.text: Arab Spring

choices.label: C
choices.text: Iranian Revolution

choices.label: D
choices.text: establishment of the Muslim League

questions.explanation: The passage describes women's rights gains in Iran under Shah Mohammad Reza Pahlavi's White Revolution (1963), which granted women the right to vote (1963), allowed women to run for parliament, and encouraged women's education and workforce participation. The 1979 Islamic Revolution under Ayatollah Khomeini reversed these gains, imposing mandatory hijab, restricting women's rights to divorce and custody, and limiting women's professional opportunities. Students must identify the Shah's modernizing policies as the context that the Islamic Revolution directly reversed.

questions.subject: Global History
questions.stem: Based on this article, what major problem is China attempting to overcome?

choices.label: A
choices.text: territorial debates in the South China Sea

choices.label: B
choices.text: China’s slowing economic growth

choices.label: C
choices.text: China’s growing geopolitical power

choices.label: D
choices.text: border disputes between Vietnam and China

questions.explanation: China's economic growth slowed from double-digit rates in the 2000s to around 6–7% in the 2010s as labor costs rose, export markets saturated, debt increased, and structural inefficiencies in state-owned enterprises limited innovation. The Chinese government has responded by attempting to transition from an export-led manufacturing economy to one driven by domestic consumption and high-technology innovation under the 'Made in China 2025' strategy. The Enduring Issue is economic development transitions and whether authoritarian political systems can manage market economies long-term.

questions.subject: Global History
questions.stem: These stamps were used by the government of Ghana as a way to promote

choices.label: A
choices.text: national pride in a largely illiterate population

choices.label: B
choices.text: British imperialism

choices.label: C
choices.text: involvement in international groups

choices.label: D
choices.text: environmental issues in Ghana

questions.explanation: Ghana's post-independence government under Kwame Nkrumah (1957–1966) used postage stamps featuring nationalist symbols — the Black Star, Pan-African colors, images of Nkrumah himself — as accessible visual media to promote national identity, Pan-African solidarity, and pride in independence among a predominantly illiterate population. Postage stamps are a widely used tool of nationalist propaganda because every piece of mail carries the state's symbols into households. This reflects the Enduring Issue of how newly independent states build national identity after colonial rule.
questions.sourceIssue: Stimulus image was reused by a different released Regents source; hidden until re-imported from the original PDF.

questions.subject: Global History
questions.stem: Which claim is best supported by this passage?

choices.label: A
choices.text: England has many economic advantages because of its island location.

choices.label: B
choices.text: The proximity of England to Germany makes it vulnerable to invasion.

choices.label: C
choices.text: There are many diverse cultures in England because it is near the Atlantic Ocean.

choices.label: D
choices.text: England is isolated from overseas trade networks.

questions.explanation: Britain's geographic position as an island protected from continental land invasions, combined with its access to coastal trade routes, natural harbors, and proximity to Atlantic shipping lanes, gave it enormous economic advantages for maritime trade. Island geography facilitated the Royal Navy's dominance, enabled the development of global trading networks, and reduced defensive military spending compared to continental European powers. The Enduring Issue is how geography shapes economic development — a theme connecting to Britain's early industrialization and imperial expansion.
questions.sourceIssue: Stimulus image was reused by a different released Regents source; hidden until re-imported from the original PDF.

questions.subject: Global History
questions.stem: Which statement best describes the difference between child labor laws and enforcement as described in this passage?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Most countries have no laws to protect children from dangerous labor situations.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Child labor laws are changing to ensure children are earning equal pay for equal work.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Unless people take action, little will change regarding child labor abuses.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Most employers are always punished for breaking child labor laws.

questions.explanation: The passage argues that the existence of child labor laws alone is insufficient without active enforcement mechanisms, international trade pressure, consumer boycotts, and cultural change to actually protect children from exploitation. Many developing countries have formal legal protections against child labor that are routinely violated due to poverty, corruption, and lack of enforcement capacity. The Enduring Issue is human rights: formal legal frameworks mean little without institutional commitment to enforcement, particularly when economic pressures incentivize violations.

questions.subject: Global History
questions.stem: In which way have many countries joined together to address the problems described in this passage?

choices.label: A
choices.text: supporting the exportation of surplus corn and soy

choices.label: B
choices.text: genetically modifying crops to increase production

choices.label: C
choices.text: signing international environmental agreements

choices.label: D
choices.text: increasing the amount of land under cultivation

questions.explanation: International environmental agreements — the Montreal Protocol (1987, ozone layer), Rio Earth Summit (1992), Kyoto Protocol (1997, greenhouse gases), and Paris Agreement (2015) — represent coordinated multinational responses to environmental problems that cross national boundaries and cannot be solved by any single state acting alone. The Enduring Issue is how nations overcome national self-interest to cooperate on shared global challenges. Students should know the Paris Agreement (2015) as the current framework requiring all nations to set national emissions reduction targets.

questions.subject: Global History
questions.stem: Which organization directly helped to end the human rights violation shown in this cartoon?

choices.label: A
choices.text: NATO – North Atlantic Treaty Organization

choices.label: B
choices.text: OAU – Organization of African Unity

choices.label: C
choices.text: OPEC – Organization of the Petroleum Exporting Countries

choices.label: D
choices.text: WTO – World Trade Organization

questions.explanation: NATO intervened militarily in Kosovo (1999) to stop Serbian ethnic cleansing of Kosovar Albanians in the former Yugoslavia — airstrikes against Serbian military targets that forced Serbian withdrawal and enabled the establishment of a UN-administered Kosovo. NATO's Kosovo intervention bypassed the UN Security Council (where Russia would have vetoed), making it controversial but ultimately credited with stopping the ethnic cleansing. Students should recognize NATO as both a Cold War defense alliance and a post-Cold War humanitarian intervention force.

questions.subject: Global History
questions.stem: Which claim is best supported by the evidence in this passage?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Freedom of dress was allowed in schools.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Reforms were not universally accepted.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Respect was given to middle-class women.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Clothing styles were considered unimportant.

questions.explanation: The passage about resistance to reforms — likely relating to Ottoman Tanzimat reforms (1839–1876), Peter the Great's Westernization, or Atatürk's secularization — demonstrates that modernizing reforms imposed from above generate opposition from traditional elites, religious authorities, or ordinary people whose livelihoods or identities are threatened. The Enduring Issue is political change and resistance: even beneficial reforms provoke backlash when they challenge established power structures or cultural practices. Regents CRQs use these documents to test cause-and-effect analysis of reform and reaction.

questions.subject: Global History
questions.stem: Based on this cartoon, which statement best describes the cartoonist’s point of view?

choices.label: A
choices.text: European peace relied on collective security.

choices.label: B
choices.text: European militarism prevented conflict.

choices.label: C
choices.text: A system of European alliances caused the escalation of tension in Europe.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Serbia was able to avoid getting involved with other European countries.

questions.explanation: The cartoon illustrates how Europe's interlocking alliance system — the Triple Alliance (Germany, Austria-Hungary, Italy) vs. the Triple Entente (France, Russia, Britain) — transformed the assassination of Archduke Franz Ferdinand in Sarajevo on June 28, 1914 from a regional dispute into a world war through automatic mobilization commitments. Each country's declaration of war pulled in treaty partners in a chain reaction within five weeks. Students must understand that alliances were a cause of WWI's rapid escalation, not simply a background condition.

questions.subject: Global History
questions.stem: What point of view is depicted in this cartoon?

choices.label: A
choices.text: China was looking for excuses to strengthen the wall.

choices.label: B
choices.text: China viewed democracy as an attractive import.

choices.label: C
choices.text: China is not participating in the global trade market at this time.

choices.label: D
choices.text: China has attempted to prevent democratic ideas from entering the country.

questions.explanation: The cartoon depicts China's 'Great Firewall' — the Chinese government's internet censorship system that blocks Google, Facebook, Twitter, Wikipedia, and news sites critical of the Communist Party, preventing Chinese citizens from accessing information about Tiananmen Square (1989), Xinjiang detention camps, Tibet, and Taiwan. This represents the Enduring Issue of political repression versus democratic ideals: China pursues economic openness while maintaining rigid information control to prevent democratic ideas from challenging CCP authority.

questions.subject: Global History
questions.stem: This timeline would be most useful to a person attempting to prove

choices.label: A
choices.text: how effective the work of the United Nations has been in addressing epidemic disease outbreaks over time

choices.label: B
choices.text: the range of United Nations efforts maintaining peace and providing humanitarian aid

choices.label: C
choices.text: the extent to which environmental policy has been supported by member states of the United Nations

choices.label: D
choices.text: the degree to which the United Nations has been able to promote women’s rights in developing regions

questions.explanation: The United Nations (founded 1945) conducts peacekeeping operations, humanitarian aid, refugee assistance, sanctions enforcement, and arms reduction negotiations across dozens of countries through agencies including the UNHCR, WFP, UNICEF, and the Security Council. The timeline demonstrates the breadth of UN activities from Korea (1950) through Rwanda (1994) to Kosovo (1999) and beyond. Students should recognize the UN's multiple roles and also its limitations when permanent Security Council members (US, Russia, China) veto action in their spheres of interest.
questions.sourceIssue: Stimulus image was reused by a different released Regents source; hidden until re-imported from the original PDF.

questions.subject: Global History
questions.stem: Which course of action did the British take in response to the situation described in this passage?

choices.label: A
choices.text: expanded British territorial control within the Indian subcontinent

choices.label: B
choices.text: moved the monarch’s headquarters to New Delhi

choices.label: C
choices.text: propped up the Mughal emperor’s government with troops and cash

choices.label: D
choices.text: withdrew all British government representa- tives from the Mughal Empire

questions.explanation: The British response to Indian resistance — whether the 1857 Sepoy Mutiny/Rebellion or Gandhi's independence movements — was typically to expand direct British governmental control rather than negotiate. After the 1857 rebellion, Britain abolished the British East India Company and established direct Crown rule (British Raj, 1858), significantly expanding administrative and military control. This pattern of colonial powers tightening control in response to resistance is the Enduring Issue of imperialism: resistance is met with intensified domination rather than reform.
questions.sourceIssue: Stimulus image was reused by a different released Regents source; hidden until re-imported from the original PDF.

questions.subject: Global History
questions.stem: What was a direct outcome of the event in this illustration?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The League of Nations was created to settle conflicts.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Austria-Hungary expanded its empire.

choices.label: C
choices.text: East Germany was occupied by the Soviets.

choices.label: D
choices.text: The Triple Entente formed to offset concerns about the Triple Alliance.

questions.explanation: WWI's catastrophic destruction — 17 million dead, 4 empires collapsed, economies ruined — created powerful international will to establish mechanisms preventing future wars, resulting in the League of Nations created by the Treaty of Versailles (1919) at Wilson's insistence. The League's covenant committed members to collective security, arbitration of disputes, and economic sanctions against aggressors. Its ultimate failure — the US never joined, Germany and USSR were excluded initially, and it could not stop Axis aggression in the 1930s — is the Regents' lesson about why the UN was built differently after WWII.

questions.subject: Global History
questions.stem: The information in this passage would be most useful to an economist researching

choices.label: A
choices.text: employment strategies used in Europe

choices.label: B
choices.text: importation of European raw materials to Africa

choices.label: C
choices.text: motivations for European imperialism in Africa

choices.label: D
choices.text: reasons for European interest in ivory

questions.explanation: The passage describing Africa's raw materials, agricultural potential, and labor resources would be most useful to an economist researching the economic motivations for European imperialism — specifically the need for raw materials (rubber, cotton, diamonds, gold, palm oil) for European industries and for markets to sell manufactured goods. European powers viewed Africa as both a resource extraction zone and a potential consumer market, reflecting the late 19th-century economic imperialism that drove the Scramble for Africa after the Berlin Conference (1884–85).

questions.subject: Global History
questions.stem: The author’s purpose in writing this passage was to

choices.label: A
choices.text: describe the causes of the Ottomans’ military success

choices.label: B
choices.text: demonstrate the advantages of state-sponsored religions

choices.label: C
choices.text: illustrate the importance of technology to an empire’s development

choices.label: D
choices.text: explain the impact of strategic location on the Ottoman Empire

questions.explanation: The Ottoman Empire's strategic location controlling the Bosphorus and Dardanelles straits connecting the Black Sea to the Mediterranean, its position at the crossroads of Europe, Asia, and Africa, and its control of key land and sea trade routes between East and West gave it enormous geopolitical leverage but also made it a target for European imperial ambitions. Geography shaped the Ottoman Empire's centuries-long ability to extract revenue from trade and also explains why European powers competed to control Ottoman territories as the empire declined. The Enduring Issue is geography as a determinant of power.

questions.subject: Global History
questions.stem: The author interprets Kenyatta’s discussion of the Kenya African Union flag as a call for

choices.label: A
choices.text: violent resistance against colonial authority

choices.label: B
choices.text: a peaceful transfer of political power to Africans

choices.label: C
choices.text: a new monotheistic religious movement

choices.label: D
choices.text: removal of restriction on political speech

questions.explanation: Jomo Kenyatta, leader of the Kenya African Union and later Kenya's first president, used nationalist symbolism — the KAU flag featuring the colors of the Maasai shield — to argue for Kenyan self-determination and resistance to British colonial rule. The author's interpretation that this symbolism signals willingness to use violent resistance reflects the Mau Mau uprising context (1952–1960), in which Kenyan fighters violently opposed British rule. Students should distinguish peaceful nationalist organizing (KAU's legal activities) from the armed Mau Mau resistance that developed in parallel.

questions.subject: Global History
questions.stem: Nehru’s views in this passage can best be described as

choices.label: A
choices.text: militarist

choices.label: B
choices.text: nationalist

choices.label: C
choices.text: communist

choices.label: D
choices.text: imperialist

questions.explanation: Jawaharlal Nehru's vision for India was deeply nationalist — committed to Indian sovereignty, self-determination, and independence from British imperialism — but also modernist, secular, and socialist in economic orientation. His 1947 'Tryst with Destiny' speech proclaimed Indian independence as a fulfillment of long-frustrated national aspirations. Nehru as a nationalist leader is contrasted on the Regents with Gandhi (who combined nationalism with religious traditionalism) and Jinnah (whose nationalism was specifically Muslim-communal rather than pan-Indian).

questions.subject: Global History
questions.stem: Which statement best represents the cartoonist’s point of view?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The nation of Italy was not guilty of any crimes in Europe.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Germany is best positioned to revive peace in Europe.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Working together will bring peace back to Europe.

choices.label: D
choices.text: There is plenty of blame to go around for the death of peace in Europe.

questions.explanation: The cartoon depicts multiple parties bearing responsibility for WWI's outbreak — Kaiser Wilhelm II's militarism, the alliance system, imperialist competition, and nationalist fervor all share culpability — rejecting any single-nation blame theory. The Versailles Treaty's 'War Guilt Clause' (Article 231) placing sole blame on Germany was historically controversial and contributed to German resentment that fueled Nazism. The Regents treats WWI causation as multi-causal (MAIN framework), and this cartoon reinforces shared responsibility.

questions.subject: Global History
questions.stem: Which statement best describes this cartoonist’s point of view of the United Nations (UN)?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The United Nations peacekeeping system is broken.

choices.label: B
choices.text: People are safe because of United Nations peacekeeping efforts.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Walls are the most effective United Nations peacekeeping tool.

choices.label: D
choices.text: United Nations peacekeeping efforts are supported by many people.

questions.explanation: The cartoon argues that the UN's peacekeeping system is dysfunctional — unable to prevent genocides (Rwanda 1994), ethnic cleansing (Bosnia 1995), or civil wars (Syria, Yemen) because the Security Council's veto mechanism allows permanent members (US, Russia, China) to block action protecting their allies or interests. The Enduring Issue is the tension between national sovereignty and international responsibility to protect human rights. Students should know the UN's structural limitations (veto power, reliance on member-state troops) that hamper effective peacekeeping.

questions.subject: Global History
questions.stem: What is a direct cause of the climate issues described in this passage?

choices.label: A
choices.text: expansion of fresh water resources

choices.label: B
choices.text: increase in greenhouse gas emissions

choices.label: C
choices.text: decrease in the volume of global trade

choices.label: D
choices.text: surplus of funding for scientifi c research

questions.explanation: Greenhouse gases — primarily carbon dioxide from fossil fuel combustion (coal, oil, natural gas), methane from agriculture and landfills, and nitrous oxide from fertilizers — trap heat in the atmosphere through the greenhouse effect, raising global average temperatures. Since industrialization, atmospheric CO2 has risen from approximately 280 ppm to over 420 ppm, driving the climate changes described in the passage. The Enduring Issue is how human economic activity produces environmental consequences that disproportionately harm developing nations and future generations.

questions.subject: Global History
questions.stem: Based on the information shown on this map, what can be inferred from the fact that 42% of imported clothing is made in China?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The Chinese government enforces strict regulations protecting factory workers.

choices.label: B
choices.text: The Chinese government encourages exportation to Central America.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Chinese governmental policies support manufacturing for export.

choices.label: D
choices.text: China has strong legal measures in place to protect the environment near factories.

questions.explanation: The map showing manufacturing clusters concentrated in China's coastal Special Economic Zones and interior industrial centers reflects Deng Xiaoping's export-oriented development strategy, in which China became 'the world's factory' by leveraging cheap labor, government investment, and infrastructure to produce goods for export to wealthy consumer markets. Chinese government policies — currency management, subsidized energy, export tax rebates — intentionally supported manufacturing growth as the engine of national development. This reflects economic globalization's concentration of manufacturing in low-wage, high-productivity economies.

questions.subject: Global History
questions.stem: A historian would find this passage useful for understanding how the

choices.label: A
choices.text: gap between the wealthy and the poor in Iran decreased as a result of the reforms

choices.label: B
choices.text: role of women in Iranian society was reduced by the Shah

choices.label: C
choices.text: exportation of oil strengthened Iran’s economy

choices.label: D
choices.text: traditional segments of Iran’s population reacted to the Shah’s reforms

questions.explanation: The passage describes the traditional Iranian population's reaction to Shah Mohammed Reza Pahlavi's White Revolution reforms (1963–1977), which rapidly Westernized Iranian society — granting women the vote, redistributing land from religious foundations, limiting clerical power, and encouraging Western cultural practices. Traditional religious Iranians, ulema (clerics), and the rural poor resented these changes as culturally alien impositions, providing the mass base for Ayatollah Khomeini's 1979 Islamic Revolution that overthrew the Shah.

questions.subject: Global History
questions.stem: Based on this passage, what was one cause of the collapse of the Mughal Empire?

choices.label: A
choices.text: a lack of trained military forces to protect against invasions

choices.label: B
choices.text: an entrepreneurial spirit and rapid expansion of trade

choices.label: C
choices.text: poor governing and excessive spending

choices.label: D
choices.text: widespread rebellions by the common people across the empire

questions.explanation: The Mughal Empire (1526–1858) peaked under Akbar (1556–1605) and declined under Aurangzeb (1658–1707), whose policies of excessive religious taxation of Hindus, extravagant military campaigns, and court corruption depleted the treasury, alienated Hindu and Sikh subjects, and weakened central authority. After Aurangzeb, regional governors declared independence, Maratha confederacy expanded, and Nadir Shah of Persia sacked Delhi (1739). British East India Company exploited this fragmentation to extend its control, completing British domination by 1858.

questions.subject: Global History
questions.stem: Which statement best describes the purpose of this document?

choices.label: A
choices.text: to protect individual freedoms

choices.label: B
choices.text: to protect against economic depression

choices.label: C
choices.text: to unite nations under a joint military

choices.label: D
choices.text: to work to resolve environmental problems

questions.explanation: The document is likely the English Bill of Rights (1689), the French Declaration of the Rights of Man (1789), or the Universal Declaration of Human Rights (1948) — all designed with the primary purpose of protecting individuals from arbitrary government power by codifying specific rights and freedoms. The Enduring Issue is how documents establish legal protections for individual liberty against state power. Students should be able to distinguish these three foundational human rights documents by their historical context and specific provisions.

questions.subject: Global History
questions.stem: Based on this cartoon, what is one function of the International Monetary Fund (IMF)?

choices.label: A
choices.text: to establish guidelines for nations that are asking for loans

choices.label: B
choices.text: to implement tariffs to protect borrowing nations’ markets

choices.label: C
choices.text: to encourage nations to apply for loans in order to increase their spending

choices.label: D
choices.text: to assist borrowing nations in taking control of businesses

questions.explanation: The International Monetary Fund (IMF), established at the 1944 Bretton Woods Conference, provides financial assistance to nations facing balance-of-payments crises with conditions attached — typically requiring recipient nations to implement austerity measures (cutting government spending), privatize state enterprises, and liberalize trade. Critics call these 'structural adjustment programs' neo-colonial impositions that force developing nations to adopt free-market policies serving wealthy creditor nations' interests. The cartoon illustrates the IMF as a powerful arbiter with significant leverage over sovereign nations' economic policies.

questions.subject: Global History
questions.stem: In what way did the situations shown in this cartoon significantly affect China?

choices.label: A
choices.text: China was able to defeat Britain and isolate itself.

choices.label: B
choices.text: China was divided into European spheres of influence.

choices.label: C
choices.text: China expanded its territory.

choices.label: D
choices.text: China unified its people and strengthened its imperial court.

questions.explanation: Following China's defeat in the First Sino-Japanese War (1895) and the Boxer Rebellion (1899–1901), European powers and Japan imposed the 'scramble for concessions,' dividing China into exclusive spheres of influence where specific powers controlled railroads, mining rights, and trade. Britain dominated the Yangtze valley, France held southern China, Germany held Shandong, Russia held Manchuria, and Japan held Fujian. This humiliation intensified Chinese nationalism, fueled the 1911 revolution ending the Qing dynasty, and is the backdrop for the May Fourth Movement (1919).

questions.subject: Global History
questions.stem: Which concern facing China’s government in 2000 can best be inferred from this cartoon?

choices.label: A
choices.text: protests against the need to shift from regional self-sufficiency to regional specialization

choices.label: B
choices.text: internal disruptions resulting from rapidly opening economic development zones

choices.label: C
choices.text: negative reactions by citizens to annexing Taiwan into the political bureaucracy

choices.label: D
choices.text: military attacks by Western powers to gain competitive market shares

questions.explanation: The 1989 Tiananmen Square pro-democracy protests — in which students and workers demanded political reform, freedom of the press, and an end to corruption — demonstrated that rapid economic liberalization (Deng's reforms since 1978) generated rising expectations for political freedoms the Communist Party refused to grant. The government's violent suppression revealed the inherent tension in China's model: economic openness without political openness. Regents CRQs use Tiananmen as the key example of China's government choosing authoritarian stability over democratic reform.

questions.subject: Global History
questions.stem: Which policy did Mikhail Gorbachev initiate during his presidency that contributed to the situation described in this excerpt?

choices.label: A
choices.text: glasnost

choices.label: B
choices.text: Russification

choices.label: C
choices.text: nuclear rearmament

choices.label: D
choices.text: collectivization

questions.explanation: Gorbachev's glasnost ('openness') policy, formally announced in 1986, allowed Soviet citizens and journalists to publicly discuss and criticize government policies, acknowledge the failures of the command economy, and access information previously suppressed — including revelations about Stalin's purges and the Chernobyl disaster's true death toll. This transparency destroyed the Soviet system's legitimacy from within: citizens who could now see the gap between official propaganda and reality lost faith in the Communist Party, accelerating the dissolution of the USSR in 1991.

questions.subject: Global History
questions.stem: A historian would find this passage most useful for explaining King Louis XIV’s attempt to

choices.label: A
choices.text: establish trust within the court

choices.label: B
choices.text: control the elite class

choices.label: C
choices.text: provide necessities to be self-sufficient

choices.label: D
choices.text: eliminate taxation on the poorer classes

questions.explanation: Louis XIV deliberately required French nobles to reside at the Palace of Versailles, where their time was consumed with elaborate court ceremonies, fashion competitions, and proximity to royal favor. This strategy kept potentially rebellious aristocrats under constant royal surveillance, financially dependent on royal pensions, and away from their provincial power bases where they might organize resistance. The passage demonstrates Louis XIV's genius for converting the nobility from a political threat into decorative courtiers — a classic example of absolutism's control mechanisms.

questions.subject: Global History
questions.stem: The actions described in this passage are most closely associated with which leader?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Deng Xiaoping

choices.label: B
choices.text: Pol Pot

choices.label: C
choices.text: Sun Yat Sen

choices.label: D
choices.text: Ho Chi Minh

questions.explanation: The actions described — forced urban evacuations, targeting of intellectuals and professionals for execution, abolition of money and religion, and enforced agricultural labor — are the signature policies of Pol Pot's Khmer Rouge regime in Cambodia (1975–1979). Pol Pot sought to create a 'Year Zero' agrarian communist utopia by eliminating all traces of the previous society, killing an estimated 1.5–2 million people (about 25% of Cambodia's population). The Regents treats the Cambodian genocide alongside the Holocaust and Rwanda as key 20th-century mass atrocity case studies.

questions.subject: Global History
questions.stem: Which group in Iran most likely opposed the policies of Shah Pahlavi described in this passage?

choices.label: A
choices.text: college students

choices.label: B
choices.text: religious leaders

choices.label: C
choices.text: merchants

choices.label: D
choices.text: women

questions.explanation: The Shia ulema (Islamic clergy) and traditional religious institutions most strongly opposed Shah Pahlavi's White Revolution reforms (1963–1977), which reduced clerical landholdings through land reform, granted women the right to vote (opposing the clergy's interpretation of Islamic law), established secular courts replacing religious ones, and promoted Western cultural values. Ayatollah Ruhollah Khomeini led clerical opposition, was exiled in 1964, and ultimately directed the 1979 Islamic Revolution that reinstated religious authority over Iranian politics.

questions.subject: Global History
questions.stem: Which policy most directly contributed to the situation shown in this cartoon?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Lenin’s New Economic Policy

choices.label: B
choices.text: Gorbachev’s policy of glasnost

choices.label: C
choices.text: Stalin’s Five-Year Plans

choices.label: D
choices.text: Deng’s Four Modernizations

questions.explanation: Glasnost ('openness') directly enabled the 1989 revolutions in Eastern Europe by allowing independent media, public debate, and frank acknowledgment that communist regimes lacked popular legitimacy. The cartoon likely depicts communist leaders scrambling to maintain power as their populations, emboldened by Gorbachev's relaxation of the Brezhnev Doctrine, openly demanded democratic reforms — leading to the peaceful 'Velvet Revolutions' in Czechoslovakia and Poland, the violent Romanian revolution, and the fall of the Berlin Wall on November 9, 1989.

questions.subject: Global History
questions.stem: A historian would find this passage most useful for understanding the

choices.label: A
choices.text: rules of diplomacy followed by Louis XIV

choices.label: B
choices.text: divine right beliefs of Louis XIV

choices.label: C
choices.text: effectiveness of Louis XIV’s reign

choices.label: D
choices.text: methods Louis XIV used to control the nobility

questions.explanation: Louis XIV's strategy of requiring nobles to reside at Versailles and participate in elaborate court rituals drained aristocratic resources, destroyed their regional political networks, and made them dependent on royal patronage — preventing the kind of noble revolts (Fronde, 1648–1653) that had plagued his early reign. Versailles thus served not merely as a royal palace but as a political weapon of control, ensuring that France's most powerful families competed for the king's attention rather than plotting against him. This is the Regents' primary example of absolutism managing the elite.

questions.subject: Global History
questions.stem: Based on this passage, what conclusion can be made regarding the tensions between tradition and modernity in Iran?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Before the revolution, the Pahlavis encouraged people to stay within traditional Islamic norms.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Ayatollah Khomeini’s revolutionaries favored a more traditional patriarchal society.

choices.label: C
choices.text: The new theocratic government encouraged modernity and the expansion of rights.

choices.label: D
choices.text: After the revolution, movement toward modernity was hindered by limitations placed on males in Iranian society.

questions.explanation: The 1979 Iranian Revolution created tension between Ayatollah Khomeini's vision of an Islamic Republic governed by clerical interpretation of sharia — the wilayat al-faqih (guardianship of the jurist) principle — and secular nationalists and leftists who had also opposed the Shah but wanted a democratic republic. Khomeini's side prevailed, imposing mandatory hijab, Islamic law courts, and restrictions on women and religious minorities. The passage captures this internal revolutionary conflict between traditional Islamist and modernist nationalist visions for post-Shah Iran.

questions.subject: Global History
questions.stem: The reach of Ho Chi Minh’s tentacles in the cartoon suggests that

choices.label: A
choices.text: his goal was to unify Vietnam

choices.label: B
choices.text: his political ambitions included conquering South Vietnam’s neighbors

choices.label: C
choices.text: he was interested in developing a strong naval presence in the region

choices.label: D
choices.text: he sought to cooperate with the United States to gain Chinese support

questions.explanation: The cartoon depicting Ho Chi Minh's tentacles reaching across Vietnam reflects US Cold War fears that North Vietnam's communist government, backed by the Soviet Union and China, sought to reunify Vietnam under communist rule — which would mean 'losing' South Vietnam to the communist bloc. Ho Chi Minh's lifelong goal was Vietnamese national unification, which he framed as completing the anti-colonial liberation struggle begun against France. US containment policy treated this nationalist unification goal as Soviet-directed communist expansion requiring military opposition.

questions.subject: Global History
questions.stem: Based on this passage, which action was being recommended by Ho Chi Minh?

choices.label: A
choices.text: armed rebellion

choices.label: B
choices.text: disarmament

choices.label: C
choices.text: peaceful protests

choices.label: D
choices.text: boycott

questions.explanation: Ho Chi Minh's passage, likely from his writings in the early 1940s during the Viet Minh's formation, argues that French colonial exploitation had left Vietnamese people with no legitimate recourse except armed rebellion — that peaceful petition had been exhausted and that violence was the only means of achieving liberation. This echoes Locke's argument that when a government violates natural rights, the people have the right to revolution. Ho explicitly cited the failure of diplomatic approaches (including his post-WWII letters to Truman) as justification for armed resistance.

questions.subject: Global History
questions.stem: A long-term effect of the reforms described in this passage was

choices.label: A
choices.text: the overthrow of the Shah’s government

choices.label: B
choices.text: the creation of a lasting democracy in Iran

choices.label: C
choices.text: an alliance between the Shah’s government and religious leaders

choices.label: D
choices.text: a period of peace and economic prosperity for all Iranians

questions.explanation: The Shah's White Revolution reforms — land redistribution, women's suffrage, secular education, Western cultural influence — modernized Iran's economy but alienated the traditional religious establishment and created rural-urban inequality. Rather than stabilizing the regime, these rapid changes fueled resentment that Ayatollah Khomeini channeled into the 1979 Islamic Revolution, which overthrew the Shah and established a theocratic republic. The Regents uses this as a case study in how rapid top-down modernization without democratic legitimacy can produce revolutionary backlash.

questions.subject: Global History
questions.stem: What changed in Turkey as a direct result of Atatürk’s efforts?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Communist forces seized power in Turkey.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Turkey adopted a more modern Western orientation.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Turkey’s government was dominated by Islamic clerics.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Secularism in Turkey was universally accepted.

questions.explanation: Atatürk's reforms (1923–1938) transformed Turkey from an Islamic Ottoman empire into a secular Western-oriented republic: abolishing the sultanate and caliphate, replacing Islamic courts with European law codes, introducing the Latin alphabet, requiring Western dress, granting women the vote (1934), and separating mosque from state. These changes aligned Turkey with European institutions and values, setting the foundation for Turkey's later NATO membership (1952) and European Union candidacy. Students should recognize Turkey's Westernization as the most extensive example of top-down secular modernization in Muslim majority societies.

questions.subject: Global History
questions.stem: Based on this illustration, which course of action is being recommended by Mustafa Kemal Atatürk?

choices.label: A
choices.text: strict following of Sharia law

choices.label: B
choices.text: adopting social and political changes

choices.label: C
choices.text: avoiding involvement with foreign powers

choices.label: D
choices.text: promoting traditional and religious roles

questions.explanation: Atatürk's illustration or speech advocates for adopting Western social and political institutions — parliamentary democracy, secular law, women's rights, modern education — rather than clinging to Ottoman religious traditions. His argument is that Turkey's survival as a sovereign nation required competing on equal terms with European powers, which meant adopting their institutional structures. This parallels the Meiji Restoration's adoption of Western institutions and is the Regents' key example of a Muslim-majority leader deliberately separating religion from state governance.

questions.subject: Global History
questions.stem: Which claim is being made by this passage?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Islamic law is the foundation of Turkish society.

choices.label: B
choices.text: The Turkish nation needs to remain isolated to survive.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Turkey has the potential to become a world leader.

choices.label: D
choices.text: A policy of imperialism is essential if Turkey is to grow.

questions.explanation: Atatürk's argument that Turkey could become a world leader rests on the premise that Westernizing reforms — secular governance, modern education, industrialization, women's participation in society — would transform Turkey from the 'Sick Man of Europe' into a competitive modern state. His vision combined nationalist pride with pragmatic adoption of Western institutional models. Regents CRQs use Atatürk's arguments to test students' ability to identify both the goals of modernization and the political ideology (nationalism combined with secularism) driving the reforms.

questions.subject: Global History
questions.stem: What is the central issue described in this article?

choices.label: A
choices.text: conflict over Turkey’s cultural identity

choices.label: B
choices.text: crackdown on protests by Turkish troops

choices.label: C
choices.text: passing aggressive reforms to restore Turkey’s democratic institutions

choices.label: D
choices.text: fighting between Kemal Atatürk and Turkey’s President Erdogan

questions.explanation: Turkey's identity conflict — between Atatürk's secular, Western-oriented republican vision and rising political Islam represented by Erdogan's AKP party since 2002 — reflects the enduring tension between modernization and religious tradition in Muslim-majority societies. Atatürk constitutionally banned religious parties and required secular education; Erdogan has reversed restrictions on religious dress, expanded Islamic education, and repositioned Turkey as a bridge between West and Islamic world. This tension mirrors the broader clash between secularism and religious revivalism across the Middle East.

questions.subject: Global History
questions.stem: Which problem is being described in this article?

choices.label: A
choices.text: insufficient funding for health care workers

choices.label: B
choices.text: a shortage of jobs for clinic technicians

choices.label: C
choices.text: a lack of smartphone access in the developing world

choices.label: D
choices.text: the need for effective testing for diseases

questions.explanation: The article describes the challenge of inadequate medical testing infrastructure for infectious diseases — either HIV/AIDS diagnosis in sub-Saharan Africa, COVID-19 testing limitations, or antibiotic-resistant tuberculosis diagnosis — in developing countries where laboratory capacity, healthcare workers, and disease surveillance systems are insufficient to identify and contain outbreaks. The Enduring Issue is science and technology applied unequally: wealthy nations have diagnostic capabilities that developing nations lack, creating public health vulnerabilities that threaten global populations.

questions.subject: Global History
questions.stem: Which later development was a result of the political situation shown in this cartoon?

choices.label: A
choices.text: European nations united in opposition to both the Soviet Union and the United States.

choices.label: B
choices.text: The United Nations oversaw the destruction of all atomic weapons.

choices.label: C
choices.text: The superpowers maintained total control over nuclear bombs.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Soviets and Americans negotiated treaties to limit nuclear weapons.
