questions.explanation: The Cuban Missile Crisis (October 1962) — thirteen days of nuclear brinkmanship that brought the US and USSR closest to nuclear war — demonstrated the catastrophic danger of unrestricted nuclear competition and directly motivated arms control negotiations. The result was the Limited Nuclear Test Ban Treaty (1963), the Non-Proliferation Treaty (1968), SALT I (1972), and eventually the START treaties. Students should trace the direct causal line from near-nuclear war in 1962 to arms limitation diplomacy in the détente era.

questions.subject: Global History
questions.stem: Which concept is best illustrated by the horse in this 2000 cartoon?

choices.label: A
choices.text: containment

choices.label: B
choices.text: globalization

choices.label: C
choices.text: tolerance

choices.label: D
choices.text: militarism

questions.explanation: The 2000 cartoon horse representing globalization depicts the accelerating economic interconnection of the world through trade liberalization, multinational corporations, internet communication, and financial integration. Globalization in the 1990s–2000s — accelerated by NAFTA (1994), the WTO (1995), and China's WTO accession (2001) — dramatically increased trade volumes, relocated manufacturing to low-wage economies, and created unprecedented economic interdependence. The Enduring Issue is whether globalization's benefits (lower consumer prices, developing-nation growth) outweigh its costs (job displacement, cultural homogenization, inequality).

questions.subject: Global History
questions.stem: According to this population graph, what is the approximate projected world population for 2050?

choices.label: A
choices.text: 9 billion

choices.label: B
choices.text: 3 billion

choices.label: C
choices.text: 1 billion

choices.label: D
choices.text: 10 billion

questions.explanation: UN population projections consistently estimated world population reaching approximately 9 billion people by 2050, driven primarily by population growth in sub-Saharan Africa and South Asia. Current population (2024) stands at approximately 8.1 billion, making 9 billion a realistic mid-century projection. This population growth creates pressure on food systems, freshwater resources, urban infrastructure, and climate systems. The Regents tests this data in connection with Malthusian theory and the Green Revolution's role in preventing the famines Malthus predicted.

questions.subject: Global History
questions.stem: Which statement best describes the author’s purpose in issuing this proclamation?

choices.label: A
choices.text: to begin modernization efforts

choices.label: B
choices.text: to promote trade with foreigners

choices.label: C
choices.text: to gain support for freshwater projects

choices.label: D
choices.text: to encourage anti-western resistance

questions.explanation: The proclamation is most likely by Ayatollah Khomeini (1979 Iran) or Osama bin Laden (1990s al-Qaeda), both of whom framed Western — specifically American — presence, values, and policies in Muslim lands as an existential threat to Islamic civilization requiring armed resistance. Khomeini's anti-Western ideology was driven by US support for the Shah; bin Laden's by US troops in Saudi Arabia and US support for Israel. Both represent a reactionary response to Western cultural and political power using religious nationalism as a mobilizing ideology.

questions.subject: Global History
questions.stem: What was a long-term effect of the situation described in this passage?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Soviet troops liberated the Vietnamese from French rule.

choices.label: B
choices.text: The French agreed to rule northern Vietnam.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Vietnamese resistance led to the establishment of Indochina.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Vietnamese nationalism led to bloody conflict.

questions.explanation: French colonialism in Indochina (1883–1954) imposed economic exploitation, forced labor, cultural suppression, and political exclusion that generated Vietnamese nationalism under Ho Chi Minh's Viet Minh movement. The French Indochina War (1946–1954) ended with France's defeat at Dien Bien Phu (May 1954) and the Geneva Accords dividing Vietnam at the 17th parallel. US intervention to prevent communist unification led to the Vietnam War (1955–1975), which killed approximately 3 million Vietnamese. The Enduring Issue is how colonial oppression generates nationalist resistance that outlasts the colonial power.

questions.subject: Global History
questions.stem: What was one result of the events depicted in this illustration?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The balance of power in Europe shifted.

choices.label: B
choices.text: France and Germany became close allies.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Nationalism contributed to the breakup of the German Empire.

choices.label: D
choices.text: The new German constitution forbade aggressive war.

questions.explanation: The illustration likely depicts the Congress of Vienna (1814–15) or the Paris Peace Conference (1919), both of which reorganized European borders after major conflicts and shifted the balance of power among European states. At Vienna, the conservative Concert of Europe system restored monarchies and balanced power among Austria, Prussia, Russia, Britain, and France. At Paris, the defeated Central Powers lost territory while new states (Poland, Czechoslovakia, Yugoslavia) were created. Both redrew the map in ways that produced new tensions. Students must identify which conference from contextual clues.

questions.subject: Global History
questions.stem: Which statement best describes the author’s purpose for writing this speech?

choices.label: A
choices.text: He is using Enlightenment ideas to highlight unfair treatment by the French.

choices.label: B
choices.text: He is demonstrating his rejection of Marxist ideas.

choices.label: C
choices.text: He is celebrating his collaboration with the Japanese to rule Indochina.

choices.label: D
choices.text: He is announcing his intention to join the Warsaw Pact.

questions.explanation: The speech — likely Ho Chi Minh's 1945 Vietnamese Declaration of Independence or Simón Bolívar's Angostura Address — uses Enlightenment concepts of natural rights, popular sovereignty, and the illegitimacy of tyranny to highlight the unfairness of colonial rule. By invoking the intellectual framework that Europeans used to justify their own revolutions against tyranny, the speaker exposes the hypocrisy of European powers denying to colonized peoples the same rights they claimed for themselves. This is a central Regents theme: Enlightenment ideas inspiring colonial subjects' resistance.

questions.subject: Global History
questions.stem: Which historical circumstance in Vietnam led to the statements in this speech?

choices.label: A
choices.text: détente

choices.label: B
choices.text: trade embargoes

choices.label: C
choices.text: colonial rule

choices.label: D
choices.text: famine

questions.explanation: Ho Chi Minh's speech is directly rooted in the context of French colonial rule in Indochina (1883–1954), which imposed economic exploitation through rubber plantations, monopolies on alcohol and opium, forced labor, and political repression of Vietnamese culture and governance. French colonialism denied Vietnamese people political representation and economic opportunity while extracting resources for French benefit. This colonial context produced the nationalist resistance that Ho Chi Minh organized through the Viet Minh (1941) and justified through Vietnamese independence declarations.
questions.sourceIssue: Stimulus image was reused by a different released Regents source; hidden until re-imported from the original PDF.

questions.subject: Global History
questions.stem: Which claim about globalization is supported by these documents?

choices.label: A
choices.text: It restricts freedom.

choices.label: B
choices.text: It minimizes trade.

choices.label: C
choices.text: It reshapes cultures.

choices.label: D
choices.text: It discourages creativity.

questions.explanation: The documents demonstrate that globalization reshapes cultures: the spread of American fast food, entertainment, fashion, and English-language media challenges local traditions, languages, and values. McDonald's in Japan, Hollywood in India, and hip-hop in Africa represent both economic integration and cultural transmission. However, globalization also generates hybrid cultures where global and local elements blend (Bollywood, K-pop, Afrobeats) and triggers 'anti-globalization' cultural preservation movements. The Regents tests whether students understand globalization as a cultural force, not only an economic one.
questions.sourceIssue: Stimulus image was reused by a different released Regents source; hidden until re-imported from the original PDF.

questions.subject: Global History
questions.stem: The location identifi ed by this photograph was considered strategically important because of its

choices.label: A
choices.text: distance from the United States

choices.label: B
choices.text: tropical climate

choices.label: C
choices.text: position in the mid-Atlantic

choices.label: D
choices.text: mineral resources

questions.explanation: Cuba's strategic importance during the Cold War derived from its proximity to the United States — only 90 miles from Florida — making Soviet nuclear missiles installed there in October 1962 capable of striking most major American cities with minimal warning time. Distance from the US (the correct answer) is crucial: missiles that close eliminated America's early warning advantage, transforming the Caribbean island into a direct strategic threat. The Cuban Missile Crisis (October 1962) was triggered precisely by this geographic proximity.

questions.subject: Global History
questions.stem: Which event led to the situation depicted in the illustration?

choices.label: A
choices.text: the fall of the Ottoman Empire

choices.label: B
choices.text: the unification of Germany

choices.label: C
choices.text: the partition of India

choices.label: D
choices.text: the rise of the Soviet Union

questions.explanation: The fall of the Ottoman Empire at the end of WWI — formalized by the Treaty of Sèvres (1920) and Lausanne (1923) — created the political vacuum from which Mustafa Kemal Atatürk launched the Turkish War of Independence (1919–1923) and established the Republic of Turkey. The illustration likely shows the dismemberment of Ottoman territories among European powers, which Atatürk rejected by fighting a successful war of resistance. This connection between Ottoman collapse and Turkish nationalist revival is a standard Regents cause-and-effect chain.

questions.subject: Global History
questions.stem: Which event is described in this excerpt from the New York Times?

choices.label: A
choices.text: invasion of Guam by the United States

choices.label: B
choices.text: attack by the Soviet Union on Germany

choices.label: C
choices.text: firebombing of Tokyo by the United States

choices.label: D
choices.text: use of a deadly new weapon against Japan

questions.explanation: The New York Times excerpt describes the atomic bombing of Hiroshima on August 6, 1945, when the US B-29 Enola Gay dropped 'Little Boy' — a uranium bomb killing approximately 70,000–80,000 people instantly, with tens of thousands dying of radiation sickness over subsequent months. A second bomb ('Fat Man') destroyed Nagasaki on August 9. These unprecedented weapons of mass destruction forced Japan's surrender on August 15, 1945, ending WWII. The bombings also inaugurated the nuclear age and arms race that defined the Cold War.

questions.subject: Global History
questions.stem: The American actions described in this article are an example of which policy?

choices.label: A
choices.text: perestroika

choices.label: B
choices.text: collectivization

choices.label: C
choices.text: globalization

choices.label: D
choices.text: containment

questions.explanation: The American actions described — whether the Berlin Airlift (1948–49), Korean War intervention (1950–53), or Vietnam War — exemplify containment, the US Cold War strategy articulated by diplomat George Kennan in his 'Long Telegram' (1946) and 'X Article' (1947) and formalized in the Truman Doctrine (1947). Containment committed the US to preventing Soviet-backed communist movements from gaining control of additional countries — using military force, economic aid, alliances, and covert operations as tools. Students should trace containment from Truman through Eisenhower, Kennedy, and beyond.

questions.subject: Global History
questions.stem: Which claim is best supported by this map?

choices.label: A
choices.text: NATO membership was restricted to European countries.

choices.label: B
choices.text: The Soviet Union bordered all Warsaw Pact nations.

choices.label: C
choices.text: NATO and Warsaw Pact nations shared some boundaries.

choices.label: D
choices.text: All European countries belonged to either NATO or the Warsaw Pact.

questions.explanation: The map shows NATO (North Atlantic Treaty Organization, founded 1949) and Warsaw Pact (founded 1955) member states whose borders were contiguous — particularly in Central Europe where West Germany (NATO) directly bordered East Germany and Czechoslovakia (Warsaw Pact). This geographic confrontation along the Iron Curtain created the most dangerous potential flashpoints of Cold War military conflict. The map confirms that the Cold War's European theater was not an abstract ideological contest but a concrete military confrontation between heavily armed opposing blocs sharing borders.

questions.subject: Global History
questions.stem: According to this article by Roff Smith, the goal of Atatürk’s reforms was to

choices.label: A
choices.text: revive Turkey’s interest in Ottoman-era customs

choices.label: B
choices.text: implement a legal system based on religious teachings

choices.label: C
choices.text: modernize Turkey in the image of European nations

choices.label: D
choices.text: prevent the elimination of the civil service system

questions.explanation: Roff Smith's article about Atatürk argues that his fundamental goal was to transform Turkey from an Islamic Ottoman empire — governing by religious law, with Arabic script, traditional dress, and theocratic legitimacy — into a modern secular European-style republic capable of competing with Western powers as an equal. Atatürk's explicit 'Europeanization' strategy included adopting Swiss civil code, German commercial law, Italian penal code, and the Latin alphabet. His reforms were more comprehensive than any other Muslim-majority nation's modernization program.

questions.subject: Global History
questions.stem: Which term is most closely associated with the situation shown in this photograph?

choices.label: A
choices.text: détente

choices.label: B
choices.text: arms race

choices.label: C
choices.text: nonalignment

choices.label: D
choices.text: perestroika

questions.explanation: The photograph depicts nuclear missiles, warheads, submarines, or strategic bombers — the hardware of the superpower nuclear arms race between the US and USSR. From the Soviet atomic bomb test (1949) through the hydrogen bomb race (1952–53), ICBM development (1957 Sputnik), and Cuban Missile Crisis (1962), the arms race consumed enormous resources on both sides and created existential threat. The arms race reflects the Enduring Issue of militarism and the security dilemma: each side's military buildup makes the other feel less secure and thus builds more weapons.

questions.subject: Global History
questions.stem: One major effect of the historical developments described in this passage was

choices.label: A
choices.text: the easing of international tensions

choices.label: B
choices.text: decreased reliance on intelligence networks

choices.label: C
choices.text: the start of a nuclear arms race

choices.label: D
choices.text: improved opportunities for negotiations

questions.explanation: The historical developments described — likely the atomic bombings of Hiroshima and Nagasaki (1945) or the first Soviet atomic bomb test (August 29, 1949) — directly triggered the nuclear arms race, as both superpowers recognized that atomic weapons provided decisive military advantage and that allowing the rival to develop a nuclear monopoly was intolerable. The arms race escalated from atomic bombs to hydrogen bombs (1952–53), ICBMs (1957), MIRVs, and submarine-launched ballistic missiles, creating the MAD framework that defined Cold War deterrence.

questions.subject: Global History
questions.stem: Which event led to changes similar to those described in this article?

choices.label: A
choices.text: the partition of India in 1948

choices.label: B
choices.text: the collapse of the Austrian Empire

choices.label: C
choices.text: the seizing of the Suez Canal by Gamal Nasser

choices.label: D
choices.text: the Islamic Revolution of 1979 in Iran

questions.explanation: The 1979 Iranian Islamic Revolution — in which Ayatollah Khomeini overthrew the US-backed Shah and established an Islamic Republic — reversed Iran's modernization program, reimposed Islamic law and mandatory hijab, restricted women's rights, expelled Western influences, and transformed Iran from a US ally into a regional adversary. The article describes changes in women's rights, religious law, or cultural life similar to those produced in Iran. Students should recognize that Islamic revolutions have produced similar cultural reversals in multiple contexts (Sudan 1989, Afghanistan 1996 Taliban).

questions.subject: Global History
questions.stem: The phrases “deeply conservative population,” “lengthy sentences of imprisonment,” and “a few hangings” suggest that

choices.label: A
choices.text: the policy of westernization was abandoned by the Turkish government

choices.label: B
choices.text: Atatürk’s reforms were eagerly embraced throughout Turkey

choices.label: C
choices.text: tensions existed between reformers and traditionalists in Turkey

choices.label: D
choices.text: most Turks preferred punishment to rapid change

questions.explanation: The phrases describing Turkey's 'deeply conservative population,' long imprisonment sentences for secular journalists, and tension between modernizers and traditionalists reveal that Atatürk's secular reforms faced persistent resistance from Turkey's rural and religiously observant population who viewed them as foreign impositions attacking Islamic identity. This internal tension between secular Kemalist state institutions (military, judiciary) and religious-traditional society has been the defining fault line of Turkish politics, culminating in Erdogan's AKP movement reversing many Kemalist restrictions since 2002.

questions.subject: Global History
questions.stem: According to this author, the purpose of the Berlin Airlift was to

choices.label: A
choices.text: evacuate the citizens from West Berlin

choices.label: B
choices.text: instill fear amongst the people of Berlin

choices.label: C
choices.text: provide necessary resources to West Berliners

choices.label: D
choices.text: prove that the Berlin blockade was effective

questions.explanation: The Berlin Airlift (June 26, 1948–May 12, 1949) was the Western Allies' response to Stalin's blockade of all land and water routes to West Berlin, cutting off the city's 2 million residents from food, fuel, and supplies. The US and Britain flew over 200,000 flights delivering 2.3 million tons of supplies over 11 months, demonstrating resolve to defend West Berlin without military confrontation. Stalin ended the blockade when it became clear the airlift was succeeding. The author correctly identifies the airlift's purpose as providing essential resources to maintain West Berliner survival and Western credibility.

questions.subject: Global History
questions.stem: Which term is most closely associated with the event shown in this cartoon?

choices.label: A
choices.text: disarmament

choices.label: B
choices.text: Holodomor

choices.label: C
choices.text: blitzkrieg

choices.label: D
choices.text: containment

questions.explanation: Blitzkrieg ('lightning war') was Germany's revolutionary WWII military strategy combining fast-moving armored tank columns (Panzers), motorized infantry, and close-air support (Stukas) in coordinated attacks designed to penetrate enemy lines rapidly before defenders could respond, encircle armies, and prevent the static trench warfare of WWI. Germany used blitzkrieg to defeat Poland in 27 days (September 1939), France in 6 weeks (May–June 1940), and Yugoslavia in 11 days (April 1941). Students should recognize that blitzkrieg overcame the WWI stalemate through speed and coordination rather than mass infantry frontal assault.

questions.subject: Global History
questions.stem: This political cartoon is most closely associated with which world event of the 20th century?

choices.label: A
choices.text: the Soviet invasion of Afghanistan

choices.label: B
choices.text: the Cuban missile crisis

choices.label: C
choices.text: the nuclear meltdown at Chernobyl

choices.label: D
choices.text: the Korean War

questions.explanation: The cartoon depicts the Cuban Missile Crisis of October 1962, when US U-2 spy plane photos revealed Soviet nuclear missile sites being constructed in Cuba, prompting a 13-day nuclear confrontation between Kennedy and Khrushchev. Kennedy imposed a naval 'quarantine' of Cuba (blockade) and demanded Soviet missiles be removed; Khrushchev ultimately agreed in exchange for US pledges not to invade Cuba. This crisis came closest to nuclear war of any Cold War episode and directly motivated arms control diplomacy (Limited Test Ban Treaty, 1963; hotline agreement, 1963).

questions.subject: Global History
questions.stem: This photograph is most often associated with the term

choices.label: A
choices.text: boycott

choices.label: B
choices.text: appeasement

choices.label: C
choices.text: segregation

choices.label: D
choices.text: containment

questions.explanation: The photograph shows Gandhi's Salt March or a Montgomery Bus Boycott scene — an economic boycott, meaning the organized refusal to purchase or use a product or service to pressure a business or government economically. Gandhi's swadeshi movement boycotting British-made textiles and the Montgomery Bus Boycott (December 1955–December 1956) both used economic pressure as nonviolent resistance. Boycotts are a key tool of nonviolent civil disobedience, and the Regents tests this term in connection with both Indian independence and the American civil rights movement.

questions.subject: Global History
questions.stem: Which historical development followed the events described in this excerpt?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The war in Europe came to an end.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Japan entered a period of isolation.

choices.label: C
choices.text: The United States and the Soviet Union began an arms race.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Japan annexed Korea and Manchuria.

questions.explanation: The excerpt describes the atomic bombing of Japan in August 1945 and/or the Soviet atomic test in August 1949, which ended the US nuclear monopoly and directly triggered the arms race. The US then developed the hydrogen bomb (first test, 1952), the USSR followed in 1953, both developed ICBMs in 1957, and submarine-launched ballistic missiles in the 1960s. The nuclear arms race consumed trillions of dollars on both sides and created the existential nuclear threat that dominated Cold War strategy. Students should connect atomic weapons directly to arms race escalation.

questions.subject: Global History
questions.stem: Which empire’s decline is depicted in this illustration?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Austro-Hungarian Empire

choices.label: B
choices.text: Ottoman Empire

choices.label: C
choices.text: Mughal Empire

choices.label: D
choices.text: British Empire

questions.explanation: The illustration depicts the declining Ottoman Empire — often called the 'Sick Man of Europe' — losing territories to nationalist independence movements in the Balkans (Greece 1821, Serbia 1830, Bulgaria 1878, Albania 1912) and to European imperial pressure (Egypt under British influence, North Africa lost to France and Italy). By 1914, the once-vast Ottoman Empire had lost most of its European territories; WWI's end (1918) dissolved the empire entirely, with the Treaty of Sèvres (1920) dividing remaining territories among victorious powers and Atatürk's resistance reconstituting the Turkish heartland.

questions.subject: Global History
questions.stem: Which course of action was taken in response to the atrocities described in this passage?

choices.label: A
choices.text: signing of the Treaty of Versailles

choices.label: B
choices.text: holding of criminal trials at Nuremberg

choices.label: C
choices.text: issuance of the Potsdam Declaration

choices.label: D
choices.text: creation of the policy of containment

questions.explanation: The Nuremberg Trials (November 1945–October 1946) were the International Military Tribunal that prosecuted 24 major Nazi war criminals for crimes against peace (waging aggressive war), war crimes, and crimes against humanity — the first international tribunal to hold individual state officials legally accountable for genocide and atrocities. Twelve defendants received death sentences; seven received prison terms. The Nuremberg precedent established that 'following orders' was not a valid legal defense and laid the foundation for the International Criminal Court (2002). Students should link Nuremberg to the Holocaust as cause-and-effect.

questions.subject: Global History
questions.stem: Which statement best supports the conclusion that Denys is a reliable source of evidence regarding the partition of India?

choices.label: A
choices.text: He provides examples of the hostility individual Indians showed toward British soldiers.

choices.label: B
choices.text: He gives an eyewitness account describing what he saw on Independence Day.

choices.label: C
choices.text: He includes details that support the British government’s position regarding the division of British India.

choices.label: D
choices.text: He reports that India requested the continued help of the British military.

questions.explanation: Eyewitness accounts are valued as historical sources because they provide firsthand observations — what the observer personally saw, heard, and experienced — rather than secondhand or official accounts. Denys's account is reliable as an eyewitness description of specific events he personally witnessed during the 1947 India-Pakistan Partition's violence and mass migration, providing details no government document or newspaper report could replicate. The Regents CRQ framework requires students to evaluate source reliability based on point of view, purpose, proximity to events, and corroboration.
questions.sourceIssue: Question text depends on a source image, but no verified stimulus crop is available.

questions.subject: Global History
questions.stem: Which statement best describes the point of view of this cartoonist?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The arms race was limited to nuclear missiles.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Disarmament talks had not been effective.

choices.label: C
choices.text: The threat of armed revolution was not taken seriously.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Nuclear arms treaties have been highly effective.

questions.explanation: The cartoon's point of view that disarmament talks had been ineffective reflects the reality that despite SALT I (1972), SALT II (1979), and multiple other negotiations, both the US and USSR continued to increase nuclear warhead counts throughout the 1970s–80s — SALT limited delivery vehicles (missiles, submarines, bombers) but not warheads per missile (MIRVs). Reagan's Strategic Defense Initiative ('Star Wars') further alarmed the Soviets. Genuine nuclear reduction (START I, 1991; START II, 1993) only came with the Cold War's end. Students should distinguish arms limitation from genuine disarmament.

questions.subject: Global History
questions.stem: Based on this passage, which long-term impact did the invasion of Afghanistan have on the Soviet Union?

choices.label: A
choices.text: It allowed the Soviets to remove themselves from the Warsaw Pact.

choices.label: B
choices.text: It helped to bring about the collapse of the Soviet Union.

choices.label: C
choices.text: It increased the Soviet Union’s ability to provide for their own people.

choices.label: D
choices.text: It encouraged the Soviets to wage foreign wars and expand their empire.

questions.explanation: The Soviet invasion of Afghanistan (December 1979–February 1989) committed approximately 100,000 Soviet troops to an unwinnable counter-insurgency against CIA-armed mujahideen, resulting in 15,000 Soviet dead and 37,000 wounded over ten years. The war exposed military weakness, demoralized Soviet society, drained an already struggling economy, and reinforced Gorbachev's conclusion that fundamental reform (glasnost, perestroika) was necessary. Combined with economic stagnation and Eastern European democratization, the Afghan failure contributed directly to the Soviet Union's 1991 dissolution.

questions.subject: Global History
questions.stem: Which event led to Kemal Atatürk’s development of New Turkey?

choices.label: A
choices.text: creation of a Jewish homeland

choices.label: B
choices.text: rise of the Soviet Union

choices.label: C
choices.text: unification of Germany

choices.label: D
choices.text: fall of the Ottoman Empire

questions.explanation: The Ottoman Empire's defeat in WWI and the subsequent dismemberment via the Treaty of Sèvres (1920) — which partitioned Anatolia among Greece, Armenia, France, Italy, and a Kurdish state — provoked Mustafa Kemal's Turkish War of Independence (1919–1923). Kemal's military victories against Greek invasion and diplomatic success in replacing Sèvres with the Treaty of Lausanne (1923) preserved Turkish Anatolia as a sovereign state. This crisis of Ottoman collapse gave Atatürk both the political opportunity and nationalist mandate to establish the modern Turkish Republic and implement sweeping secularizing reforms.

questions.subject: Global History
questions.stem: Which situation led to the creation of the alliances shown on this map?

choices.label: A
choices.text: the successful launching of Sputnik by the Soviet Union

choices.label: B
choices.text: tensions between the Soviet Union and the United States

choices.label: C
choices.text: the rejection of a military-industrial complex by the West

choices.label: D
choices.text: Nikita Khrushchev’s goal to expand the Soviet economy

questions.explanation: NATO (1949) was created by Western democracies responding to Soviet expansion in Eastern Europe — the Berlin Blockade, communist coups in Czechoslovakia (1948) and elsewhere — while the Warsaw Pact (1955) was the Soviet-bloc counteralliance created after West Germany joined NATO. These opposing military alliances materialized the Cold War's ideological division into a concrete military confrontation, dividing Europe along the Iron Curtain. The US-Soviet ideological and geopolitical tensions are the underlying cause of both alliance structures.

questions.subject: Global History
questions.stem: What was one result of Atatürk’s policies described in this passage?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Islamic religious leaders gained greater influence over health care in Turkey.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Turkey aligned itself with the Soviet Union.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Turkey adopted a more restrictive approach to social issues.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Secularism began to influence many aspects of Turkish society.

questions.explanation: Atatürk's secularist policies — establishing secular courts, banning religious dress in public buildings, closing religious schools, separating mosque from state — fundamentally reshaped Turkish society by removing Islamic law and clergy from governance, education, and public life. Secularism became the defining principle of the Turkish Republic, codified in the constitution, and enforced by the military as the guardian of Kemalist principles for decades. However, religious practice remained strong in private life, creating the persistent public/private religion divide that shapes Turkish politics today.

questions.subject: Global History
questions.stem: Based on this passage, which statement best describes Atatürk’s point of view?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Turkey would benefit from greater education and training for women.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Women should replace men as leaders in the workforce and the family.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Women should focus completely on motherhood and the care of the home.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Turkey should establish strict separation of men and women.

questions.explanation: Atatürk's point of view holds that Turkey's modernization and international competitiveness required a educated, technically skilled population — particularly women — who could participate fully in economic and civic life. His 1934 grant of women's suffrage, promotion of women in professions (as doctors, lawyers, teachers, pilots), and mandatory secular education reflected his belief that educated women were essential to national strength. This perspective directly parallels the Meiji Restoration's investment in mass education as the foundation of national modernization.

questions.subject: Global History
questions.stem: What is the primary concern from the Arab perspective in these letters?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The international community failed to support their land claims.

choices.label: B
choices.text: The survival of Israel is threatened by distorted and sketchy reporting.

choices.label: C
choices.text: History cannot be used to vindicate the Arab cause.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Israel may not fulfi ll its Biblical destiny.

questions.explanation: The Arab letters following the 1967 Six-Day War express the primary grievance that the international community — particularly the United Nations and Western powers — failed to support Palestinian Arab land claims and prevent or reverse Israel's territorial expansion into the West Bank, Gaza, Sinai, and Golan Heights. The 1947 UN Partition Plan had already rejected Arab claims to all of Palestine; Israel's 1967 territorial gains further diminished prospects for a Palestinian state. The Enduring Issue is competing nationalisms (Israeli and Palestinian) both claiming international legitimacy for their territorial claims.

questions.subject: U.S. History
questions.stem: How did the Pennsylvania Resolutions move the colonies toward independence?

choices.text: They closed the harbor to international trade.
They ordered that citizens of Pennsylvania contribute to relief for fellow colonists.
They aided the process of unifying the colonists in defense of traditional rights.
They censured Boston for destroying valuable British property.

questions.explanation: The Pennsylvania Resolves (1774) were drafted in response to the Intolerable Acts and called on all colonists to stand together in defense of their constitutional rights as Englishmen. By publicly endorsing the convening of a continental congress, they helped unify the thirteen colonies around a common grievance and a shared framework for resistance. On the Regents, this question tests the process by which colonial protests evolved from local complaints into coordinated intercolonial action before independence.

questions.subject: U.S. History
questions.stem: The court case discussed in this passage was important in the development of colonial America because it helped establish the principle of

choices.text: freedom of assembly
freedom of the press
double jeopardy
judicial independence

questions.explanation: The 1735 trial of printer John Peter Zenger established a landmark precedent for freedom of the press in colonial America: a jury acquitted Zenger of seditious libel because his criticism of the royal governor was true, signaling that truth could be a defense against libel charges. This case influenced later First Amendment protections and demonstrated that juries, not just crown-appointed judges, had power over press freedom cases. Regents questions use Zenger to connect colonial legal traditions to the Bill of Rights.
questions.sourceIssue: Question text depends on a source image, but no verified stimulus crop is available.

questions.subject: U.S. History
questions.stem: Under the Articles of Confederation, the years between 1781 and 1787 are often referred to as the "critical period" because the

choices.text: southern states threatened to secede from the Union over the issue of slavery
states were fighting the French and Indian War
colonies were forced to pay high reparations to England
central government lacked the power to deal with major problems

questions.explanation: Under the Articles of Confederation (1781–1789), Congress lacked the power to tax, regulate commerce, or enforce its own laws, creating financial chaos and interstate disputes that threatened national survival. Shays' Rebellion (1786–1787)—a Massachusetts farmers' revolt against debt—exposed these weaknesses and convinced nationalists like Madison and Hamilton that a stronger central government was essential. The 'critical period' label reflects Federalist arguments for replacing the Articles with the Constitution.

questions.subject: U.S. History
questions.stem: Which document includes this passage?

choices.text: Northwest Ordinance
Mayflower Compact
Monroe Doctrine
Declaration of Independence

questions.explanation: The Declaration of Independence (1776) opens with the famous preamble asserting natural rights, the social contract, and the right to revolution—ideas drawn from John Locke's Second Treatise on Government. Jefferson wrote that governments derive their just powers from the consent of the governed and that people may alter or abolish a government that destroys natural rights. Regents students must distinguish this Lockean passage from later constitutional documents like the Preamble to the 1787 Constitution.
questions.sourceIssue: Question text depends on a source image, but no verified stimulus crop is available.

questions.subject: U.S. History
questions.stem: Which generalization is most clearly supported by the information provided by the map?

choices.text: Rivers and harbors played a major role in the location of early settlements.
Only Dutch colonists settled away from the Atlantic coast.
Most early European settlements were located west of the Appalachian Mountains.
The Great Lakes were important shipping routes for the early settlers.

questions.explanation: Early colonial settlements in America clustered along rivers and harbors because water provided transportation, trade routes, fresh water, and defense—explaining why major cities like Boston, New York, and Philadelphia developed at natural harbors or river mouths. This geographic pattern shaped the colonial economy around Atlantic trade and plantation agriculture in the South. Regents questions about colonial geography test students' ability to connect physical geography to patterns of economic and political development.
questions.sourceIssue: Question text depends on a source image, but no verified stimulus crop is available.

questions.subject: U.S. History
questions.stem: The author of this statement is expressing dissatisfaction with a provision included in the

choices.text: Oregon Treaty of 1846
Compromise of 1850
Kansas-Nebraska Act (1854)
Treaty of Ghent (1815)

questions.explanation: The Compromise of 1850 included the Fugitive Slave Act, which required Northern citizens to assist in returning escaped enslaved people to the South and imposed penalties on those who aided escapees. Many Northerners, including abolitionists and ordinary citizens, were deeply offended by being legally compelled to participate in slavery, fueling the growth of antislavery sentiment and the Underground Railroad. This Northern backlash against the Fugitive Slave Act is a key cause of the sectional tensions leading to the Civil War.

questions.subject: U.S. History
questions.stem: In colonial America, the Magna Carta, the English Bill of Rights, and the writings of John Locke contributed to the

choices.text: demands of colonists to end the slave trade and the practice of slav ery
diversity of religious beliefs among the colonists
economic relationships between the colonists and the mother country
political ideals and practices of the colonists

questions.explanation: The Magna Carta (1215) established the principle that even monarchs are subject to the rule of law; the English Bill of Rights (1689) confirmed parliamentary supremacy and individual liberties; John Locke's Second Treatise (1689) articulated natural rights theory and the right to revolution. Together, these documents provided American colonists with a philosophical and legal framework for challenging British authority and justifying independence. The Regents regularly asks students to trace the intellectual origins of American democratic ideals to these British and Enlightenment sources.

questions.subject: U.S. History
questions.stem: What was the primary purpose of the Three-fifths Clause?

choices.text: to ban slavery in the western territories
to quickly end the importation of slaves
to provide for the return of escaped slaves
to help determine a state's congressional representation

questions.explanation: The Three-Fifths Clause (Article I, Section 2) counted three-fifths of 'all other Persons' (enslaved individuals) for determining each state's congressional representation and direct taxation. This gave slave states disproportionate political power in the House of Representatives and the Electoral College without granting any rights to enslaved people themselves. The 14th Amendment (1868) effectively nullified the Three-Fifths Clause by granting full citizenship and counting all persons equally.

questions.subject: U.S. History
questions.stem: Based on this pa ssage, why did Antifederalists oppose ratification of the Constitution?

choices.text: A judicial branch did not exist.
The executive branch had no limits on its authority.
The legislative branch had too much power.
The president was elected by popular vote.

questions.explanation: Antifederalists like Patrick Henry and Mercy Otis Warren argued that the proposed Constitution concentrated too much power in the national government and lacked sufficient protections for individual rights. A key concern was that Congress's enumerated powers plus the Necessary and Proper Clause gave the legislature virtually unlimited authority, threatening state sovereignty and personal liberties. Antifederalist pressure ultimately secured the addition of the Bill of Rights (1791) as the price of ratification in key states.
questions.sourceIssue: Stimulus image was reused by a different released Regents source; hidden until re-imported from the original PDF.

questions.subject: U.S. History
questions.stem: What was one reason for the Senate's action illustrated in this cartoon?

choices.text: There was concern over comm unist control of international organizations.
The Treaty of Versailles did not punish Germany enough.
There was fear of becoming involved in another European war.
It was unconstitutional for a president to negotiate a treaty.

questions.explanation: After World War I, the U.S. Senate rejected membership in President Wilson's League of Nations in 1919–1920, motivated primarily by fears of being drawn into future European wars without a congressional declaration of war. Senators like Henry Cabot Lodge objected to Article X, which could obligate the U.S. to defend member nations, threatening congressional war-making power. This rejection reflected a return to the pre-war tradition of avoiding 'entangling alliances' and previewed the isolationism of the 1930s.
questions.sourceIssue: Question text depends on a source image, but no verified stimulus crop is available.

questions.subject: U.S. History
questions.stem: This 1950 "Declaration of Conscience" speech by Senator Smith was written in respons e to

choices.text: President Dwight D. Eisenhower's support for the French in Indochina
President Harry Truman's integration of the armed forces
Senator Joseph McCarthy's unproven accusations of treason
General Douglas MacArthur's military strategy in Korea

questions.explanation: In June 1950, Republican Senator Margaret Chase Smith of Maine delivered her 'Declaration of Conscience,' calling on the Senate to stop using 'fear, ignorance, bigotry, and smear' as political weapons—a direct rebuke of Senator Joseph McCarthy's (R-WI) reckless accusations of communist infiltration in the government. Smith was the first senator to publicly challenge McCarthyism, doing so at great political risk. Her speech is a Regents touchstone for questions about civil liberties and the dangers of political demagoguery.

questions.subject: U.S. History
questions.stem: Which event did President Bush refer to in this quotation?

choices.text: terrorist actions on September 11, 2001
start of Operation Iraqi Freedom
Soviet invasion of Afghanistan
Oklahoma City bombing on April 17, 1995

questions.explanation: On September 11, 2001, al-Qaeda terrorists hijacked four commercial airliners, crashing two into the World Trade Center in New York City, one into the Pentagon, and one into a Pennsylvania field—killing nearly 3,000 people in the deadliest terrorist attack on American soil. President George W. Bush used this event to justify the Authorization for Use of Military Force (AUMF), the PATRIOT Act, and the invasion of Afghanistan (2001) and Iraq (2003). The 9/11 attacks fundamentally reshaped U.S. foreign and domestic policy for the following two decades.

questions.subject: U.S. History
questions.stem: This cartoonist is expressing a concern that the current Social Security system

choices.text: does not support a decent standard of living
imposes taxes which are too high
does not cover most American workers
faces threats from future increases in the number of recipients

questions.explanation: The Social Security system, created by the Social Security Act (1935) under FDR, is funded by payroll taxes from current workers and paid to current retirees—a pay-as-you-go structure. As the Baby Boom generation retires and birth rates decline, fewer workers will support more retirees, threatening the system's long-term solvency. Regents cartoons on this topic test students' understanding of demographic challenges to entitlement programs, a recurring theme in modern domestic policy questions.
questions.sourceIssue: Question text depends on a source image, but no verified stimulus crop is available.

questions.subject: U.S. History
questions.stem: Which constitutional amendment was adopted in response to the issue raised on this postcard?

choices.text: graduated income tax
limit on the number of years a president can serve
ban on poll taxes in presidential elections
direct election of United States senators

questions.explanation: The 22nd Amendment (1951) limits the president to two terms in office, a direct response to Franklin D. Roosevelt's unprecedented four elections (1932, 1936, 1940, 1944). Critics argued that FDR's four terms threatened the two-term tradition established by George Washington and concentrated too much power in the executive branch. The postcard depicted likely showed public pressure for term limits following the FDR era, which the 22nd Amendment formalized.
questions.sourceIssue: Stimulus image was reused by a different released Regents source; hidden until re-imported from the original PDF.

questions.subject: U.S. History
questions.stem: Which change to the Constitution is most often suggested by critics of the election results shown in Cthese charts?

choices.text: making the loser of the election the vice president
eliminating the electoral college
banning third-party candidates from presidential elections
requiring a revote

questions.explanation: Critics of the Electoral College system argue that elections like 1876 and 2000, where the popular-vote winner lost the presidency, demonstrate a fundamental flaw in American democracy. The most commonly proposed reform is eliminating the Electoral College in favor of a direct popular vote, which would require a constitutional amendment. The Regents uses this question to test students' ability to identify constitutional reform proposals in response to contested election outcomes.

questions.subject: U.S. History
questions.stem: The crisis highlighted in these newspaper headlines was the

choices.text: invasion of the Bay of Pigs in Cuba
opposition of Cuba to the Alliance for Progress
election of Fidel Castro as president of Cuba
placement of nuclear missiles in Cuba by the Soviet Union

questions.explanation: The Cuban Missile Crisis (October 1962) began when American U-2 spy planes photographed Soviet nuclear missile installations being built in Cuba, placing nuclear warheads 90 miles from Florida. For 13 days, President Kennedy and Soviet Premier Khrushchev engaged in a tense standoff before reaching an agreement: the Soviets would remove missiles from Cuba in exchange for a U.S. pledge not to invade Cuba and the secret removal of U.S. missiles from Turkey. The crisis is considered the closest the Cold War came to nuclear conflict.
questions.sourceIssue: Stimulus image was reused by a different released Regents source; hidden until re-imported from the original PDF.

questions.subject: U.S. History
questions.stem: The main idea of the cartoon is that President Franklin D. Roosevelt wanted to

choices.text: encourage the Supreme Court to act more efficiently
impeach justices who did not support him
control the decisions of the Supreme Court
create higher qualifications for justices

questions.explanation: In 1937, frustrated that the Supreme Court was striking down key New Deal programs as unconstitutional, President Roosevelt proposed the Judiciary Reorganization Bill—the 'court-packing plan'—which would allow him to appoint an additional justice for every sitting justice over age 70, potentially adding up to six new justices. Congress rejected the plan as a dangerous attack on judicial independence and the separation of powers. The crisis is a key Regents example of how the system of checks and balances can check even popular presidents.

questions.subject: U.S. History
questions.stem: In Korematsu v. United States (1944), the Supreme Court upheld the military order excl uding Japanese Americans from the West Coast on the basis that the action was considered

choices.text: a way to protect Japanese Americans from anti-Japanese hysteria
an attempt to limit immigration from Japan
a necessity for the economy
a matter of national security

questions.explanation: In Korematsu v. United States (1944), the Supreme Court ruled 6–3 that the wartime internment of Japanese Americans (Executive Order 9066) was constitutional because the military deemed it a necessary security measure following the attack on Pearl Harbor. The Court accepted the government's national security argument over civil liberties objections, setting a troubling precedent for the suspension of rights during wartime. The decision was effectively repudiated by Congress with the Civil Liberties Act of 1988, which offered a formal apology and reparations to internment survivors.

questions.subject: U.S. History
questions.stem: What is a major result of the Supreme Court decisions in McCulloch v. Maryland (1819) and Gibbons v. Ogden (1824)?

choices.text: The powers of the federal government were expanded.
The powers of Congress over the territories were reduced.
Abuses of power by the president were prevented.
Freedoms guaranteed in the Bill of Rights were restricted.

questions.explanation: McCulloch v. Maryland (1819) and Gibbons v. Ogden (1824) were both decided by the Marshall Court and both significantly expanded the implied powers of the federal government at the expense of state authority. McCulloch established that the Necessary and Proper Clause allowed Congress to create a national bank even though it was not explicitly authorized, while Gibbons broadly defined 'commerce' to give Congress sweeping regulatory power. Together, these cases form the constitutional basis for the modern regulatory state.

questions.subject: U.S. History
questions.stem: Which action by the federal government was a step toward the approach to business favored by Speaker B?

choices.text: decision by the Supreme Court in United States v. E. C. Knight Co.
creation of the civil service system
passage of the Transcontinental Railway Act
passage of the Sherman Antitrust Act

questions.explanation: Speaker B in the paired quotes would favor government intervention to limit monopoly power, consistent with the Progressive Era regulatory philosophy. The Sherman Antitrust Act (1890) was the federal government's first major attempt to break up trusts and restore competition in the marketplace. Roosevelt's aggressive use of the Act—filing suit against Northern Securities (1902) and Standard Oil (1906)—marked the first time the federal government actively used antitrust law to check corporate power as a matter of public policy.

questions.subject: U.S. History
questions.stem: The "separate but equal" doctrine established by the Supreme Court in Plessy v. Ferguson (1896) upheld the legality of

choices.text: the activities of the Ku Klux Klan
racial Nsegregation in public facilities
restrictions on voting rights of African Americans
wom an's suffrage in state elections

questions.explanation: Plessy v. Ferguson (1896) upheld a Louisiana law requiring 'separate but equal' railroad cars for Black and white passengers, establishing the legal basis for Jim Crow segregation throughout the South. The lone dissenter, Justice John Marshall Harlan, warned that the Constitution is 'color-blind.' Plessy stood for 58 years until Brown v. Board of Education (1954) overturned the 'separate but equal' doctrine in public education, ruling that segregated schools were inherently unequal.

questions.subject: U.S. History
questions.stem: The Supreme Court can influence the actions of the other two branches of the federal government by

choices.text: impeaching the president
exercising judicial review
pardoning criminals
vetoing legislation

questions.explanation: Judicial review—established in Marbury v. Madison (1803) and grounded in Article III of the Constitution—allows the Supreme Court to strike down laws passed by Congress or executive actions taken by the president if they violate the Constitution. This power makes the judiciary a critical check on the other two branches, balancing the system of checks and balances the Framers designed. Students often confuse judicial review with judicial activism; the distinction is that review refers to the constitutional power, while activism describes how aggressively it is exercised.

questions.subject: U.S. History
questions.stem: The issue discussed in this interview resulted from an effort to enforce the Supreme Court decision in

choices.text: Brown v. Board of Education of Topeka
Dred Scott v. Sanford
Plessy v. Ferguson
Heart of Atlanta Motel v. United States

questions.explanation: The Brown v. Board of Education (1954) ruling declared racially segregated public schools unconstitutional, overturning Plessy v. Ferguson (1896), but many Southern states resisted implementation. In September 1957, Governor Orval Faubus of Arkansas ordered the National Guard to block nine Black students from entering Little Rock's Central High School; President Eisenhower responded by federalizing the Arkansas National Guard and sending 101st Airborne Division troops to escort the students. The 'Little Rock Crisis' illustrated the clash between federal judicial authority and states'-rights resistance to desegregation.

questions.subject: U.S. History
questions.stem: E The Supreme Court decision described in this excerpt is an example of which principle?

choices.text: sectionalism
republican government
federal supremacy
popular sovereignty

questions.explanation: The Supreme Court decision in McCulloch v. Maryland (1819) established the principle of federal supremacy—that federal law and federal institutions take precedence over conflicting state laws and actions. Marshall grounded this in the Supremacy Clause (Article VI) and the Necessary and Proper Clause (Article I, Section 8), ruling that Maryland's tax on the national bank unconstitutionally interfered with a legitimate federal function. This principle is regularly tested on the Regents through questions about the relationship between state and federal authority.

questions.subject: U.S. History
questions.stem: Which constitutional amendment was adopted in response to the issue raised on this postcard?

choices.text: limit on the number of years a president can serve
ban on poll taxes in presidential elections
direct election of United States senators
graduated income tax

questions.explanation: The 22nd Amendment (1951), ratified after FDR's four-term presidency (1933–1945), formally limited presidents to two terms (10 years maximum if a vice president finishes a partial term). Before FDR, the two-term limit was an unwritten custom established by Washington and observed by all presidents except Grant (who sought but failed to win a third term). The postcard depicted in the original question likely called for a formal term limit after FDR's unprecedented electoral dominance.

questions.subject: U.S. History
questions.stem: Which statement best represents a reason why this protest was considered controversial?

choices.text: Suffragists were picketing while German submarines were attacking American ships.
The country was dealing with public outrage over government corruption.
The Senate had already repealed the Prohibition amendment.
President Wilson did not include women's demands in his Fourteen Points.

questions.explanation: During World War I (1917–1918), women suffragists picketed the White House carrying banners quoting President Wilson's war rhetoric about democracy and freedom, demanding he support votes for women. The protest was controversial because critics argued it was unpatriotic to picket while American soldiers were dying on the Western Front. Despite arrests and imprisonment, the suffragists' persistence contributed to the passage of the 19th Amendment (1920), granting women the right to vote.

questions.subject: U.S. History
questions.stem: The principle expressed in this proposed law was also contained in the

choices.text: Alien and Sedition Acts
Zenger case decision
Albany Plan of Union
First amendment

questions.explanation: The proposed law in the passage reflects the principle of religious freedom—specifically the separation of church and state—which is enshrined in the First Amendment's Establishment and Free Exercise Clauses. The First Amendment prohibits Congress from making any law establishing a religion or restricting its free exercise, making religious freedom a fundamental constitutional right. Regents questions on the First Amendment test students' ability to distinguish its five protections: religion, speech, press, assembly, and petition.

questions.subject: U.S. History
questions.stem: Which action would be considered an example of the use of the unwritten constitution?

choices.text: passage of the Civil Rights Act of 1964
cabinet meeting called by President Bill Clinton in 1993
declaration of war against Japan in 1941
ratification of the 19th amendment in 1920

questions.explanation: The 'unwritten constitution' refers to political practices and customs that have developed over time without being explicitly written in the Constitution itself, such as the cabinet, political parties, senatorial courtesy, and executive agreements. President Washington established the cabinet precedent in 1789 when he began meeting with department heads for advice; this practice has been followed by every president since. The Regents tests students' knowledge of how American government has evolved beyond the written constitutional text.

questions.subject: U.S. History
questions.stem: The opinion expressed by the cartoonist is that the USA Patriot Act allows the federal government to

choices.text: eliminate States rights reserved in the 10th amendment
set new term limits for federal judges
restrict the investigative powers of the police
violate freedoms guaranteed in the Bill of Rights

questions.explanation: The USA PATRIOT Act (2001), passed six weeks after the September 11 attacks, expanded the government's surveillance powers—allowing roving wiretaps, monitoring of internet activity, and access to library and business records—with reduced judicial oversight. Civil liberties advocates argued these provisions violated Fourth Amendment protections against unreasonable search and seizure and First Amendment rights of free expression. The PATRIOT Act became a flashpoint in the ongoing debate between national security and civil liberties.
questions.sourceIssue: Question text depends on a source image, but no verified stimulus crop is available.

questions.subject: U.S. History
questions.stem: Based on this passage, the purpose of the Federalist Papers was to

choices.text: support ratification of the Co nstitution
justify the American Revolution to the colonists
discourage the creation of political parties
argue for a bill of rights

questions.explanation: The Federalist Papers (1787–1788), written by Hamilton, Madison, and Jay, were essays published in New York newspapers arguing for ratification of the proposed Constitution. Key pieces like Federalist No. 51 explained how the separation of powers and checks and balances would prevent tyranny, while No. 10 addressed how a large republic could manage the problem of factions. On the Regents, students must know the purpose, authorship, and key arguments of the Federalist Papers as primary sources for constitutional intent.
questions.sourceIssue: Question text depends on a source image, but no verified stimulus crop is available.

questions.subject: U.S. History
questions.stem: Based on this passage, why did Antifederalists oppose ratification of the Constitution?

choices.text: The executive branch had no limits on its authority.
The pres ident was elected by popular vote.
A judicial branch did not exist.
The legislative branch had too much power.

questions.explanation: Antifederalists feared the proposed Constitution gave the national legislature too much power through the Necessary and Proper Clause and the Supremacy Clause, leaving states and citizens vulnerable to federal overreach. Mercy Otis Warren, in her 'Observations on the New Constitution' (1788), argued that the House and Senate's broad powers were inadequately separated from each other and from the executive. Their opposition forced the Federalists to promise a Bill of Rights, which was added in 1791 to protect individual freedoms from federal encroachment.

questions.subject: U.S. History
questions.stem: Which United States foreign policy was most often used to carry out the actions shown on the map?

choices.text: Open Door
Wilson's Fourteen Points
Kellogg-Briand Pact
Roosevelt Corollary to the Monroe Doctrine

questions.explanation: The Roosevelt Corollary (1904) to the Monroe Doctrine gave the U.S. the right to intervene militarily in Latin American nations that were unable or unwilling to pay their international debts. The U.S. subsequently used this justification to intervene in the Dominican Republic (1905, 1916), Nicaragua (1912), Haiti (1915), and other nations. This 'Big Stick' policy of direct military intervention defined early 20th-century U.S.-Latin American relations and is the foreign policy most directly associated with the map of Caribbean interventions.
questions.sourceIssue: Question text depends on a source image, but no verified stimulus crop is available.

questions.subject: U.S. History
questions.stem: Which statement most accurately expresses the main idea of the cartoon?

choices.text: American taxpayers hope the Marshall Plan will quickly stabilize Europe's economy.
The Marshall Plan will bankrupt the American taxpayer.
The Marshall Plan will limit Europe's economic recovery.
Europe will not take advantage of the Marshall Plan.

questions.explanation: The Marshall Plan cartoon depicts American taxpayers as hoping their $13 billion investment would quickly stabilize Western Europe's war-ravaged economies and prevent communist takeover. The plan succeeded in rebuilding European economies and strengthening democratic governments, but critics noted that the aid also served American economic interests by creating markets for U.S. exports. The Regents regularly tests the dual purposes—humanitarian and strategic—of the Marshall Plan within the context of early Cold War containment policy.

questions.subject: U.S. History
questions.stem: The purpose of this statement was to

choices.text: provide justification for declaring independence
advocate obedience to Great Britain
criticize the Articles of Confederation
urge support for the Albany Plan of Union

questions.explanation: The Declaration of Independence (1776) listed 27 specific grievances against King George III to justify the colonial break from Britain, drawing on John Locke's social contract theory to argue that a government that fails its obligations to the people may rightfully be overthrown. The passage asking for this identifies the purpose as providing justification for declaring independence—framing revolution as a constitutional right rather than an act of rebellion. Regents questions frequently pair this with the Constitution to test understanding of the differences between the two founding documents.
questions.sourceIssue: Question text depends on a source statement, but no verified stimulus crop is available.

questions.subject: U.S. History
questions.stem: Which document was issued primarily to prevent European nations from future colonization in Latin America?

choices.text: Alien and Sedition Acts (1798)
Jay Treaty (1795)
Monroe Doctrine (1823)
Embargo Act (1807)

questions.explanation: The Monroe Doctrine (1823), written by Secretary of State John Quincy Adams and delivered by President Monroe, declared the Western Hemisphere off-limits to further European colonization and warned that any attempt to extend the European political system to the Americas would be viewed as a threat to U.S. security. It was issued in response to fears of Spanish reconquest of Latin American nations that had recently achieved independence and Russian expansion along the Pacific Northwest coast. The Doctrine became the cornerstone of U.S. Latin American policy for nearly two centuries.
questions.sourceIssue: Question text depends on a source image, but no verified stimulus crop is available.

questions.subject: U.S. History
questions.stem: The alleged danger referred to in this passage contributed to the

choices.text: ratification of the Treaty of Versailles
elimination of the military draft
changing of United States immigration policy
failure of the American Federation of Labor

questions.explanation: The passage describes fears of communist and anarchist immigrants—a key element of the first Red Scare (1919–1921)—which directly led Congress to pass the Emergency Quota Act (1921) and the National Origins Act (1924), drastically limiting immigration from Southern and Eastern Europe. Attorney General A. Mitchell Palmer's raids in 1919–1920 targeted suspected radicals, deporting hundreds of aliens without due process. The Regents connects the Red Scare to restrictive immigration legislation as a cause-and-effect relationship students must master.
questions.sourceIssue: Question text depends on a source image, but no verified stimulus crop is available.

questions.subject: U.S. History
questions.stem: In this 1941 passage, Charles Lindbergh is supporting the idea that the United States should

choices.text: decrease its military spending
enter the war on the side of the democratic nations
isolate itself from the war
commit to the defense of East Asia

questions.explanation: In September 1941, aviation hero Charles Lindbergh gave an 'America First' speech in Des Moines, Iowa, urging the U.S. to stay out of World War II and avoid the 'Jewish influence' he blamed for pushing America toward war—a speech widely condemned as antisemitic and isolationist. Lindbergh had been the country's most prominent isolationist since 1939, arguing America had no strategic interest in Europe's war. After Pearl Harbor (December 7, 1941), isolationism collapsed and Lindbergh's reputation was permanently damaged.
questions.sourceIssue: Stimulus image was reused by a different released Regents source; hidden until re-imported from the original PDF.

questions.subject: U.S. History
questions.stem: The Northwest Ordinance (1787) and the Homestead Act (1862) both reflected the national government's policy of

choices.text: protecting the tribal lands of Native American Indians
encouraging the settlement of frontier lands
purchasing land from foreign countries
expa nding slavery onto the Great Plains

questions.explanation: The Northwest Ordinance (1787) created a process for admitting new states from the Northwest Territory and banned slavery north of the Ohio River, establishing the federal government's role in organizing westward expansion. The Homestead Act (1862) gave 160 acres of federal land to settlers willing to farm it for five years, opening the Great Plains to mass settlement. Both laws reflected the national government's consistent policy of actively encouraging citizens to settle and develop the frontier, a theme the Regents links to Manifest Destiny and westward expansion.

questions.subject: U.S. History
questions.stem: What was one action President Lincoln took to achieve his primary goal?

choices.text: He supported passage of the Pacific Railway Act.
He signed the Homestead Act.
He vetoed a federal draft law.
He issued the Emancipation Proclamation.

questions.explanation: When Lincoln took office in March 1861, his primary goal was preserving the Union, not abolishing slavery—he pledged in his First Inaugural not to interfere with slavery where it existed. As the war dragged on, however, Lincoln came to see emancipation as a military necessity: freeing enslaved people in rebel states would deprive the Confederacy of labor, encourage Black enlistment in the Union Army, and help prevent European powers from recognizing the Confederacy. The Emancipation Proclamation (January 1, 1863) reflected this shift from purely preserving the Union to making emancipation a war aim.

questions.subject: U.S. History
questions.stem: The purpose of each of these provisions is to

choices.text: create a process for allowing amendments
limit the power of the executive branch
determine the division of power between state and central governments
grant the central government power to control the states

questions.explanation: The Articles of Confederation (1781) and the Constitution (1789) both contained provisions addressing the division of power between the states and the central government, but they drew the line very differently: the Articles left most power with the states, while the Constitution created a much stronger federal government. The Regents frequently asks students to compare these two founding documents in terms of how they allocated governmental authority, connecting this to the historical debate between Federalists and Antifederalists.

questions.subject: U.S. History
questions.stem: Which later action by President Lincoln demonstrated a change from his 1861 position?

choices.text: issuing the Emancipation Proclamation
signing the Pacific Railway Act
appointing William Seward Secretary of State
suspending habeas corpus

questions.explanation: In his First Inaugural Address (1861), Lincoln promised not to interfere with slavery where it existed, hoping to keep border states loyal and reunite the nation peacefully. By issuing the Emancipation Proclamation (January 1, 1863), Lincoln demonstrated a fundamental change in war aims—from purely preserving the Union to also abolishing slavery in rebel states. This shift reflected both military necessity (weakening the Confederacy) and moral evolution in Lincoln's thinking as the war's human cost mounted.

questions.subject: U.S. History
questions.stem: The Soviet response to the Marshall Plan contributed to

choices.text: the Cold War
the end of the North Atlantic Treaty Organization (NATO)
increased aid to African nations
a decline in United States-European trade

questions.explanation: The Soviet Union rejected the Marshall Plan and instead created COMECON (1949) to bind Eastern European economies to Moscow, deepening the division of Europe into Western and Soviet spheres. This rejection hardened Cold War tensions, accelerated the formation of NATO (1949), and solidified the Iron Curtain that Churchill had described in 1946. On the Regents, the Marshall Plan's aftermath is a key cause of the Cold War division of Europe.

questions.subject: U.S. History
questions.stem: The Patriot Act was a direct response to

choices.text: international terrorism
religious toleration
unbalanced trade
global genocide

questions.explanation: The USA PATRIOT Act (Uniting and Strengthening America by Providing Appropriate Tools Required to Intercept and Obstruct Terrorism Act) was signed into law October 26, 2001—just 45 days after the September 11 attacks—in direct response to the worst terrorist attack on American soil. It dramatically expanded the federal government's surveillance, search, and monitoring powers to combat terrorism, with provisions allowing 'sneak and peek' searches and broad monitoring of financial and communication records. Critics argued the speed of passage left insufficient time for deliberation on its civil liberties implications.

questions.subject: U.S. History
questions.stem: What is the best title for this map?

choices.text: Territorial Expansion
Results of the Revolution
Eastward Migration
Immigration Before the Civil War

questions.explanation: U.S. territorial expansion—from the original 13 states to the Pacific—was driven by the Louisiana Purchase (1803), the acquisition of Florida (1819), the Texas annexation (1845), the Oregon Territory agreement (1846), the Mexican Cession (1848), and the Gadsden Purchase (1853), among others. 'Territorial Expansion' is the correct map title because it reflects this systematic growth of U.S. national territory over the 18th and 19th centuries. The Regents tests students' knowledge of the key territorial acquisitions that shaped modern U.S. geography.
questions.sourceIssue: Question text depends on a source image, but no verified stimulus crop is available.

questions.subject: U.S. History
questions.stem: Which conclusion is most clearly supported by the information in the time line?

choices.text: Most Americans are opposed to government intervention in the economy.
Government intervention in the economy has limited the growth of the gross domestic product.
United States economic policies have been modified to meet ch anging conditions.
The United States government has shown little interest in regulating big business.

questions.explanation: U.S. economic policies have shifted dramatically in response to changing conditions: from laissez-faire in the Gilded Age, to Progressive-era regulation, to New Deal interventionism, to Cold War mixed economy, to Reagan-era deregulation. The timeline question tests students' ability to recognize that U.S. economic policy is not fixed but has repeatedly evolved in response to crises, political movements, and changing economic realities. This theme of adaptation in economic policy is a recurring topic on the Regents.

questions.subject: U.S. History
questions.stem: Which conclusion is best supported by the information on these graphs?

choices.text: Nations that produce the most energy are the richest nations in the world.
The United States uses more energy than it produces.
More nations produce energy than consume it.
South American nations are the greatest producers of energy.

questions.explanation: The graphs showing U.S. energy consumption exceeding domestic production reflect a structural gap that first became acute during the 1973 Arab Oil Embargo, when OPEC nations cut off oil exports to the U.S. in response to American support for Israel in the Yom Kippur War. This energy dependence has remained a persistent feature of U.S. foreign policy, connecting domestic energy needs to decisions about alliances in the Middle East, military commitments, and environmental policy. Regents questions on energy use this data to test understanding of the foreign policy implications of economic interdependence.

questions.subject: U.S. History
questions.stem: Which action by the federal government was a step toward the approach to business favored by Speaker B?

choices.text: passage of the Sherman Antitrust Act
creation of the civil service system
passage of the Transcontinental Railway Act
decision by the Supreme Court in United States v. E. C. Knight Co.

questions.explanation: Speaker B represents the Progressive-era position that government must regulate business to protect consumers, workers, and competition from monopolistic abuse. The Sherman Antitrust Act (1890), used aggressively by Theodore Roosevelt starting in 1902, was the federal government's main tool for pursuing this regulatory philosophy. On the Regents, paired-speaker questions about business regulation typically contrast laissez-faire (government should not interfere) with Progressive (government must regulate to protect the public) perspectives.

questions.subject: U.S. History
questions.stem: The author of this statement is expressing dissatisfaction with a provision included in the

choices.text: Treaty of Ghent (1815)
Oregon Treaty of 1846
Kansas-Nebraska Act (1854)
Compromise of 1850

questions.explanation: The Compromise of 1850's Fugitive Slave Act required Northern citizens to assist federal marshals in capturing and returning escaped enslaved people, making Northerners complicit in slavery for the first time. This provision enraged many Northerners who had previously been indifferent to slavery, fueling abolitionist sentiment and the Underground Railroad's expansion. The author's 'dissatisfaction' with the Compromise reflects the Northern perspective that the Fugitive Slave Act was a moral outrage that made sectional reconciliation impossible.

questions.subject: U.S. History
questions.stem: The information provided by the map best supports the conclusion that

choices.text: most railroads were owned and operated by the United States go vernment
the North had transportation advantages by the start of the Civil War
the South's transportation system was more efficient
the transcontinental railroad linked the West and the South

questions.explanation: By the eve of the Civil War, the North had a far more extensive railroad network than the South—about 70% of total U.S. rail mileage—giving it significant advantages in moving troops, supplies, and industrial goods during the conflict. This transportation infrastructure advantage was one key reason the Union was ultimately able to mobilize and supply armies across vast distances while the Confederacy struggled with logistics.
