The Regents uses Civil War maps to test students' ability to connect geographic and economic advantages to military outcomes.

questions.subject: U.S. History
questions.stem: The tactics illustrated in the cartoon were most closely associated with

choices.text: government leaders investigating communist activities after World War II
Congress promoting increased security after the September Y11, 2001 attacks
increased federal spending for education during the 1960s
isolationists supporting neutrality policies during the 1930s

questions.explanation: The cartoon depicting tactics of accusation, guilt by association, and innuendo refers to McCarthyism (1950–1954), when Senator Joseph McCarthy (R-WI) made sweeping, largely unsubstantiated accusations of communist infiltration in the government, military, and entertainment industry. These tactics—labeling opponents as communists without evidence—created a climate of fear that destroyed careers and suppressed dissent, echoing the earlier Red Scare of 1919–1921. The Army-McCarthy Hearings (1954), televised nationally, ultimately ended McCarthy's influence when attorney Joseph Welch asked: 'Have you no sense of decency?'
questions.sourceIssue: Stimulus image was reused by a different released Regents source; hidden until re-imported from the original PDF.

questions.subject: U.S. History
questions.stem: What is the most accurate title for this map?

choices.text: Causes of World War II
World War II in the Pacific
Creation of Wartime Alliances
American Imperialism in the 20th Century

questions.explanation: The Pacific theater of World War II (1941–1945) involved American forces 'island-hopping' across the Pacific—capturing key Japanese-held islands like Guadalcanal, Iwo Jima, and Okinawa—to establish bases close enough to bomb the Japanese home islands. The map shows the gradual Allied advance across the vast Pacific distances, culminating in the atomic bombings of Hiroshima (August 6, 1945) and Nagasaki (August 9, 1945) and Japan's surrender. Regents students must know the key battles, strategies, and turning points of the Pacific theater.
questions.sourceIssue: Question text depends on a source image, but no verified stimulus crop is available.

questions.subject: U.S. History
questions.stem: The low level of immigration between 1930 and 1945 is most directly related to

choices.text: passage of the Chinese Exclusion Act
the Great Depression and World War II
the Cold War and the civil rights movement
improvements in living standards in Europe and Asia

questions.explanation: Immigration fell dramatically between 1930 and 1945 because the Great Depression made the U.S. economically unappealing and led to nativist backlash and deportation drives (including the forced deportation of 400,000+ Mexican Americans), while World War II disrupted Atlantic shipping routes and made emigration from Europe extremely dangerous. This double coincidence of economic depression and wartime disruption produced the lowest immigration levels since the colonial era. The Regents tests students' ability to link specific historical events to quantitative data shown on graphs.

questions.subject: U.S. History
questions.stem: Which conclusion is most clearly supported by information on the map?

choices.text: The United States respected the sovereignty of Latin American nations.
The United States rarely used its armed forces in Latin America before World War II.
United States military action in Latin America supported European colonies.
United States military action was used to protect American interests.

questions.explanation: The map of U.S. military interventions in the Caribbean and Central America between 1898 and 1934 illustrates the Roosevelt Corollary in action: the U.S. used military force to protect American business investments and prevent European creditors from intervening in Latin American affairs. These interventions—in Cuba, Haiti, Nicaragua, Panama, and the Dominican Republic—were driven by a combination of economic interest, strategic concern about the Panama Canal, and paternalistic assumptions about Latin American self-governance. The Good Neighbor Policy (1933) under FDR ended most of these direct interventions.
questions.sourceIssue: Question text depends on a source image, but no verified stimulus crop is available.

questions.subject: U.S. History
questions.stem: What was the immediate cause of the action taken by the United States that is shown on the map?

choices.text: Cuban refugees lobbied President Dwight Eisenhower to overthro w Fidel Castro.
United States military bases in the Caribbean were closed by Cuban armed forces.
Puerto Rican citizens asked Congress to assist them in repelling communist advances.
The Soviet Union built nuclear missile launch sites within range of United States cities.

questions.explanation: The Cuban Missile Crisis (October 1962) was triggered when American U-2 reconnaissance planes photographed Soviet nuclear missile launch sites under construction in Cuba, placing nuclear warheads within striking distance of major U.S. cities including Washington, D.C. President Kennedy responded with a naval 'quarantine' of Cuba and demanded the missiles be removed, confronting Soviet Premier Khrushchev in a 13-day standoff that brought the world closer to nuclear war than at any other point in the Cold War. The crisis was resolved diplomatically: the Soviets withdrew missiles from Cuba; the U.S. pledged not to invade Cuba and secretly agreed to remove Jupiter missiles from Turkey.

questions.subject: U.S. History
questions.stem: What is the best title for this map?

choices.text: Territorial Expansion
Immigration Before the Civil War
Results of the Revolution
Eastward Migration

questions.explanation: Between 1803 and 1853, the United States grew from the original 13 states on the Atlantic seaboard to a continental nation stretching to the Pacific Ocean through a series of purchases, annexations, and treaties. The Louisiana Purchase (1803) doubled U.S. territory; the Florida Purchase (1819), Texas Annexation (1845), Oregon Treaty (1846), Mexican Cession (1848), and Gadsden Purchase (1853) completed the continental expansion. 'Territorial Expansion' is the appropriate title because it reflects this deliberate, systematic growth driven by Manifest Destiny and economic motives.
questions.sourceIssue: Question text depends on a source image, but no verified stimulus crop is available.

questions.subject: U.S. History
questions.stem: Which event did President Bush refer to in this quotation?

choices.text: terrorist actions on September 11, 2001
start of Operation Iraqi Freedom
Oklahoma City bombing on April 17, 1995
Soviet invasion of Afghanistan

questions.explanation: The September 11, 2001, terrorist attacks—in which al-Qaeda hijackers flew commercial airplanes into the World Trade Center, the Pentagon, and a Pennsylvania field, killing nearly 3,000 people—represented the deadliest foreign attack on American soil since Pearl Harbor. President Bush cited 9/11 to justify the PATRIOT Act (2001), the invasion of Afghanistan (October 2001), and the 2002 National Security Strategy doctrine of preemptive war. The attacks fundamentally reshaped U.S. foreign and domestic policy, initiating the 'War on Terror' that continues to shape American security debates.
questions.sourceIssue: Stimulus image was reused by a different released Regents source; hidden until re-imported from the original PDF.

questions.subject: U.S. History
questions.stem: Which statement most accurately expresses the main idea of the cartoon?

choices.text: The Marshall Plan will limit Europe's economic recovery.
Europe will not take advantage of the Marshall Plan.
The Marshall Plan will bankrupt the American taxpayer. - President John F. Kennedy, Inaugural Address, January 20, 1961
American taxpayers hope the Marshall Plan will quickly stabilize Europe's economy.

questions.explanation: The Marshall Plan cartoon shows American taxpayers as stakeholders in the plan's success, reflecting the economic rationale that a stable, prosperous Europe would buy American exports, creating jobs and growth at home as well as abroad. The plan's primary stated goal was to prevent the spread of communism by rebuilding democratic market economies, but it also served clear American economic interests. Regents questions test students' understanding that U.S. foreign policy decisions often blend idealistic goals (democracy, freedom) with realist interests (trade, markets, strategic stability).

questions.subject: U.S. History
questions.stem: This cartoon is portraying a situation that resulted from the

choices.text: public rejection of the New Deal
failure of the Civilian Conservation Corps
economic downturn of the late 1920s
opposition to the Treaty of Versailles

questions.explanation: The Stock Market Crash of October 1929 triggered the Great Depression, wiping out billions in paper wealth, collapsing thousands of banks, and plunging unemployment from 3% to 25% by 1933. The cartoon depicting this economic collapse—typically showing breadlines, foreclosures, or bank failures—reflects the devastating human consequences of the worst economic crisis in American history. Regents questions connect the Depression's causes (speculative bubble, overproduction, tight credit) to the political context that brought FDR and the New Deal to power.
questions.sourceIssue: Stimulus image was reused by a different released Regents source; hidden until re-imported from the original PDF.

questions.subject: U.S. History
questions.stem: What is the main idea of the cartoon?

choices.text: President Franklin D. Roosevelt is determined to supply the United States military and its allies with whatever it takes to defeat the Axis powers.
The federal government will most likely need to seize ownership of manufacturing plants.
President Franklin D. Roosevelt expects other nations to supply the same amount of armaments as the United States.
United States factories will not be able to manufacture military supplies in sufficient quantities.

questions.explanation: The 'Arsenal of Democracy' cartoon depicts FDR determined to supply the Allies (Britain, France, the Soviet Union) with war materiel despite not yet entering the war directly. The Lend-Lease Act (March 1941) formalized this, allowing the U.S. to 'lend or lease' weapons and supplies to any nation whose defense the president deemed vital to U.S. security—a policy FDR compared to lending a neighbor a garden hose to put out a fire. Lend-Lease ended U.S. neutrality in practice, if not in law, months before Pearl Harbor brought the U.S. into the war.
questions.sourceIssue: Question text depends on a source image, but no verified stimulus crop is available.

questions.subject: U.S. History
questions.stem: Photographs and posters showing scenes similar to these were used by the federal government to

choices.text: support the goal of equal pay for equal work
discourage women from taking jobs from men with families
encourage women to accept combat roles
recruit women to fill wartime manufacturing jobs

questions.explanation: 'Rosie the Riveter' and similar wartime propaganda images celebrated women who took factory jobs vacated by men serving in the military, particularly in aircraft, shipbuilding, and munitions manufacturing. The federal government and private industry used posters, films, and photographs to recruit women and present factory work as a patriotic duty during World War II (1941–1945). While many women were laid off when veterans returned after the war, the experience challenged gender norms and planted seeds for the women's rights movement of the 1960s.
questions.sourceIssue: Question text depends on a source image, but no verified stimulus crop is available.

questions.subject: U.S. History
questions.stem: Which conclusion can be drawn from an examination of these 1947 newspaper headlines concerning O Jackie Robinson?

choices.text: Robinson's major league debut had no impact on race relations in the United States.
Sports fans overwhelmingly encouraged the desegregation of public accommodations.
Robinson's integration of major league baseball was an important event in the history of civil rights.
Robinson went on to organize the civil rights movement.

questions.explanation: Jackie Robinson broke Major League Baseball's color barrier on April 15, 1947, when he took the field for the Brooklyn Dodgers—the first Black player in the major leagues since the color line was drawn in the 1880s. The 1947 newspaper headlines reflect both celebration of this milestone and the fierce resistance Robinson faced, including death threats, racist taunts from opposing players, and initial resistance from some teammates. Robinson's integration of baseball preceded the landmark civil rights legislation of the 1950s and 1960s and is studied as an early signal of the coming civil rights revolution.
questions.sourceIssue: Question text depends on a source image, but no verified stimulus crop is available.

questions.subject: U.S. History
questions.stem: These illustrations were used during World War II to encourage women to

choices.text: donate food to help feed the Allied forces
conserve household products to support the war effort
raise revenue by buying war bonds
support the war effort by working in defense industries

questions.explanation: During World War II, the federal government asked American civilians on the home front to conserve rationed goods—including rubber, gasoline, sugar, meat, nylon, and metal—to support military production. The Office of Price Administration (OPA) rationed these materials, and posters and radio broadcasts urged patriotic conservation. These illustrations show women as active participants in the war effort on the home front, a theme the Regents connects to both wartime mobilization and the long-term impact on women's roles in American society.
questions.sourceIssue: Stimulus image was reused by a different released Regents source; hidden until re-imported from the original PDF.

questions.subject: U.S. History
questions.stem: President Franklin D. Roosevelt addressed the situation described in this speech by

choices.text: supporting programs to aid the poor and unemployed
reducing the influence of labor unions
promoting the interests of big business
adopting the trickle-down economic theory

questions.explanation: When FDR took office in March 1933, the United States was in the depths of the Great Depression—25% unemployment, bank panics, farm foreclosures, and mass poverty. Roosevelt responded with the New Deal: the First Hundred Days produced emergency banking legislation, the CCC, the AAA, the NIRA, and other programs providing relief, recovery, and reform. The New Deal represented a fundamental shift from Hoover's belief in voluntary self-correction to Roosevelt's belief that the federal government had an obligation to actively intervene to protect citizens' economic welfare.
questions.sourceIssue: Question text depends on an unshown released source, but no verified stimulus crop is available.

questions.subject: U.S. History
questions.stem: One positive outcome of the situation shown on the map was that the United States and Soviet Union increased

choices.text: joint efforts to end the cruel practices of Cuba's leaders
communication between the two nations to avert war
military control of their Latin American colonies
cooperative humanitarian efforts in Caribbean nations suffering from natural disasters

questions.explanation: The Cuban Missile Crisis (1962) ended when Kennedy and Khrushchev reached a secret agreement: the Soviets would remove missiles from Cuba; the U.S. would pledge not to invade Cuba and quietly remove Jupiter missiles from Turkey. A positive outcome was the establishment of the Moscow-Washington 'hotline' (1963)—a direct communication link between the two leaders to prevent accidental nuclear war—and the beginning of a period of cautious dialogue that would eventually lead to the SALT arms control agreements. This crisis demonstrated both the dangers and the potential for diplomatic communication during the Cold War.
questions.sourceIssue: Stimulus image was reused by a different released Regents source; hidden until re-imported from the original PDF.

questions.subject: U.S. History
questions.stem: These statements illustrate a difference in opinion between the two presidents over

choices.text: promoting free-trade policies in the Western Hemisphere
regulating supply and demand
expanding the federal government's role in the economy
granting subsidies to big business

questions.explanation: During the Great Depression, Hoover believed the federal government should not directly intervene in the economy, relying instead on voluntary cooperation and local charity; Roosevelt believed the government had a direct responsibility to provide relief, recovery, and reform through active federal programs. This fundamental disagreement about the proper role of the federal government in the economy is one of the central tensions in U.S. political history. The Regents uses paired presidential quotes to test students' ability to identify ideological differences on government's economic role.

questions.subject: U.S. History
questions.stem: One result of the situation shown on the graphs is that the United States

choices.text: relies mainly on European nations for its energy
must consider its need for energy when making foreign policy decisions
must find markets for its surplus energy
leads the world in the production of oil and steel

questions.explanation: The oil dependency graphs illustrate that the U.S. has been a net energy importer since the early 1970s, when domestic oil production peaked and OPEC's 1973 oil embargo exposed America's vulnerability to foreign oil supply disruptions. This dependence on Middle Eastern oil has shaped U.S. foreign policy decisions—from maintaining military presence in the Persian Gulf to supporting the Camp David Accords—as energy security became a key national interest. The Regents connects economic data on energy to foreign policy decision-making as an example of how domestic needs shape international relations.
questions.sourceIssue: Stimulus image was reused by a different released Regents source; hidden until re-imported from the original PDF.

questions.subject: U.S. History
questions.stem: The map shows that the Allied forces

choices.text: had military support from the Soviet Union throughout the war
made slow but steady progress in defeating Japan
maintained control of the Philippines from the beginning of the war
conducted most of the battles north of the Hawaiian Islands

questions.explanation: The Allied 'island-hopping' campaign in the Pacific (1942–1945) involved capturing strategically important Japanese-held islands—Guadalcanal, Tarawa, Saipan, Iwo Jima, Okinawa—while bypassing heavily fortified Japanese strongholds, gradually advancing Allied air and naval bases toward the Japanese home islands. Each island battle was costly in American and Japanese lives, and the map shows the slow but systematic progression toward Japan. The atomic bombings of Hiroshima (August 6, 1945) and Nagasaki (August 9, 1945) ended the war before a planned invasion of Japan's home islands.
questions.sourceIssue: Stimulus image was reused by a different released Regents source; hidden until re-imported from the original PDF.

questions.subject: U.S. History
questions.stem: What event most directly led to the passage of this act?

choices.text: imposition of the Arab oil embargo
President Nixon's visit to Ch ina
President Kennedy's response to the Cuban missile crisis
involvement in the Vietnam War

questions.explanation: The War Powers Resolution (1973) was passed over President Nixon's veto in direct response to frustration over the Vietnam War (1964–1973), during which presidents Johnson and Nixon committed hundreds of thousands of troops and conducted massive bombing campaigns without a formal declaration of war. The Resolution requires the president to notify Congress within 48 hours of committing armed forces and limits unauthorized deployments to 60 days. It represented Congress's attempt to reclaim its constitutional war-making authority after years of executive overreach.

questions.subject: U.S. History
questions.stem: A historian would make best use of this cartoon to study which topic?

choices.text: the United States as a world power
United States entry into World War
the purchase of Alaska
the Open Door policy

questions.explanation: The cartoon showing a large American figure (often depicted as Uncle Sam or a big-stick-wielding Roosevelt) looming over Latin America reflects the imperial foreign policy of the early 20th century, particularly the Roosevelt Corollary (1904) and the 'Big Stick' diplomacy that justified U.S. military interventions throughout the Caribbean. A historian studying 'the United States as a world power' would use this cartoon to analyze how American expansion beyond its borders was depicted and justified at the time. This theme connects to the Spanish-American War, Panama Canal acquisition, and the Monroe Doctrine's evolution.
questions.sourceIssue: Question text depends on a source image, but no verified stimulus crop is available.

questions.subject: U.S. History
questions.stem: According to the passage, what is one argument in support of free speech during wartime?

choices.text: Citizens must maintain their right to control the government even in times of war.
Citizens must support the decisions of elected officials.
Military leaders are bound by constitutional oath to defend the rights of the people.
Congress has the authority to pass laws limiting first amendment rights.

questions.explanation: The passage arguing for free speech during wartime likely reflects a civil libertarian perspective like that of Eugene Debs or the ACLU, challenging the Espionage Act (1917) and Sedition Act (1918) that criminalized criticism of the draft and the war. The argument—that citizens must retain the right to criticize government even in wartime because democracy requires informed consent—was rejected by the Supreme Court in Schenck v. United States (1919), which upheld wartime speech restrictions under the 'clear and present danger' test. The Regents uses this debate to test students' understanding of First Amendment limits during national emergencies.
questions.sourceIssue: Question text depends on a source image, but no verified stimulus crop is available.

questions.subject: U.S. History
questions.stem: The purpose of this law was to

choices.text: limit the power of the president from involving the United States in extended wars
expand the power of the president as commander in chief
allow the president to declare war on foreign countries
prevent the president from signing a treaty without the consent of Congress

questions.explanation: The War Powers Resolution (1973) limits the president's ability to engage U.S. armed forces in sustained combat without congressional authorization, requiring the president to notify Congress within 48 hours of deploying forces and to withdraw them within 60 days unless Congress authorizes continued action. Passed over Nixon's veto as a reaction to the Vietnam War's undeclared expansion under both Johnson and Nixon, the Resolution represents Congress's most significant attempt to reassert its constitutional power to declare war. Presidents of both parties have challenged the Resolution's constitutionality, but no definitive Supreme Court ruling has resolved the dispute.
questions.sourceIssue: Question text depends on an unshown released source, but no verified stimulus crop is available.

questions.subject: U.S. History
questions.stem: Why were the writers of the United States Constitution willing to compromise on these issues?

choices.text: to end slavery after 1808
to give Northern states an advantage in the House of Representatives
to make approval of the Bill of Rights easier
to gain Southern support for ratification

questions.explanation: Southern slave states would only agree to ratify the Constitution if they received protection for their economic interests in slavery, including the Three-Fifths Compromise (counting enslaved persons for representation), the continuation of the slave trade until 1808, and the Fugitive Slave Clause. Without these compromises, the slave states would have rejected the Constitution, leaving the nation fragmented and the union impossible. The Regents tests students' understanding that the Constitution was a product of political compromise, not ideological purity.

questions.subject: U.S. History
questions.stem: Which later action by President Lincoln demonstrated a change from his 1861 position?

choices.text: issuing the Emancipation Proclamation
signing the Pacific Railway Act
suspending habeas corpus
appointing William Seward Secretary of State

questions.explanation: Lincoln's First Inaugural Address (March 1861) pledged not to interfere with slavery in states where it existed, hoping to prevent secession and keep border states loyal to the Union. The Emancipation Proclamation (January 1, 1863) represented a fundamental change in Lincoln's stated position: he now used his war powers as commander in chief to declare enslaved people in rebel states free, transforming the war's moral character. This shift reflected Lincoln's recognition that emancipation was both a military necessity and a moral imperative as the war's cost in human life mounted.

questions.subject: U.S. History
questions.stem: President Franklin D. Roosevelt responded to the circumstances described in this passage by

choices.text: renewing New Deal programs to help big business
raising taxes to assure a balanced federal budget
stressing free market economic principles
increasing New Deal efforts for relief and reform

questions.explanation: FDR's Second New Deal (1935–1936) intensified the relief and reform programs after critics from the left (Huey Long, Father Coughlin) and right (business leaders, the Supreme Court) attacked the First New Deal. Key programs included the Social Security Act (1935), the Wagner Act (National Labor Relations Act), and the Works Progress Administration—all responding to demands for greater economic security and worker protections. The Regents tests students' understanding of how political pressure from multiple directions shaped the evolution of New Deal policy.
questions.sourceIssue: Stimulus image was reused by a different released Regents source; hidden until re-imported from the original PDF.

questions.subject: U.S. History
questions.stem: The low level of immigration between 1930 and 1945 is most directly related to

choices.text: the Great Depression and World War II
passage of the Chinese Exclusion Act
improvements in living standards in Europe and Asia
the Cold War and the civil rights movement

questions.explanation: Immigration fell sharply between 1930 and 1945 for two reinforcing reasons: the Great Depression (1929–1941) made the U.S. economically unattractive and led to mass deportations of immigrants (especially Mexican Americans), while World War II (1939–1945) disrupted transatlantic travel and trapped potential immigrants in occupied Europe. The graph shows the lowest immigration levels since the colonial era, reflecting how both economic crisis and global war can simultaneously choke off migration flows. Regents data-analysis questions ask students to identify multiple causes for a single historical trend.

questions.subject: U.S. History
questions.stem: Which United States foreign policy was most often used to carry out the actions shown on the map?

choices.text: Roosevelt Corollary to the Monroe Doctrine
Wilson's Fourteen Points
Kellogg-Briand Pact
Open Door

questions.explanation: The Roosevelt Corollary (1904) gave the U.S. justification for military intervention throughout the Caribbean and Central America whenever a Latin American nation was unable or unwilling to meet its international financial obligations. The map of U.S. military interventions in this period shows the direct application of this policy in Cuba, Haiti, Nicaragua, the Dominican Republic, and Panama. The Good Neighbor Policy (1933) under FDR formally ended the era of direct intervention, pledging the U.S. would not interfere in the internal affairs of Latin American nations.

questions.subject: U.S. History
questions.stem: This cartoonist is expressing a concern that the current Social Security system

choices.text: does not cover most American workers
faces threats from future increases in the number of recipients
imposes taxes which are too high
does not support a decent standard of living

questions.explanation: The Social Security system is funded by a pay-as-you-go model: current workers' payroll taxes fund current retirees' benefits. As the Baby Boom generation (born 1946–1964) retires and life expectancy increases, the ratio of workers to retirees will shrink, straining the system's finances. The cartoon expresses concern that a growing retiree population will make Social Security's long-term solvency unsustainable without either tax increases, benefit cuts, or fundamental reform.
questions.sourceIssue: Question text depends on a source image, but no verified stimulus crop is available.

questions.subject: U.S. History
questions.stem: The alleged danger referred to in this passage contributed to the

choices.text: elimination of the military draft
ratification of the Treaty of Versailles
changing of United States immigration policy
failure of the American Federation of Labor

questions.explanation: Post-World War I fears of immigrant radicalism—fueled by the Bolshevik Revolution (1917), anarchist bombings, and labor strikes—contributed to the Red Scare (1919–1921) and directly influenced Congress to pass restrictive immigration laws. The Emergency Quota Act (1921) and the National Origins Act (1924) reduced total immigration and heavily favored Northern and Western European nations over Southern and Eastern European countries, explicitly targeting the 'new immigrants' from Italy, Poland, Russia, and the Austro-Hungarian Empire. This nativist backlash reflected both economic anxiety and ethnic prejudice.
questions.sourceIssue: Stimulus image was reused by a different released Regents source; hidden until re-imported from the original PDF.

questions.subject: U.S. History
questions.stem: What was a result of the events reflected in these magazine covers?

choices.text: Many Americans lost trust in their government.
The Supreme Court ruled in favor of the president's actions.Y
Freedom of the press was limited.
President Richard Nixon was defeated in his bid for reelection.

questions.explanation: The Watergate scandal (1972–1974)—in which President Nixon's campaign operatives broke into the Democratic National Committee headquarters and Nixon covered it up—and the Vietnam War (1964–1975)—in which successive administrations misled the public about the war's prospects—each destroyed public trust in government. The 'credibility gap' that opened between official statements and reality in Vietnam was compounded by Watergate's revelation that the president himself had committed crimes. Gallup polls from the mid-1970s showed a collapse in public trust in government that lingered for decades.

questions.subject: U.S. History
questions.stem: What was the immediate cause of the action taken by the United States that is shown on the map?

choices.text: United States military bases in the Caribbean were closed by Cuban armed forces.
Cuban refugees lobbied President Dwight Eisenhower to overthrow Fidel Castro.
The Soviet Union built nuclear missile launch sites within range of United States cities.
Puerto Rican citizens asked Congress to assist them in repelling communist advances.

questions.explanation: The Cuban Missile Crisis map shows the U.S. naval quarantine zone around Cuba (October 1962) established after American U-2 spy planes photographed Soviet nuclear missile launch sites under construction on the island. The Soviet placement of missiles in Cuba—capable of striking major U.S. cities with minimal warning time—was the direct provocation that led Kennedy to impose the quarantine and demand their removal. The crisis was resolved when the Soviets agreed to remove the missiles in exchange for a U.S. non-invasion pledge and a secret agreement to remove American missiles from Turkey.

questions.subject: U.S. History
questions.stem: What is the best title for this map?

choices.text: Territorial Expansion
Immigration Before the Civil War
Eastward Migration
Results of the Revolution

questions.explanation: The map showing U.S. territorial growth from 1783 to 1853 illustrates how the young nation expanded from the original 13 states to a continental republic spanning the Pacific through purchases (Louisiana 1803, Florida 1819, Gadsden 1853), negotiated agreements (Oregon 1846), annexation (Texas 1845), and military conquest (Mexican Cession 1848). 'Territorial Expansion' captures this systematic growth, which was justified by the ideology of Manifest Destiny—the belief that the U.S. was destined to extend its democratic institutions across the continent.
