questions.subject: U.S. History
questions.stem: After the Watergate scandal of the 1970s, Congress made an effort to deal with the problem shown in this cartoon by

choices.text: banning wealthy individuals from financing their own campaigns
requiring that all elections be financed by the federal government
limiting donations to campaigns by individuals and political action committees
requiring all candidates to spend the same amount on campaigns

questions.explanation: The Watergate scandal revealed the danger of large, unregulated political contributions: Nixon's Committee to Re-Elect the President received millions in illegal contributions, some of which funded the break-in. Congress responded with the Federal Election Campaign Act amendments (1974), which established the Federal Election Commission, limited individual campaign contributions, and required public disclosure of donors. The Regents uses Watergate to illustrate how political scandals can lead to structural reforms in campaign finance law.

questions.subject: U.S. History
questions.stem: Which conclusion is most clearly supported by the information in the time line?

choices.text: Most Americans are opposed to government intervention in the economy.
Government intervention in the economy has limited the growth of the gross domestic product.
The United States government has shown little interest in regula ting big business.
United States economic policies have been modified to meet changing conditions.

questions.explanation: U.S. economic policy has consistently adapted to changing national circumstances: from Alexander Hamilton's mercantile financial plan (1790s), to 19th-century laissez-faire, to Progressive-era regulation (Sherman Act, Federal Reserve), to New Deal interventionism (1933–1941), to post-war mixed economy and welfare state, to Reagan-era deregulation (1981+). The timeline illustrates that there is no single fixed U.S. economic philosophy—policy has swung between intervention and laissez-faire in response to crises, elections, and changing economic conditions. Regents questions test students' ability to read timelines and identify patterns of change over time.

questions.subject: U.S. History
questions.stem: The constitutional amendments referred to in this statement were ratified to

choices.text: end Rthe importation of slaves
increase federal revenue
institute national Prohibition
provide legal rights to African Americans

questions.explanation: The 13th Amendment (1865) abolished slavery; the 14th Amendment (1868) granted citizenship and equal protection under law to all persons born or naturalized in the U.S.; and the 15th Amendment (1870) prohibited denying the right to vote on the basis of race, color, or previous condition of servitude. Together, the Reconstruction Amendments were designed to provide African Americans with full legal equality following the Civil War and the abolition of slavery. Despite these amendments, Southern states used Jim Crow laws, violence, and disenfranchisement tactics to circumvent their protections for nearly a century.

questions.subject: U.S. History
questions.stem: Which action by the federal government was a step toward the approach to business favored by Speaker B?

choices.text: passage of the Sherman Antitrust Act
decision by the Supreme Court in United States v. E. C. Knight Co.
Ecreation of the civil service system
passage of the Transcontinental Railway Act

questions.explanation: The debate between 'Speaker A' (laissez-faire) and 'Speaker B' (pro-regulation) about industrial monopolies reflects the Progressive-era conflict over whether the federal government should intervene in the economy to protect workers and consumers from corporate abuse. The Sherman Antitrust Act (1890) was the federal government's first major regulatory step toward Speaker B's position, though its vague language limited its effectiveness until Roosevelt aggressively enforced it after 1901. This question tests students' ability to connect a philosophical position about government's role to specific historical legislation.

questions.subject: U.S. History
questions.stem: Which attitude about Cold War rivalries is expressed by President Ronald Reagan in this speech?

choices.text: The United States has made no attempt to pursue peaceful coexistence with communist nations.
Actions taken by communist nations to secure their borders have brought stability to Europe.
Communist nations, not western democracies, have threatened world peace.
Efforts to reduce the nuclear arms race have resulted in lower defense spending.

questions.explanation: In his 1983 'Evil Empire' speech, President Reagan cast the Cold War as a moral struggle between American freedom and Soviet tyranny, rejecting détente in favor of vigorous ideological and military competition. Reagan argued that Western moral weakness and appeasement had allowed Soviet aggression, not that Western democracies themselves threatened peace. This framing—communism as the aggressor, democracy as the defender—justified Reagan's massive military buildup and support for anti-communist insurgencies in Afghanistan, Nicaragua, and Angola.
questions.sourceIssue: Question text depends on an unshown released source, but no verified stimulus crop is available.

questions.subject: U.S. History
questions.stem: Which conclusion can be drawn from an examination of these 1947 newspaper headlines concerning O Jackie Robinson?

choices.text: Robinson's integration of major league baseball was an important event in the history of civil rights.
Robinson went on to organize the civil rights movement.
Sports fans overwhelmingly encouraged the desegregation of public accommodations.

Robinson's major league debut had no im pact on race relations in the United States. 36 "... From Stettin in the Baltic to Trieste in the Adriatic, an iron curtain has descended across the Continent...."

questions.explanation: Jackie Robinson's signing by Branch Rickey of the Brooklyn Dodgers in 1945 and his first game in April 1947 made him the first Black player in Major League Baseball since the informal color line was established in the 1880s. The 1947 headlines documenting both the excitement and resistance surrounding Robinson's integration demonstrate that sports can serve as a leading edge of social change, breaking barriers before civil rights legislation. Robinson's courage in facing racial abuse while excelling on the field made him a powerful symbol of the civil rights struggle.
questions.sourceIssue: Question text depends on a source image, but no verified stimulus crop is available.

questions.subject: U.S. History
questions.stem: Based on the information provided by the cartoon, President Theodore Roosevelt's goal was to

choices.text: assist businesses in resisting interference by investors
establish worker safety regulations in factories
persuade businesses to accept nationalization
use federal power to control monopolies

questions.explanation: The Roosevelt 'trustbuster' cartoon—typically showing TR wielding a 'Big Stick' against corporate trusts—reflects his use of the Sherman Antitrust Act (1890) and executive power to break up industrial monopolies. Roosevelt filed 44 antitrust suits, notably against Northern Securities (1902) and Standard Oil (1906), on the principle that concentrated corporate power threatened democratic equality. Unlike his successor Taft, who prosecuted trusts more mechanically, Roosevelt sought to distinguish between 'good' trusts (efficiently large) and 'bad' trusts (abusively monopolistic).
questions.sourceIssue: Question text depends on a source image, but no verified stimulus crop is available.

questions.subject: U.S. History
questions.stem: Which statement most accurately expresses the main idea of the cartoon?

choices.text: The Marshall Plan will bankrupt the American taxpayer. - President John F. Kennedy, Inaugural Address, January 20, 1961
The Marshall Plan will limit Europe's economic recovery.
American taxpayers hope the Marshall Plan will quickly stabilize Europe's economy.
Europe will not take advantage of the Marshall Plan.

questions.explanation: The Marshall Plan cartoon shows American taxpayers as invested in the plan's success, reflecting the argument that a prosperous democratic Europe would both advance U.S. strategic interests (containing communism) and create export markets for American goods. Secretary Marshall carefully framed the plan as open to all European nations—including the USSR—to avoid the appearance of targeting the Soviet Union, though Moscow rejected it. The plan succeeded beyond expectations, with Western European GDP returning to pre-war levels by 1951.
questions.sourceIssue: Question text depends on a source image, but no verified stimulus crop is available.

questions.subject: U.S. History
questions.stem: The United States government published these World War I posters to encourage Americans to

choices.text: conserve scarce resources for the military
invest in the war effort
reduce the use of consumer goods
increase military enlistments

questions.explanation: During World War I, the federal government used Liberty Bond drives—asking citizens to buy government bonds to finance the war—accompanied by patriotic posters designed by artists including Howard Chandler Christy and James Montgomery Flagg. These poster campaigns appealed to patriotism, fear of German militarism, and civic duty to persuade Americans to invest in the war effort. The bond drives raised $21.5 billion for the war and represent an early example of the government mobilizing mass media for wartime propaganda purposes.
questions.sourceIssue: Stimulus image was reused by a different released Regents source; hidden until re-imported from the original PDF.

questions.subject: U.S. History
questions.stem: Which event led to this letter being written?

choices.text: Teapot Dome scandal
Kent State shootings
Iran-Contra affair
Watergate break-in

questions.explanation: The Watergate break-in (June 17, 1972), in which operatives of Nixon's reelection campaign broke into the Democratic National Committee headquarters at the Watergate complex in Washington, D.C., triggered the constitutional crisis that led to Nixon's resignation. Nixon's subsequent efforts to obstruct the FBI investigation—documented in White House tape recordings—led the House Judiciary Committee to approve articles of impeachment in July 1974. Rather than face certain impeachment and removal, Nixon resigned on August 9, 1974—the only U.S. president to resign from office.
questions.sourceIssue: Question text depends on a source letter, but no verified stimulus crop is available.

questions.subject: U.S. History
questions.stem: What has been the principal cause of the population changes shown on the map?

choices.text: continued westward migration
employment opportunities in northeastern states
movement from farms to cities
enactment of immigration quotas

questions.explanation: The post-World War II era saw continued westward population movement as Americans followed economic opportunities in the Sunbelt states (California, Texas, Arizona, Florida), driven by the aerospace, defense, and technology industries, air conditioning making hot climates livable, and federal highway construction that opened suburban development. This demographic shift, documented in U.S. Census maps, has dramatically redistributed political power from the Northeast and Midwest to the South and West. The Regents connects population data to political consequences like the reapportionment of congressional seats.
questions.sourceIssue: Question text depends on a source image, but no verified stimulus crop is available.

questions.subject: U.S. History
questions.stem: Which conclusion is most clearly supported by the information provided on the graph?

choices.text: Unemployment after the New Deal was the same as before the stock market crash.
New Deal programs only partially relieved unemployment.
World War II increased unemployment.
Deficit spending ended unemployment.

questions.explanation: The unemployment graph for the New Deal era shows that while Roosevelt's programs reduced unemployment from 25% (1933) to about 14% (1937), a premature effort to balance the budget in 1937–1938 triggered a sharp recession, pushing unemployment back up to 19%. Full employment was only achieved with wartime mobilization after Pearl Harbor (1941). This data demonstrates that the New Deal provided important relief and partial recovery, but full economic recovery required the massive government spending of World War II—a conclusion economists have long debated.
questions.sourceIssue: Question text depends on a source image, but no verified stimulus crop is available.

questions.subject: U.S. History
questions.stem: The policy described by Secretary Dulles was most evident in the later actions of the United States during the

choices.text: Cuban missile crisis
Nuclear Test Ban Treaty
energy crisis of 1973
Camp David talks between Egypt and Israel $

questions.explanation: Secretary of State Dulles' 'brinkmanship' strategy—pushing confrontations to the edge of nuclear war to force Soviet capitulation—was tested during the Cuban Missile Crisis (October 1962), when Kennedy imposed a naval quarantine on Cuba and threatened military action to force the removal of Soviet nuclear missiles. The 13-day standoff demonstrated both the terrifying logic and the ultimate limitations of brinkmanship: the crisis was resolved not by nuclear escalation but by quiet diplomacy and mutual face-saving compromises. Regents questions connect Dulles's Cold War doctrine to its specific application in crisis situations.

questions.subject: U.S. History
questions.stem: Which later presidential program is most similar to the idea expressed in this passage?

choices.text: President George W. Bush's War on Terror in the 2000s
President Ronald Reagan's supply-side fiscal policy in the 1980s
President Jimmy Carter's Camp David Accords in the 1970s
President Lyndon B. Johnson's Great Society in the 1960s

questions.explanation: FDR's proposal for a 'second bill of rights' (1944) or similar New Deal legislative vision—guaranteeing economic security as a fundamental right—directly inspired Lyndon B. Johnson's Great Society programs of the 1960s, which created Medicare (health insurance for the elderly), Medicaid, federal education aid, housing programs, and anti-poverty initiatives. Both FDR and LBJ shared the progressive conviction that government had an obligation to protect citizens from the insecurities of poverty, illness, and unemployment. The Regents connects these two eras of liberal reform to test students' understanding of ideological continuity in Democratic Party policy.
questions.sourceIssue: Question text depends on a source image, but no verified stimulus crop is available.

questions.subject: U.S. History
questions.stem: This 1950 "Declaration of Conscience" speech by Senator Smith was written in response to

choices.text: President Harry Truman's integration of the armed forces
President Dwight D. Eisenhower's support for the French in Indochina
Senator Joseph McCarthy's unproven accusations of treason
General Douglas MacArthur's military stra tegy in Korea

questions.explanation: Senator Margaret Chase Smith's 'Declaration of Conscience' (June 1950) was the first significant Senate speech challenging Joseph McCarthy's anti-communist smear tactics, calling on the Senate to restore its dignity by renouncing 'fear, ignorance, bigotry, and smear.' Smith accused unnamed colleagues of 'selfish political exploitation of fear, bigotry, ignorance, and intolerance.' McCarthy responded by removing Smith from her subcommittee post, but her speech inspired later critics and contributed to the anti-McCarthy coalition that emerged after the Army-McCarthy Hearings (1954).

questions.subject: U.S. History
questions.stem: Which statement best represents a reason why this protest was considered controversial?

choices.text: Suffragists were picketing while German submarines were attacki ng American ships.
The country was dealing with public outrage over government corruption.
The Senate had already repealed the Prohibition amendment.
President Wilson did not include women's demands in his Fourteen Points.

questions.explanation: During World War I, women suffragists picketed the White House with banners quoting Wilson's war rhetoric ('democracy should not be denied to those who fight for it'), which critics saw as hypocritical and unpatriotic given that American soldiers were dying fighting Germany. The National Woman's Party's 'Silent Sentinels' were arrested for 'obstructing traffic' and imprisoned at Occoquan Workhouse, where they staged hunger strikes and suffered forced feedings. Despite—or because of—the controversy, their persistence contributed to Wilson's eventual endorsement of the 19th Amendment in 1918.

questions.subject: U.S. History
questions.stem: What is one reason President Abraham Lincoln included these statements in this address?

choices.text: to encourage the expansion of slavery into the West
to increase support of northern abolitionists
to reestablish the foreign slave trade
to reduce the fears of slave-holding states

questions.explanation: Lincoln's First Inaugural Address (March 4, 1861) opened by pledging not to interfere with slavery in states where it already existed, explicitly citing the Constitution and the Republican Party platform to reassure Southern states that their 'peculiar institution' was safe under his presidency. This reassurance was designed to prevent border states (Maryland, Kentucky, Missouri, Delaware) from seceding and to hold the Upper South in the Union. Lincoln's primary goal at this point was preserving the Union, not abolishing slavery—a priority that would shift as the war progressed.
questions.sourceIssue: Question text depends on an unshown released source, but no verified stimulus crop is available.

questions.subject: U.S. History
questions.stem: The purpose of this law was to

choices.text: limit the power of the president from involving the United States in extended wars
expand the power of the president as commander in chief
allow the president to declare war on foreign countries
prevent the president from signing a treaty without the consent of Congress

questions.explanation: The War Powers Resolution (1973), passed over Nixon's veto, requires the president to notify Congress within 48 hours of committing U.S. forces to hostilities and to withdraw them within 60 days absent congressional authorization for continued deployment. The law was a direct congressional response to the Vietnam War, during which both Johnson and Nixon committed hundreds of thousands of troops and dramatically expanded the war into Cambodia and Laos without formal declarations of war. Every president since Nixon has challenged the Resolution's constitutionality while nominally complying with its reporting requirements.

questions.subject: U.S. History
questions.stem: Which claim is best supported by the views of President Thomas Jeffers on as stated in his second Yinaugural address?

choices.text: The United States lacks the funds to purchase the Louisiana Territory.
The purchase of Louisiana greatly benefits the United States.
The United States Constitution forbids buying land.
The Spanish would support United States westward expansion.

questions.explanation: Jefferson's Second Inaugural Address (1805) and his broader writings about the Louisiana Purchase (1803) reflected his satisfaction that doubling the nation's territory would provide 'room enough for our descendants to the thousandth and thousandth generation,' fulfilling his vision of an 'Empire of Liberty' of independent yeoman farmers. Jefferson had actually agonized over the constitutionality of the purchase (nothing in the Constitution explicitly authorized buying territory), but concluded the national interest outweighed strict constructionist scruples. The Regents tests this tension between Jefferson's constitutional philosophy and his presidential actions.

questions.subject: U.S. History
questions.stem: What was a main reason for the movement of people as shown on this map?

choices.text: Foreign food imports replaced domestic production of crops.
Abolitionists promised a means of escape from slavery.
Industrialization provided more employment opportunities.
Northern states banned all forms of racial discrimination.

questions.explanation: The Great Migration (1910–1970) saw approximately six million Black Americans move from the rural South to Northern and Midwestern industrial cities—Chicago, Detroit, New York, Philadelphia—in two waves (1910–1940 and 1940–1970). The primary 'pull' factor was the availability of industrial jobs in steel mills, meatpacking plants, and automobile factories, which paid wages far above what sharecropping offered, while the 'push' factor was the violence, poverty, and political disenfranchisement of Jim Crow. The Regents connects migration data to themes of economic opportunity, urbanization, and the roots of the modern civil rights movement.
questions.sourceIssue: Question text depends on a source image, but no verified stimulus crop is available.

questions.subject: U.S. History
questions.stem: Which event did President Bush refer to in this quotation?

choices.text: start of Operation Iraqi Freedom
Oklahoma City bombing on April 17, 1995
Soviet invasion of Afghanistan
terrorist actions on September 11, 2001

questions.explanation: On September 11, 2001, al-Qaeda terrorists hijacked four commercial airliners, flying two into the World Trade Center towers in New York City, one into the Pentagon in Arlington, Virginia, and crashing one in a Pennsylvania field after passengers fought back. The attacks killed nearly 3,000 people and led directly to the USA PATRIOT Act (October 2001), the creation of the Department of Homeland Security (2002), and U.S. military operations in Afghanistan (2001) and Iraq (2003). President Bush cited 9/11 in every major national security address of his presidency, fundamentally reorienting U.S. foreign and domestic policy.
questions.sourceIssue: Stimulus image was reused by a different released Regents source; hidden until re-imported from the original PDF.

questions.subject: U.S. History
questions.stem: E One way in which President Abraham Lincoln’s suspension of habeas corpus (1861), the Espionage Act (1917), and the USA Patriot Act (2001) are similar is that these actions

choices.text: expanded political cooperation
enforced international treaties
restricted civil liberties during wartime
encouraged economic development

questions.explanation: Lincoln's suspension of habeas corpus (1861), which allowed indefinite military detention without trial, the Espionage Act (1917) and Sedition Act (1918), which criminalized anti-war speech, and the PATRIOT Act (2001), which expanded surveillance powers, all restricted civil liberties in the name of national security during wartime or military emergencies. This recurring pattern—civil liberties contracting during crises and partially recovering afterward—is a central theme in U.S. constitutional history. The Regents tests students' ability to identify this pattern across different historical periods using specific examples.

questions.subject: U.S. History
questions.stem: Which statement is a fact rather than an opinion?

choices.text: The power of judicial review was established by a Supreme Court decision.
Supreme Court Justices should have a mandatory retirement age.
The Supreme Court’s decision in Roe v. Wade is its most controversial decision.
The Supreme Court achieved its greatest accomplishments under the leadership of Chief Justice John Marshall.

questions.explanation: The fact that judicial review was established by a Supreme Court decision (Marbury v. Madison, 1803) is a factual, verifiable historical claim—not a value judgment—while other choices about whether judicial review is good or bad are opinions. Regents questions that ask students to distinguish fact from opinion test a core critical thinking skill: facts can be verified through evidence and historical documentation, while opinions reflect judgments about what is good, desirable, or appropriate. Marbury v. Madison is the most precisely documented and factually verifiable of the choices.

questions.subject: U.S. History
questions.stem: Which development has resulted from the creation of the North American Free Trade Agreement (NAFTA) and the World Trade Organization (WTO) in the 1990s?

choices.text: growth in membership of labor unions in the United States
more protective tariffs between the member nations
fewer industries leaving the United States
greater global economic interdependence

questions.explanation: The North American Free Trade Agreement (NAFTA, 1994) and the World Trade Organization (WTO, 1995) reduced trade barriers and created new rules governing international commerce, accelerating the integration of national economies into a global marketplace. NAFTA increased trade among the U.S., Canada, and Mexico to $1.3 trillion annually, but critics argued it cost American manufacturing jobs to lower-wage Mexican workers. The Regents connects these 1990s trade agreements to the theme of globalization and economic interdependence.

questions.subject: U.S. History
questions.stem: Based on this excerpt, President Ronald Reagan’s economic policy called for

choices.text: raising taxes to decrease inflation
reducing taxes to increase investment by private businesses
limiting military spending to balance the federal budget
increasing government spending on social Nwelfare programs

questions.explanation: President Reagan's supply-side economic policy—popularly called 'Reaganomics' or 'trickle-down economics'—was based on the theory that cutting taxes for businesses and high earners would stimulate investment, job creation, and economic growth that would benefit all Americans. The Economic Recovery Tax Act (1981) cut the top marginal income tax rate from 70% to 50% (eventually to 28%), while Reagan simultaneously increased defense spending and cut domestic social programs. Critics argued that the benefits primarily flowed to the wealthy, while the resulting federal deficits constrained future government investment.
questions.sourceIssue: Question text depends on a source image, but no verified stimulus crop is available.

questions.subject: U.S. History
questions.stem: Which action is an example of the use of checks and balances?

choices.text: choosing a vice presidential running mate by a presidential candida te
impeachment of President Bill Clinton
election of the Speaker of the House by the majority party
starting negotiations for a treaty by President George Washington

questions.explanation: The 1998 impeachment of President Bill Clinton by the House of Representatives on charges of perjury and obstruction of justice arising from the Monica Lewinsky affair is a textbook example of checks and balances: the House exercised its sole constitutional power of impeachment to formally accuse the president of high crimes and misdemeanors, and the Senate then conducted a trial at which Clinton was acquitted. The episode illustrates that checks and balances function even when used for partisan purposes, as the Republican-controlled House impeached a Democratic president. Clinton remained in office, and his approval ratings actually rose during the impeachment proceedings.

questions.subject: U.S. History
questions.stem: The division in public opinion over the Scopes trial and Prohibition demonstrates which characteristic of the 1920s?

choices.text: opposition to the Ku Klux Klan
clash of cultural values
fear of international communism
discrimination against immigrants

questions.explanation: The Scopes 'Monkey Trial' (1925)—in which Tennessee high school teacher John Scopes was prosecuted for teaching evolution in violation of state law—and Prohibition (1920–1933) both exposed a fundamental clash between modernist and traditionalist values in 1920s America. Rural, fundamentalist Christians saw both evolution and alcohol as symptoms of urban moral decay, while educated, urban progressives viewed both prosecutions as anti-scientific and anti-liberty. This cultural divide between 'old' and 'new' America—rural vs. urban, fundamentalist vs. modernist, nativist vs. immigrant—defines the 1920s as a decade of cultural conflict.

questions.subject: U.S. History
questions.stem: What was the goal of President Franklin D. Roosevelt’s plan to add more justices to the Supreme Court?

choices.text: to limit judicial opposition to New Deal programs
to help the Supreme Court implement its decisions
to replace the Chief Justice of the Supreme Court
to co nvince Congress to enact new economic laws

questions.explanation: President Roosevelt's 'court-packing plan' (1937) proposed adding up to six new justices to the Supreme Court—one for each sitting justice over age 70—to overcome the conservative 'Four Horsemen' who had been striking down New Deal programs as unconstitutional. Congress, alarmed by what they saw as an executive power grab threatening judicial independence, rejected the plan even though Democrats held commanding majorities. However, one justice (Owen Roberts) soon began voting to uphold New Deal legislation in what journalists called the 'switch in time that saved nine,' effectively solving Roosevelt's problem without the court-packing plan passing.

questions.subject: U.S. History
questions.stem: Senator Huey Long, Dr. Francis Townsend, and Father Charles Coughlin are best known as

choices.text: outspoken critics of President Franklin D. Roosevelt’s New Deal
members of President Franklin D. Roosevelt’s cabinet
members of the Supreme Court nominated by President Franklin D. Roosevelt
supporters of President Franklin D. Roosevelt’s reelection campaign in 1940

questions.explanation: Senator Huey Long (D-LA), Dr. Francis Townsend (advocate for old-age pensions), and Father Charles Coughlin (radio priest who evolved from FDR supporter to bitter critic) all argued from different angles that the New Deal was too timid and too friendly to bankers and corporations. Long's 'Share Our Wealth' movement proposed radical redistribution; Townsend wanted guaranteed pensions of $200/month for the elderly; Coughlin's National Union for Social Justice reflected populist Catholic suspicion of Wall Street. Their critiques pushed FDR toward the more ambitious Second New Deal (1935), including Social Security and the Wagner Act.

questions.subject: U.S. History
questions.stem: Which action by the federal government was a step toward the approach to business favored by O Speaker B?

choices.text: passage of the Transcontinental Railway Act
passage of the Sherman Antitrust Act
decision by the Supreme Court in United States v. E. C. Knight Co.
creation of the civil service system

questions.explanation: Speaker B, representing the Progressive reform position, would favor using antitrust law to break up monopolies that used their market dominance to harm consumers and workers. The Sherman Antitrust Act (1890) provided the legal tool, though it was only effectively used beginning with Roosevelt's Northern Securities suit (1902). This question tests students' ability to connect a philosophical argument about business regulation to the specific legislative measure (Sherman Act) that implemented that philosophy.

questions.subject: U.S. History
questions.stem: One foreign policy goal of President Theodore Roosevelt was to

choices.text: restrict European intervention in the Western Hemisphere
create future states by annexing Pacific island territories
limit United States investment in Latin America
encourage independence movements in Africa

questions.explanation: The Roosevelt Corollary (1904), an extension of the Monroe Doctrine, stated that the U.S. would intervene in Latin American nations to prevent European powers from using debt collection as a pretext for military intervention in the Western Hemisphere. This 'Big Stick' approach restricted European intervention by substituting U.S. intervention—a policy Roosevelt claimed fulfilled the Monroe Doctrine's original protective purpose. The Corollary was formally repudiated by FDR's Good Neighbor Policy (1933), which pledged non-intervention in Latin American affairs.

questions.subject: U.S. History
questions.stem: W. E. B. Du Bois and Booker T. Washington most strongly disagreed over which issue?

choices.text: methods to achieve racial equality
the constitutionality of the Sherman Antitrust Act
the construction of the Panama Canal
support for the Allies in World War

questions.explanation: Booker T. Washington (Atlanta Compromise speech, 1895) advocated vocational education and economic self-improvement as the practical path forward for Black Americans in the Jim Crow South, counseling acceptance of temporary political inequality as the price of white tolerance. W.E.B. Du Bois rejected this as 'submissiveness' and demanded immediate civil and political equality, founding the NAACP (1909) to pursue legal challenges to segregation. Their debate defined the strategic divide in the early civil rights movement between gradualism and immediate direct action.

questions.subject: U.S. History
questions.stem: The main idea of this 1937 cartoon about President Franklin D. Roosevelt is that the president is

choices.text: attempting to exercise too much influence over the Supreme Court
seeking the Supreme Court’s help against his political opponents
trying to increase the public’s respect for the Supreme Court
refusing to allow the Supreme Court to review legislation

questions.explanation: FDR's 1937 'court-packing' cartoon typically depicts him trying to stuff additional justices into an already-crowded Supreme Court building, criticizing his proposal to add six justices (one for each sitting justice over age 70) as a blatant attempt to pack the Court with New Deal sympathizers. The cartoon's point is that FDR was trying to use the appointment power to control the Court's decisions—a dangerous undermining of judicial independence and the separation of powers. Congress ultimately rejected the plan, though the Court subsequently began upholding New Deal legislation.
questions.sourceIssue: Question text depends on a source image, but no verified stimulus crop is available.

questions.subject: U.S. History
questions.stem: Based on the information provided by the cartoon, President Theodore Roosevelt’s goal was to

choices.text: establish worker safety regulations in factories
assist businesses in resisting interference by investors
use federal power to control monopolies
persuade businesses to accept nationalization

questions.explanation: Theodore Roosevelt's antitrust actions—filing 44 suits including the landmark Northern Securities case (1904) and Standard Oil case (1906)—were depicted in political cartoons as TR wielding a 'Big Stick' against corporate monopolists. Roosevelt believed in 'regulated capitalism': large corporations were not inherently evil, but those that abused their power to harm workers, consumers, or competitors deserved federal prosecution. This 'trustbusting' philosophy distinguished Roosevelt from his laissez-faire predecessors and made him the first president to actively use federal regulatory power against corporations.

questions.subject: U.S. History
questions.stem: This cartoon is portraying a situation that resulted from the

choices.text: failure of the Civilian Conservation Corps
public rejection of the New Deal
economic downturn of the late 1920s
opposition to the Treaty of Versailles

questions.explanation: The Great Depression cartoon (typically depicting breadlines, Hoovervilles, or the Dust Bowl) illustrates the devastating economic conditions following the 1929 stock market crash: unemployment peaked at 25%, banks failed by the thousands, and industrial production dropped by half. The 'economic downturn of the late 1920s' began with the crash in October 1929 but had deep roots in agricultural depression throughout the 1920s, overextended consumer credit, and structural weakness in banking. The Regents connects Depression imagery to its causes, impacts, and the political response (New Deal).
questions.sourceIssue: Question text depends on a source image, but no verified stimulus crop is available.

questions.subject: U.S. History
questions.stem: During World War I, President Woodrow Wilson used his wartime powers to

choices.text: expand freedom of the press
win passage of quota acts
increase government control of the economy
grant all women the right to vote

questions.explanation: During World War I (1917–1918), President Wilson used the Lever Food and Fuel Control Act (1917), the War Industries Board, the Railroad Administration, and other wartime agencies to bring large sectors of the economy under direct federal control: fixing prices, allocating raw materials, nationalizing railroads, and managing food production. This wartime mobilization of the economy represented the most extensive federal economic intervention in U.S. history to that point, setting precedents that FDR would draw on during the New Deal. After the war, these controls were dismantled, reflecting the temporary, emergency nature of wartime economic regulation.

questions.subject: U.S. History
questions.stem: Which constitutional power was most directly responsible for the action taken in this letter?

choices.text: power of the Senate to approve proposed treaties
power of the president to appoint justices to the Supreme Court
power of Congress to override vetoes
power of impeachment by the House of Representatives

questions.explanation: The constitutional power of impeachment vests solely in the House of Representatives (Article I, Section 2)—meaning only the House can formally charge a president, vice president, or other federal official with 'high crimes and misdemeanors.' The letter referenced (likely from the Nixon impeachment inquiry, 1974) would have been an official House Judiciary Committee communication invoking this power. The Senate then conducts the trial, presided over by the Chief Justice, and a two-thirds vote of the Senate is required for conviction and removal.
questions.sourceIssue: Question text depends on a source letter, but no verified stimulus crop is available.

questions.subject: U.S. History
questions.stem: How did Alexander Hamilton’s financial plan affect the economy of the United States during the 1790s?

choices.text: The newly created Bank of the United States helped stabilize the economy.
Treasury policies contributed to widespread inflation.
High tariffs increased foreign trade.
National tax revenues decreased.

questions.explanation: Alexander Hamilton's financial plan (1790–1791), implemented during Washington's first term, established the First Bank of the United States to stabilize the currency and manage government finances, assumed all state debts from the Revolution to build national credit, and created an excise tax on whiskey to fund the debt repayment. The Bank provided a sound currency and stable credit that facilitated commercial development and economic integration of the new nation. Jefferson and Madison opposed the Bank as unconstitutional (it had no explicit authorization), setting up the first major constitutional debate over federal implied powers.

questions.subject: U.S. History
questions.stem: After World War I, one way in which the Red Scare, the passing of the Quota Acts, and the growth of the Ku Klux Klan were similar is that they all

choices.text: encouraged the assimilation of new immigrants into American society
exhibited prejudice against African Americans
exploited fears about people who were considered un-American
supported the goals of the suffrage movement

questions.explanation: After World War I, the Red Scare (1919–1921)—with its Palmer Raids and deportations of suspected communists—the Immigration Quota Acts (1921, 1924)—targeting Southern and Eastern European immigrants—and the revived Ku Klux Klan (which peaked at 4–5 million members in the 1920s targeting Catholics, Jews, and immigrants, not just Black Americans) all exploited anxiety about social, ethnic, and ideological changes threatening 'traditional' American identity. All three represented nativist and xenophobic responses to the demographic and political changes accelerated by World War I. The Regents groups these events to test students' understanding of nativism as a recurring theme in American history.

questions.subject: U.S. History
questions.stem: During the 1920s, the trial of Sacco and Vanzetti, the Palmer raids, and the revival of the Ku Klux Klan demonstrate that

choices.text: popular support for international involvement was increasing
nativist sentiments were on the rise
cultural values were similar between urban and rural Americans
public support for limiting immigration was declining

questions.explanation: The 1921 Sacco and Vanzetti case—two Italian immigrant anarchists executed for murder in a trial many believed was influenced by their ethnicity and political beliefs—the Palmer Raids (1919–1920) targeting foreign-born radicals, and the Klan's resurgence all demonstrate that nativist fears of 'alien' influences reached a peak in the early 1920s. These incidents reflected widespread anxiety about immigration, communism, and cultural change, fueling support for the Immigration Act of 1924. The Regents groups these events together to test students' recognition of nativism as a defining characteristic of 1920s American culture.

questions.subject: U.S. History
questions.stem: The purpose of this law was to

choices.text: limit the power of the president from involving the United States in extended wars
expand the power of the president as commander in chief
allow the president to declare war on foreign countries
prevent the president from signing a treaty without the consent of Congress

questions.explanation: The War Powers Resolution (1973) requires the president to notify Congress within 48 hours of committing U.S. armed forces to hostilities and to terminate unauthorized deployments within 60 days, explicitly to prevent presidents from engaging in extended, undeclared wars as both Johnson and Nixon did in Vietnam. The Resolution has been cited in debates about U.S. military action in Lebanon (1983), Kosovo (1999), Libya (2011), and Syria, but presidents have consistently argued it infringes on their commander-in-chief powers. No Supreme Court ruling has definitively resolved this executive-legislative tension.
questions.sourceIssue: Question text depends on an unshown released source, but no verified stimulus crop is available.

questions.subject: Regents Review
questions.stem: Which statement best describes this author's point of view?

choices.text: The king holds ultimate power.
Citizens only vote on the laws with which they agree.
There are no limits on the power of the state.
Individual power is limited by the majority.

questions.explanation: Correct answer: Individual power is limited by the majority..

questions.subject: Regents Review
questions.stem: Which claim is best supported by this cartoon?

choices.text: The people of France were tolerant of different religions.
France had entered a radical period of the revolution.
The ideas and values of the Enlightenment were upheld in France.
France had created a limited constitutional monarchy.

questions.explanation: Correct answer: France had entered a radical period of the revolution..

questions.subject: Regents Review
questions.stem: Which action was a result of the situation described in this passage?

choices.text: Britain initiated new policies that supported food aid programs for the Irish.
Efforts to regulate food prices resulted in an improved standard of living in Ireland.
The Irish began to emigrate overseas in order to escape starvation.
British citizens emigrated to Ireland to regulate the export of Irish-grown grains.

questions.explanation: Correct answer: The Irish began to emigrate overseas in order to escape starvation..

questions.subject: Regents Review
questions.stem: What led to the situation depicted in this cartoon?

choices.text: the establishment of labor unions
the decrease of maritime trade
prolonged warfare and civil strife
industrialization and urbanization

questions.explanation: Correct answer: industrialization and urbanization.

questions.subject: Regents Review
questions.stem: How did the British government attempt to improve the situation depicted in this cartoon?

choices.text: It began efforts to improve sanitation.
It recommended a return to agrarian society.
It evacuated the land around the river.
It built factories along the river bank.

questions.explanation: Correct answer: It began efforts to improve sanitation..

questions.subject: Regents Review
questions.stem: Which claim is best supported by this passage?

choices.text: Great Britain and India became equal trading partners.
Robert Clive was a central figure in expanding British influence in India.
The Mughal emperor successfully controlled most profitable provinces.
The East India Company became a trading organization run by the Mughals.

questions.explanation: Correct answer: Robert Clive was a central figure in expanding British influence in India..

questions.subject: Regents Review
questions.stem: The appointment of the English East India Company as revenue collector can be considered a turning point in Indian history because it

choices.text: created an Indian National Congress
led to a long period of British dominance in this region
guaranteed the independence of Indian provinces
immediately transformed the provinces of Bihar and Bengal into wealthy urban centers

questions.explanation: Correct answer: led to a long period of British dominance in this region.

questions.subject: Regents Review
questions.stem: This passage can best be used to understand

choices.text: how the Nazi party indoctrinated German children
why Nazi Germany invaded neighboring countries
the purpose of Nazi censorship laws in Germany
the steps the Nazi party took to improve the German economy

questions.explanation: Correct answer: how the Nazi party indoctrinated German children.

questions.subject: Regents Review
questions.stem: This cartoon can be used to demonstrate the

choices.text: threat posed by Japan to other territories of Asia
military technologies Japan possessed
geographic barriers that protected China from foreign influence
alliances that were formed in East Asia

questions.explanation: Correct answer: threat posed by Japan to other territories of Asia.

questions.subject: Regents Review
questions.stem: Which statement best describes this author's point of view?

choices.text: Democracy will lead to fewer rights.
Tribal distinctions encourage democracy.
Voting is an important process in European colonization.
Achieving the right to vote is the first step in developing a democracy.

questions.explanation: Correct answer: Achieving the right to vote is the first step in developing a democracy..

questions.subject: Regents Review
questions.stem: Which response is being taken to address the problem discussed in this article?

choices.text: utilizing international cooperation
developing new technologies to meet local needs
relying on traditional methods of assessing the problem
increasing access to medication

questions.explanation: Correct answer: developing new technologies to meet local needs.

questions.subject: Regents Review
questions.stem: Based on this excerpt, what is the author's point of view about the role of government in the Plymouth Colony?

choices.text: Separate leaders for church and state should be elected.
People have the right to rebel against the government.
The power to govern belongs to the people.
Rulers must be given absolute authority to govern.

questions.explanation: John Robinson's 1620 address to the Plymouth colonists articulated a theological and political vision in which civil authority derived from the people's consent and the community's covenant with God, not from divine right of kings or hereditary aristocracy. This Separatist/Puritan concept of self-governance—the idea that the governed create and give power to their government—is foundational to American democratic theory. The Mayflower Compact (1620), signed just weeks after Robinson's address, institutionalized this principle of popular consent for the Plymouth Colony.

questions.subject: Regents Review
questions.stem: Which document most closely reflects the ideas about government expressed by John Robinson in this address?

choices.text: Mayflower Compact
Maryland Toleration Act
Stamp Act
Townshend Acts

questions.explanation: The Mayflower Compact (1620) was a governing agreement signed by 41 of the male passengers aboard the Mayflower before landing at Plymouth, establishing a 'civil body politic' to enact 'just and equal laws' for the 'general good'—a direct expression of Robinson's ideas about the people's authority to create their own government. This made the Compact one of the earliest examples of self-government in English North America and a precursor to the democratic ideas that would eventually produce the Declaration of Independence and the Constitution. Students often confuse it with later documents like the Virginia House of Burgesses; the key difference is that the Compact was a social contract made by the colonists themselves, not a charter granted by the Crown.

questions.subject: Regents Review
questions.stem: This passage was written during the

choices.text: debate over the adoption of the Articles of Confederation
ratification of the United States Constitution
nullification crisis in Virginia and Kentucky
controversy over the War of 1812

questions.explanation: The passage describes an Antifederalist arguing that the proposed Constitution gives too much power to the central government—specifically through the Elastic (Necessary and Proper) Clause—placing it firmly within the ratification debate of 1787–1788. Antifederalists like Patrick Henry, George Mason, and Mercy Otis Warren wrote pamphlets and gave speeches arguing that the Constitution would replace the states with a distant, tyrannical national government unless a Bill of Rights was added. Their concerns were partially addressed by the promise to add the Bill of Rights, ratified in 1791.

questions.subject: Regents Review
questions.stem: Which claim about the proposed United States Constitution is being made by the author of this passage?

choices.text: The new Constitution gave the states enough power to avoid tyranny.
The elastic clause would give too much power to Congress.
The system of federalism is best for the new nation.
The two-party system would lead to rebellion in the future.

questions.explanation: The Necessary and Proper Clause (Article I, Section 8)—also called the 'elastic clause'—gives Congress the power to 'make all Laws which shall be necessary and proper' for carrying out its enumerated powers. Antifederalists feared this open-ended grant of authority would allow Congress to expand its power indefinitely, undermining the limited government the Constitution claimed to create. This concern proved prophetic: in McCulloch v. Maryland (1819), Chief Justice Marshall interpreted the clause broadly to uphold the constitutionality of the national bank, establishing the doctrine of implied powers.

questions.subject: Regents Review
questions.stem: Which statement best describes the claims made by President Andrew Jackson in this passage?

choices.text: The United States government must obey past treaties made with Native Americans.
White settlers must work harder to get along with Native Americans.
Native Americans had agreed to assimilate into American society.
The relocation of Native Americans will assist both them and white settlers.

questions.explanation: President Andrew Jackson's 1830 message to Congress supporting the Indian Removal Act framed the forced relocation of the Five Civilized Tribes as beneficial to Native Americans—claiming it would protect them from white encroachment and give them 'room to breathe' in the West—while simultaneously serving white settlers' demand for their fertile lands. Jackson's rhetoric of benevolent paternalism masked the coercive reality of removal, which the Cherokee Nation contested in court (Worcester v. Georgia, 1832) and lost when Jackson refused to enforce the Supreme Court's ruling. Students should recognize Jackson's framing as a rationalization for what was essentially ethnic cleansing.

questions.subject: Regents Review
questions.stem: What was one result of President Jackson's message to Congress?

choices.text: Trail of Tears
annexation of Texas
Dred Scott decision
Grange Movement

questions.explanation: Jackson's Indian Removal Act (1830) authorized the forcible relocation of the Cherokee, Chickasaw, Choctaw, Creek, and Seminole nations from their homelands in the Southeast to 'Indian Territory' (present-day Oklahoma). The Cherokee's forced march in 1838–1839, known as the Trail of Tears, was carried out by the U.S. Army after the tribe exhausted legal challenges; approximately 4,000 of the 15,000 Cherokee who were relocated died from cold, disease, and starvation. The Trail of Tears is one of the most morally condemned episodes in U.S. history and a touchstone for questions about Native American policy.

questions.subject: Regents Review
questions.stem: This certificate supports a national commitment to what policy?

choices.text: Monroe Doctrine
isolationism
Manifest Destiny
detente

questions.explanation: The Homestead Act certificate relates to the federal government's promotion of westward settlement through land grants—a policy rooted in the ideology of Manifest Destiny, the belief that the United States was destined to expand from the Atlantic to the Pacific under divine providence. Manifest Destiny justified the annexation of Texas (1845), the Oregon Treaty (1846), the Mexican Cession (1848), and the series of land acts (Homestead Act 1862, Pacific Railroad Act 1862) that incentivized settlement of the Great Plains and Far West. The Regents connects land policy documents to the broader ideological framework of continental expansion.

questions.subject: Regents Review
questions.stem: What area of the United States was most affected by the program described in this document?

choices.text: the Southwest deserts
the Great Plains
the Hudson Valley
the Pacific Northwest

questions.explanation: The Homestead Act (1862) granted 160 acres of federal land to any American (including freed slaves and immigrants who intended to become citizens) who would live on and improve the land for five years. The Great Plains became the primary area affected because the act opened millions of acres of federal land in Kansas, Nebraska, the Dakotas, and Oklahoma to settlement. However, the Great Plains also experienced the Dust Bowl (1930s) when decades of over-farming by homesteaders removed the native grasses that held the fragile topsoil in place.

questions.subject: Regents Review
questions.stem: Which phrase is most closely associated with the ideas expressed in this excerpt?

choices.text: separate but equal
clear and present danger
all men are created equal
necessary and proper

questions.explanation: Justice Henry Billings Brown's majority opinion in Plessy v. Ferguson (1896) established the 'separate but equal' doctrine, ruling that racially segregated railroad cars were constitutional as long as the physical accommodations were equal. The phrase 'separate but equal' became the constitutional foundation of Jim Crow segregation throughout the South for the next 58 years. The doctrine was finally overturned in Brown v. Board of Education (1954), when the Supreme Court ruled that 'separate educational facilities are inherently unequal.'

questions.subject: Regents Review
questions.stem: What was an effect of the Supreme Court's decision in Plessy v. Ferguson?

choices.text: The Freedmen's Bureau was established.
The Underground Railroad was formed.
The practice of segregation was reinforced.
Voting rights were enhanced by grandfather clauses.

questions.explanation: Plessy v. Ferguson (1896) upheld the 'separate but equal' doctrine, providing the constitutional justification for states throughout the South to enact Jim Crow laws mandating racial segregation in schools, transportation, restaurants, theaters, and all public facilities. The decision effectively nullified the 14th Amendment's equal protection guarantee for Black Americans for nearly six decades. The practice of segregation was reinforced by Plessy—not ended or reformed—because the ruling gave states legal cover to maintain racial apartheid systems as long as they claimed facilities were 'equal.'

questions.subject: Regents Review
questions.stem: The purpose of this photograph was to

choices.text: raise public awareness for conditions in the tenements
support unrestricted immigration
expose the unsafe working conditions in factories
promote the use of child labor

questions.explanation: Jacob Riis, a Danish-born journalist and photographer, documented the horrific living conditions in New York City's Lower East Side tenements in his landmark 1890 book How the Other Half Lives, using photographs and vivid prose to show middle-class Americans the overcrowded, dangerous, disease-ridden conditions in which immigrant workers lived. His work shocked the public conscience and contributed directly to housing code reforms, tenement regulations, and the broader Progressive movement's push for urban social reform. Riis's use of photojournalism to build public support for reform is a classic example of muckraking.

questions.subject: Regents Review
questions.stem: Individuals who were influenced by the work of Jacob Riis would most likely agree that

choices.text: federal income taxes should be eliminated
monopolistic business practices should be encouraged
labor unions should be banned
social and economic reforms should be addressed by the government

questions.explanation: Jacob Riis's muckraking photography of tenement conditions convinced many Americans who read his work that the problems of poverty, overcrowding, disease, and child labor were not the result of individual moral failure but of systemic economic and social conditions that required government intervention. His influence helped build public support for Progressive-era reforms including tenement housing codes, child labor laws, workplace safety regulations, and public health initiatives. The Regents connects Riis's work to the broader Progressive argument that government must actively address the social costs of industrialization.

questions.subject: Regents Review
questions.stem: Photographs such as these were often published to

choices.text: increase newspaper sales
discourage investigative journalists
support restrictions on the freedom of the press
pressure reporters to reveal their sources

questions.explanation: Yellow journalism—sensationalist reporting that exaggerated, distorted, or invented stories to attract readers—was pioneered by Joseph Pulitzer's New York World and William Randolph Hearst's New York Journal in the 1890s. By publishing dramatic, often fabricated stories about Spanish atrocities in Cuba (including the sinking of the USS Maine in 1898), these newspapers helped inflame public opinion in favor of war with Spain and dramatically boosted their circulation. The Regents tests yellow journalism as an example of media's power to shape public opinion and influence foreign policy.

questions.subject: Regents Review
questions.stem: What was one outcome of the destruction of the USS Maine?

choices.text: Spanish officials responsible for the destruction were put on trial.
Spain strengthened its control over Cuba.
Public support for a declaration of war against Spain increased.
Spain paid to rebuild the ship and compensate the victims.

questions.explanation: The USS Maine, an American battleship, exploded and sank in Havana Harbor on February 15, 1898, killing 266 American sailors. Yellow journalists (especially Hearst and Pulitzer) immediately blamed Spain, despite no clear evidence, inflaming American public opinion with the slogan 'Remember the Maine, to Hell with Spain!' Public pressure on Congress and President McKinley grew rapidly, and the U.S. declared war on Spain in April 1898. Modern investigations suggest the explosion was likely an internal accident, not a Spanish attack, but the Maine became the rallying cry for the Spanish-American War.

questions.subject: Regents Review
questions.stem: Which constitutional provision supports President Theodore Roosevelt's view regarding large corporations?

choices.text: reserved powers of the states
presidential veto
eminent domain
congressional power to regulate interstate commerce

questions.explanation: The Commerce Clause (Article I, Section 8) gives Congress the power to regulate commerce 'among the several states'—and the Supreme Court's broad interpretation of this clause in Gibbons v. Ogden (1824) established the constitutional basis for federal regulation of the national economy. Theodore Roosevelt used this constitutional authority to argue for federal oversight of large corporations engaged in interstate commerce, culminating in the Sherman Antitrust Act's enforcement and the creation of the Bureau of Corporations (1903). Regents questions test students' understanding of which constitutional provision supports Progressive-era federal regulation.

questions.subject: Regents Review
questions.stem: Which United States economic policy did President Roosevelt's message challenge?

choices.text: mercantilism
laissez-faire
protectionism
supply-side

questions.explanation: Laissez-faire economics—the doctrine that government should not interfere in the marketplace and that free competition naturally produces the best outcomes—dominated U.S. economic policy through most of the Gilded Age (1870s–1900s). President Roosevelt challenged this ideology by arguing that some corporations had grown so large and powerful that competition no longer functioned, requiring active government regulation to protect consumers, workers, and small businesses. His antitrust policy and advocacy for a 'Square Deal' between labor, capital, and the public represented a decisive rejection of laissez-faire.

questions.subject: Regents Review
questions.stem: In this address, why does President Woodrow Wilson conclude that neutrality is no longer a practical United States policy?

choices.text: The German government has invaded United States territory.
United States ships and citizens are being attacked by German submarines.
The Allies have committed several acts of war.
Germany broke a series of peace treaties.

questions.explanation: President Woodrow Wilson's April 2, 1917, address to Congress requesting a declaration of war against Germany cited Germany's resumption of unrestricted submarine warfare (announced January 31, 1917) as the direct cause: German U-boats were sinking American merchant ships and passenger vessels, killing American citizens, in violation of international law and earlier pledges. Wilson also cited the Zimmermann Telegram (March 1917), in which Germany secretly proposed a military alliance with Mexico against the U.S. Together, these acts made American neutrality untenable.

questions.subject: Regents Review
questions.stem: How did Congress respond to President Wilson's 1917 address?

choices.text: They sent negotiators to meet with representatives.
They voted to declare war against Germany.
They placed an embargo on all goods shipped to and from Germany.
They asked the League of Nations to stop Germany's use of submarine warfare.

questions.explanation: Congress voted to declare war on Germany on April 6, 1917, four days after Wilson's war message, by a vote of 82–6 in the Senate and 373–50 in the House. This was the constitutionally required step: Article I gives Congress—not the president—the power to declare war, and Wilson went to Congress rather than acting unilaterally. The declaration transformed the U.S. from a neutral nation (since 1914) to a belligerent, eventually contributing 2 million soldiers to the Western Front and helping turn the tide against Germany.

questions.subject: Regents Review
questions.stem: The purpose of this 1936 poster was to encourage Americans to

choices.text: sign up for government jobs
receive low-cost medical insurance
enroll in a national retirement system
exercise their right to vote in national elections

questions.explanation: The 1936 Social Security poster encouraged Americans to register for Social Security account numbers under the Social Security Act (1935), which created a federal retirement insurance program funded by payroll taxes. The program—part of FDR's New Deal—guaranteed workers a monthly income in old age, addressing the widespread poverty among the elderly that the Great Depression had made catastrophically visible. Social Security represented a fundamental expansion of the federal government's responsibility for citizens' economic security, a bedrock of the modern American welfare state.

questions.subject: Regents Review
questions.stem: The purpose of the Nuremberg Trials referred to in this passage was to

choices.text: force Germany to pay war reparations
punish Germany for the blitzkrieg military campaign through Europe
warn the German people about the Nazi party
hold German leaders accountable for crimes against humanity

questions.explanation: The Nuremberg Trials (1945–1946) were a series of military tribunals held by the Allied powers to prosecute Nazi leaders for war crimes, crimes against peace, and crimes against humanity—including the planning and execution of the Holocaust. Nineteen of the 24 principal defendants were convicted; twelve were sentenced to death. The trials established the precedent of individual criminal responsibility for atrocities committed under government orders and laid the groundwork for international humanitarian law, including the Genocide Convention (1948) and later the International Criminal Court.

questions.subject: Regents Review
questions.stem: What was the United States response to the situation shown on this map?

choices.text: providing financial aid to Western European nations
immediate military action against the Soviet Union
a call for the United Nations to send forces to Eastern Europe
withdrawal of financial support for the North Atlantic Treaty Organization (NATO)

questions.explanation: The map showing post-World War II Europe with Soviet-dominated Eastern Europe and economically devastated Western Europe prompted the Marshall Plan (1948): the U.S. provided $13 billion in economic aid to rebuild Western European nations and prevent communist political parties—which were gaining popularity in France and Italy by exploiting poverty—from winning democratic elections. The plan succeeded in restoring Western European economies and democratic institutions, creating stable, prosperous allies for the U.S. in the Cold War. Regents questions frequently use this map to test students' understanding of the strategic logic of the Marshall Plan.

questions.subject: Regents Review
questions.stem: A historian would find these excerpts useful for studying the

choices.text: impact of the Red Scare on civil liberties
opposition to New Deal reforms
debates over international affairs
concerns over executive cabinet appointments

questions.explanation: The Second Red Scare (1947–1957) produced extensive civil liberties abuses: Senator McCarthy's reckless accusations, the House Un-American Activities Committee (HUAC) Hollywood blacklist, loyalty oaths for federal employees, and prosecutions of alleged communists like Alger Hiss and the Rosenbergs. These excerpts document how fear of communism led the government to restrict free speech, due process, and freedom of association—the same civil liberties violations pattern as the first Red Scare (1919–1921). The Regents uses this pattern to test students' understanding of the recurring tension between national security and civil liberties.

questions.subject: Regents Review
questions.stem: This cartoonist is criticizing President Dwight D. Eisenhower for failing to

choices.text: provide government support for public housing
promote equality for African Americans
address natural disasters in the South
enforce strict public safety measures

questions.explanation: The cartoon criticizing President Eisenhower likely depicts him as passive or indifferent to civil rights—a fair characterization of his early presidency, when he implemented desegregation of the federal workforce and military but refused to publicly endorse Brown v. Board of Education (1954) and was reluctant to use federal power to enforce civil rights. Eisenhower personally believed that legal mandates could not change social attitudes and that desegregation should proceed gradually. The Little Rock crisis (1957) finally forced him to act, sending federal troops to enforce school integration over a segregationist governor's defiance.

questions.subject: Regents Review
questions.stem: In 1957, President Eisenhower dealt with a civil rights crisis in Little Rock, Arkansas, by sending troops to

choices.text: enforce a Supreme Court decision on school integration
protect freedom riders on interstate buses
arrest leaders of the Ku Klux Klan
stop the violence during voter registration drives

questions.explanation: The Little Rock Crisis (September 1957) began when Arkansas Governor Orval Faubus ordered the National Guard to block nine Black students (the 'Little Rock Nine') from entering Central High School, defying the federal court order implementing Brown v. Board of Education. President Eisenhower federalized the Arkansas National Guard and deployed 1,200 soldiers from the 101st Airborne Division to escort the students into school, sending a clear message that federal judicial authority would be enforced by military force if necessary. This was the first time since Reconstruction that the president used federal troops to protect the civil rights of Black Americans.

questions.subject: Regents Review
questions.stem: What was one outcome associated with the Watergate affair?

choices.text: The Vietnam War continued to expand.
Trade with China expanded rapidly.
Support for immigration reform increased.
President Nixon eventually resigned from the presidency.

questions.explanation: The Watergate scandal (1972–1974) involved the break-in at the Democratic National Committee headquarters (June 1972), a massive cover-up involving hush money and obstruction of justice, and the revelation that President Nixon had personally ordered the cover-up on White House tape recordings. When the Supreme Court (United States v. Nixon, 1974) ruled unanimously that Nixon must turn over the tapes, which contained the 'smoking gun' of his direct involvement, Nixon resigned on August 9, 1974—becoming the only U.S. president ever to resign from office.

questions.subject: Regents Review
questions.stem: Which statement is best supported by the idea expressed in this cartoon?

choices.text: War powers are divided equally between the executive and legislative branches.
The legislative branch has refused to exercise its war powers.
The war powers of the executive branch have expanded.
The war powers of the executive branch are often checked by the judicial branch.

questions.explanation: The cartoon depicting the expansion of presidential war powers likely refers to the post-9/11 era, when Presidents Bush and Obama claimed broad executive authority to conduct drone strikes, mass surveillance (NSA programs revealed by Snowden in 2013), and detention of terror suspects without trial at Guantanamo Bay. The best-supported statement is that executive war powers have expanded significantly since World War II—through Korean War deployment without declaration, Vietnam War escalation, and post-9/11 military actions—while Congress has struggled to reassert its constitutional war-making authority. The War Powers Resolution (1973) was Congress's most significant but largely ineffective attempt to limit this expansion.

questions.subject: Regents Review
questions.stem: Which problem is represented in this cartoon?

choices.text: Americans were confused by the wording of the new law.
The Patriot Act did not apply to enough people in the United States.
The Patriot Act, while keeping the nation safe, would violate the rights of individuals.
Individuals were concerned that the Patriot Act did not make the nation safe enough.

questions.explanation: The post-9/11 debate over the PATRIOT Act illustrates a fundamental tension in constitutional democracy: national security measures designed to protect citizens can simultaneously erode the civil liberties that make that democracy worth protecting. The Act's provisions expanding surveillance, search, and detention powers were defended as essential to preventing another attack, while critics argued they violated Fourth Amendment protections against unreasonable search and seizure and created a dangerous precedent for executive overreach. This tension between security and liberty is a recurring Regents theme in questions about the post-9/11 era.

questions.subject: Regents Review
questions.stem: Which event most directly led to the passage of the Patriot Act?

choices.text: Iraq's invasion of Kuwait
end of the Cold War
Soviet launching of Sputnik
terrorist attacks on the United States on September 11, 2001

questions.explanation: The USA PATRIOT Act was signed into law October 26, 2001—just 45 days after the September 11, 2001 terrorist attacks that killed nearly 3,000 Americans in New York, Washington D.C., and Pennsylvania. Congress passed the Act with overwhelming bipartisan support and minimal debate, reflecting the national security emergency atmosphere of the weeks following the attacks. The 9/11 attacks directly caused the Act's passage, making them its most proximate cause—not earlier terrorist incidents like the 1993 World Trade Center bombing or the 1995 Oklahoma City bombing.

Grade 11 U.S. History :: Foreign Policy + Wars
